The U.S. hopes this will lead Mali to grant the U.S. permission to fly intelligence-gathering missions over the country's vast terrain where jihadists have been gaining ground, according to the current and former U.S. officials.
The Trump administration has sought to rebuild ties with Mali after they came under strain during the previous administration.
In Washington, the goal to resume intelligence gathering is partly driven by a ‚Äãdesire to find an American pilot who was kidnapped by armed men while working for Christian missionaries in neighboring Niger. The pilot is currently believed to be held in Mali by ‚Äãthe local al Qaeda affiliate, Jama'at Nusrat al-Islam wal-Muslimin (JNIM), the officials said.
The State Department declined to comment.
A Malian government spokesperson did not respond to a request ‚ÅÝfor comment.
But after the sanctions were lifted on February 27, Mali's government praised the move, describing it as a step that would help "improve relations between our two countries, while reiterating respect for national sovereignty."
Mali, ‚Äãa gold-producing, landlocked nation in the Sahel region of Africa, is roughly twice the size of France. Like several of its neighbors in the Sahel, it is battling an increasingly violent insurgency led by JNIM.
JNIM ‚Äãhas pursued widespread kidnapping campaigns targeting foreign nationals to finance its operations in West Africa, according to U.S. crisis-monitoring group Armed Conflict Location & Event Data.
The top U.S. envoy to Africa, Nick Checker, visited Mali last month to meet with Malian Foreign Minister Abdoulaye Diop. The goal was to convey Washington's "desire to chart a new course in the bilateral relationship and move past policy missteps," according to the State Department, an apparent reference to Biden ‚Äãadministration policies.
Diop praised American efforts to relaunch the relationship with a new approach based on mutual respect for its sovereignty and non-interference.
The Trump administration has largely dropped efforts to promote elections in the Sahel ‚Äãregion, where military officials have overthrown elected but unpopular, Western-backed governments in recent years and turned to Russia for security support.
That has been a welcome move in Bamako and other Sahelian capitals. In the Sahel, perceived lecturing by ‚ÄåWashington is often ‚ÅÝviewed as paternalistic and even racist, according to Catherine Nzuki of the Center for Strategic and International Studies, a U.S.-based think tank, which published a note about the U.S. loss of a base in Niger.
The Biden administration‚Äôs efforts to promote democracy after the coups helped trigger a break with Niger in 2024, with Niamey ordering all U.S. troops to leave a newly built, sprawling drone base in Agadez after a meeting with Biden officials went poorly. The multimillion-dollar base was intended to underpin intelligence-gathering operations across the region.
But the Trump administration's effort to improve relations has also encountered setbacks. Mali, Niger and Burkina Faso in December took ‚Äãsteps to implement a reciprocal travel ban on Americans after ‚Äãthe three nations were added to the ‚ÅÝWhite House's travel ban list.
In Mali, militants allied with al Qaeda and other groups have gained influence, spreading across the region despite a nearly decade-long intervention backed by France and the West, and later the efforts of Russian mercenaries. Over the past year, suspected jihadists have attacked gold mining assets ‚Äãand key highways , starving the capital of fuel for businesses and schools.
Reuters was unable to determine whether Mali was taking steps to give Washington permission ‚Äãto conduct missions in its ‚ÅÝairspace or when it was expected to take action.
The former U.S. official said cooperation was likely to come eventually because it was in Mali's interest to allow the U.S. to resume intelligence-gathering operations. ‚ÄúThey will derive direct benefit from U.S. ISR activities where their national capabilities are insufficient,‚Äù the former official said, referring to intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance.
"This overflight would build on likely ongoing cooperation with other USG entities,‚Äù the former official added, referring to ‚ÅÝU.S. government agencies.
The U.S. ‚Äãhas shared intelligence with Mali that helped lead to a strike on senior JNIM leadership last year.
It wasn‚Äôt immediately clear where ‚Äãthe U.S. might base aircraft if Mali proceeds with granting permission to resume overflight operations. Ivory Coast has previously been discussed as an option, as it shares its northern border with Mali. Ghana hosted U.S. contractors late last year when the Trump administration began surveillance ‚Äãflights over Nigeria, ahead of its Christmas Day bombing of what the U.S. said were Islamic State positions.

item.title: Trump says Australia has taken care of some Iranian women soccer players
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump on Monday said Australian Prime ‚ÄãMinister Anthony Albanese told him ‚Äåfive members of the Iranian national women's soccer team had been "taken ‚Äãcare of" amid fears ‚Äãthey could be punished.
Australia's SBS ‚ÅÝNews earlier said five players ‚Äãhad "broken free" after the team ‚Äãwas eliminated from the Asian Cup tournament and were now under the ‚Äãprotection of the federal police, ‚Äãseeking assistance from the government.
"Five have already ‚Äåbeen ‚ÅÝtaken care of, and the rest are on their way," Trump said in a ‚ÄãTruth Social ‚ÄãPost ‚ÅÝafter talking to Albanese. "Some, however, feel they must ‚Äãgo back because they ‚Äãare ‚ÅÝworried about the safety of their families, including threats to ‚ÅÝthose ‚Äãfamily members if ‚Äãthey don't return."

item.title: Israel will target anyone promoting radical ideas against it, says UN envoy
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Israel's U.N. ambassador Danny Danon said on Monday that the new supreme leader of Iran , Mojtaba Khamenei, ‚Äãhas the same radical ideas as in the past ‚Äåand Israel will target anyone who promotes radical ideas against it.
"Changing the man at the top does not change the regime," Danon told reporters at the United ‚ÄãNations when asked about Iran's naming of Khamenei to replace ‚Äãhis father Ali Khamenei, who was killed on the ‚ÅÝfirst day of the war the U.S. and Israel launched against Iran at ‚Äãthe end of last month.
"The new leader, unfortunately, is more of the ‚Äãsame ideology, the same radical ideas, and ... anyone who will promote those radical ideas against us, we will target them, we will find them," Danon said.
Danon said ‚Äãthe people of Iran should rise up to choose their next ‚Äãleader and added: "We will have to create the conditions for them, and that ‚Äåis ‚ÅÝwhat we are doing now."
Asked about the threat to crucial energy traffic in the Strait of Hormuz and Iran's attacks against neighboring countries, Danon said Israel and the U.S. were hunting Iran's missile launchers and ‚Äãdegrading its capabilities.
"So ‚Äãit's going to ‚ÅÝbe harder for them to attack civilians in Dubai and to attack vessels in the Strait of ‚ÄãHormuz. It doesn't mean it will be 100% guaranteed, ‚Äãbut ‚ÅÝit will be harder for them to do that.
"So I'm optimistic about that. Every day we see the numbers of attacks, going down," Danon ‚ÅÝsaid.
The ‚Äãwar has effectively shut the Strait of ‚ÄãHormuz, where a fifth of global oil and liquefied natural gas is shipped along ‚ÄãIran's coast.

item.title: Anthropic sues to block Pentagon blacklisting over AI use restrictions
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters) - Anthropic on Monday filed a lawsuit to block ‚Äãthe Pentagon from placing it on a national security blacklist , escalating the artificial intelligence lab‚Äôs high-stakes battle with the U.S. military over usage ‚Äårestrictions on its technology.
Anthropic said in its lawsuit that the designation was unlawful and violated its free speech and due process rights. The filing in federal court in California asked a judge to undo the designation and block federal agencies from enforcing it.
‚ÄúThese actions are unprecedented and unlawful. The Constitution does not allow the government to wield its enormous power to punish a ‚Äãcompany for its protected speech," Anthropic said.
The Pentagon on Thursday slapped a formal supply-chain risk designation on Anthropic, limiting use of a technology that a ‚Äãsource said was being used for military operations in Iran . Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth designated Anthropic after the startup refused to remove ‚ÅÝguardrails against using its AI for autonomous weapons or domestic surveillance. The two sides had been in increasingly contentious talks over those limitations for months, Reuters first reported.
Anthropic ‚Äãofficials said the lawsuit doesn't preclude re-opening negotiations with the U.S. government and reaching a settlement. The company has said it does not want to be fighting with ‚Äãthe U.S. government. The Pentagon said it wouldn't comment on litigation. Last week, a Pentagon official said the two sides were no longer active talks.
The designation poses a big threat to Anthropic‚Äôs business with the government, and the outcome could shape how other AI companies negotiate restrictions on military use of their technology, though the company's CEO Dario Amodei clarified on Thursday that the ‚Äãdesignation had "a narrow scope" and businesses could still use its tools in projects unrelated to the Pentagon.
President Donald Trump has also directed the government to stop working ‚Äãwith Anthropic, whose financial backers include Alphabet's Google (GOOGL.O) , opens new tab and Amazon.com (AMZN.O) , opens new tab . Trump and Hegseth said there would be a six-month phase-out.
Reuters has reported that Anthropic's investors were racing to contain the damage ‚Äåcaused by ‚ÅÝthe fallout with the Pentagon.
Trump and Hegseth‚Äôs actions on February 27 came after months of talks with Anthropic over whether the company‚Äôs policies could constrain military action and shortly after Amodei met with Hegseth in hopes of reaching a deal.
The Pentagon said U.S. law, not a private company, would determine how to defend the country and insisted on having full flexibility in using AI for ‚Äúany lawful use,‚Äù asserting that Anthropic‚Äôs restrictions could endanger American lives.
Anthropic said even the best AI models were not ‚Äãreliable enough for fully autonomous weapons ‚Äãand that using them for that ‚ÅÝpurpose would be dangerous. The company also drew a red line on domestic surveillance of Americans, calling that a violation of fundamental rights.
After Hegseth‚Äôs announcement, Anthropic said in a statement that the designation would be legally unsound and set a dangerous ‚Äãprecedent for companies that negotiate with the government. The company said it would not be swayed by ‚Äúintimidation or punishment,‚Äù and ‚Äãon Thursday Amodei reiterated ‚ÅÝthat Anthropic would challenge the designation in court.
He also apologized for an internal memo published on Wednesday by tech news site The Information. In the memo, which was written last Friday, Amodei said Pentagon officials did not like the company in part because ‚Äúwe haven‚Äôt given dictator-style praise to Trump."
The Defense Department signed agreements worth up to $200 million ‚ÅÝeach with major ‚ÄãAI labs in the past year, including Anthropic, OpenAI and Google.
Microsoft-backed (MSFT.O) , opens new tab OpenAI announced a deal to ‚Äãuse its technology in the Defense Department network shortly after Hegseth moved to blacklist Anthropic. CEO Sam Altman said the Pentagon shared OpenAI's principles of ensuring human oversight of weapon systems and opposing ‚Äãmass U.S. surveillance.

item.title: Brazil's finance minister expected to step down next week, sources say
item.description:
BRASILIA, March 9 (Reuters) - Brazilian Finance Minister Fernando Haddad is expected to step down by the end of next week ‚Äãto run for governor of Sao Paulo state as pushed ‚Äåfor by President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, four sources told Reuters on Monday.
Haddad has not yet had a final conversation with Lula about running ‚Äãfor governor, one of the sources said, but the two ‚Äãhave been speaking frequently about the matter.
The move was ‚ÅÝreported by newspaper O Globo earlier in the day. The finance ‚Äãministry declined to comment.
Lula's push for Haddad to run in Sao ‚ÄãPaulo reflects the president's need for a strong presence in a state that often votes heavily for the right-wing opposition. A weak performance for the leftist ‚Äãleader in Sao Paulo could undermine his presidential campaign, according ‚Äãto his party.
Haddad had not planned to run for office this year and previously ‚Äåvoiced ‚ÅÝhis intention to serve as one of the coordinators of Lula's re-election campaign .
Sources told Reuters, however, that expectations of a tough election for the president have put pressure on Haddad to become a candidate ‚Äãin Sao Paulo.
A Datafolha ‚Äãpoll released ‚ÅÝover the weekend showed that a potential second-round runoff between Lula and Senator Flavio Bolsonaro, the eldest ‚Äãson of former President Jair Bolsonaro, would result in ‚Äãa ‚ÅÝtechnical tie.
Another Datafolha poll focused on Sao Paulo state showed Haddad would take 31% of the vote in a first round against 44% ‚ÅÝfor Governor ‚ÄãTarcisio de Freitas, backed by the ‚ÄãBolsonaro family, who defeated Haddad in 2022.

item.title: US indicts two alleged 'terrorists' accused of trying to bomb NYC protest
item.description: WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - The U.S. ‚ÄãDepartment of ‚ÄåJustice has indicted ‚Äãtwo ‚Äãpeople accused of trying ‚ÅÝto ‚Äãbomb a ‚Äãprotest in New York City, ‚ÄãAttorney ‚ÄãGeneral Pam Bondi ‚Äåsaid ‚ÅÝin a post on X ‚Äãon ‚ÄãMonday, ‚ÅÝcalling them "ISIS-inspired ‚Äãterrorists."

item.title: Ukraine ready for new peace talks 'at any moment', Zelenskiy says
item.description:
KYIV, March 9 (Reuters) - Ukraine is ready for ‚Äãnew U.S.-backed peace talks "at ‚Äåany moment", but its partners' attention is ‚Äãcurrently focused ‚Äãon the Iran conflict, President ‚ÅÝVolodymyr Zelenskiy ‚Äãsaid on Monday, adding ‚Äãthat the U.S. had asked to postpone an ‚Äãupcoming meeting.
Writing on ‚ÄãX, Zelenskiy said Moscow was ‚Äåtrying ‚ÅÝto manipulate the conflict in its favour and aiming ‚Äãto ‚Äãturn ‚ÅÝIran's strikes on its neighbours ‚Äãand U.S. bases ‚Äãinto "a ‚ÅÝsecond front of Russia's war against ‚ÅÝUkraine".

item.title: Two right-wing politicians lead in Peru's presidential race, but most are undecided, poll says
item.description:
LIMA, March 9 (Reuters) - Two right-wing candidates - the daughter ‚Äåof a former jailed president and the former mayor of Lima - are out in front of other contenders for Peru's April 12 presidential election, according to the latest poll, though many voters are either undecided or do not plan ‚Äãon voting.
Keiko Fujimori and Rafael Lopez Aliaga are the only candidates out of a record ‚Äãroster of 36 presidential hopefuls polling in the double digits. Fujimori polled at ‚ÅÝ10.7% versus her rival's 10%, according to a Datum Internacional poll released Sunday night on ‚Äãlocal TV, though roughly two-fifths of those polled either declined to choose a candidate or do not ‚Äãplan on voting in the first place.
The scant support for the leaders suggests that a June 7 runoff is likely, which would be typical for the Andean nation, as no candidate has won in the first round since ‚Äãthe end of the last century.
The job of Peru's head of state has become a revolving ‚Äãdoor, with eight presidents since 2018. Four former leaders are currently behind bars. Its unpopular right-wing-controlled Congress removed its ‚Äålatest ‚ÅÝpresident last month after he held secret meetings with a Chinese businessman.
Over 38% of voters polled between February 27 and March 4 were undecided or had decided not to vote, Datum found. Many of those who do vote will not decide until a week before the election, CEO Urpi Torrado ‚Äãsaid.
Fujimori, daughter of late ‚Äãformer president Alberto Fujimori, ‚ÅÝwho was imprisoned for 16 years for crimes against humanity - including the use of death squads during his decade-long tenure - is on her fourth run ‚Äãfor the presidency.
She has been trading places in polling with Lopez Aliaga, ‚Äãan ultra-conservative ‚ÅÝformer mayor who has embraced his nickname "Porky" for his resemblance to a U.S. cartoon pig, as part of his campaign. A fortnight ago, another Datum poll showed Lopez Aliaga in the lead with 13.4% and ‚ÅÝFujimori at ‚Äã9.7%.
Other candidates polling around 5% include leftist Alfonso Lopez-Chau, ‚Äãa former central bank director; millionaire businessman Cesar Acuna; Carlos Alvarez, a popular comedian and impersonator of former presidents; and retired ‚Äãgeneral and former intelligence chief Wolfgang Grozo.

item.title: Trump tells NY Post he is 'nowhere near' deciding to send troops to Iran to secure nuclear stockpile
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump said on Monday that he ‚Äãwas "nowhere near" deciding whether to send ‚ÄåU.S. troops into Iran to secure the stockpile of highly enriched uranium there.
"We haven‚Äôt made any ‚Äãdecision on that. We‚Äôre nowhere near ‚Äãit,‚Äù Trump told the New York Post ‚ÅÝwhen asked about reported discussions between ‚ÄãIsrael and the United States on ‚Äãpossibly deploying special forces to Iran to seize and secure the material.
A longstanding dispute over Iran's ‚Äãnuclear program escalated into military conflict ten ‚Äãdays ago, with the U.S. and Israel launching strikes ‚Äåagainst ‚ÅÝIran and Iran responding with attacks across the Middle East.
Without providing evidence, Trump said last month that Tehran was ‚Äãbeginning to ‚Äãrebuild the ‚ÅÝnuclear program that he claimed had been ‚Äúobliterated‚Äù by U.S. strikes ‚Äãin June last year.
Iran denies ‚Äãseeking ‚ÅÝa nuclear arsenal, saying its enrichment of uranium ‚Äì a process that produces fuel for ‚ÅÝpower ‚Äãplants and nuclear warheads ‚Äãdepending on its duration ‚Äì is strictly for civilian use.

item.title: Rubio could designate Afghanistan as sponsor of wrongful detention, source says
item.description: WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. ‚ÄãSecretary of ‚ÄåState Marco Rubio ‚Äãis ‚Äãconsidering whether to ‚ÅÝdesignate ‚ÄãAfghanistan as ‚Äãa state sponsor of ‚Äãwrongful ‚Äãdetention and could ‚Äåact ‚ÅÝas soon as Monday, ‚Äãaccording ‚Äãto ‚ÅÝa source ‚Äãfamiliar ‚Äãwith ‚ÅÝthe matter.

item.title: Islamist militants kill 15 in coordinated raids across northeast Nigeria
item.description:
MAIDUGURI, Nigeria, March 9 (Reuters) - Islamist militants killed at least 12 soldiers and three civilians in coordinated overnight attacks in northeast Nigeria, military ‚Äãsources and residents said on Monday, as attacks on armed forces intensify.
A ‚Äå17-year Islamist insurgency in the region has killed thousands of people and displaced 2 million, according to aid groups, despite major military campaigns.
The assaults in Kukawa, Dalwa and Goniri came days after a similar ‚Äãattack on an army post in Ngoshe and showed the militant groups' ‚Äãability to hit multiple fronts at once.
Boko Haram and Islamic State West ‚ÅÝAfrica Province (ISWAP) fighters swept into Kukawa district in Borno state before dawn on Monday, ‚Äãpushing towards the nearby military camp in a three-hour battle, soldiers involved told Reuters.
Troops ‚Äãlater retook the camp but not before the commanding officer and five other soldiers were killed, a military source said. Karta Maina Ma'aji Lawan, the lawmaker representing Kukawa, confirmed the attack and the officer's ‚Äãdeath.
In Dalwa, militants killed two soldiers and three residents before torching more than 250 ‚Äãhomes, said resident and traditional leader Shetima Isa.
In neighbouring Yobe state, insurgents overran the Goniri military ‚Äåbase, killing ‚ÅÝfour soldiers and setting vehicles and buildings ablaze, another soldier said.
Military spokesperson Sani Uba said troops had repelled several coordinated attacks on military positions in the northeast, and that all locations remained firmly under control.
"Regrettably, the engagements claimed the lives of some brave ‚Äãand gallant soldiers, including ‚Äãan outstanding officer ‚ÅÝin Kukawa, who paid the ultimate price," Uba said, without providing further casualty details.
Ground forces, backed by air support, were conducting follow-up ‚Äãoperations in the affected areas, while cordon-and-search missions continued in nearby ‚Äãvillages where ‚ÅÝwounded militants were reported to be hiding, he said.
Nigeria's military has ramped up raids on insurgent hideouts this year as part of a renewed offensive, but ISWAP and Boko Haram continue ‚ÅÝto ‚Äãexploit the tough terrain, porous borders and a thin ‚Äãstate presence across the northeast's arid belt.
The latest attacks came just hours after military intelligence had warned of ‚Äãa likely ISWAP offensive.

item.title: China's foreign minister calls for ceasefire in Gulf region
item.description:
BEIJING, March 9 (Reuters) - Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi on Monday ‚Äãcalled for a ceasefire in ‚Äåthe Gulf region as soon as possible, stressing that the ‚Äãsovereignty, security and territorial ‚Äãintegrity of Gulf countries should be ‚ÅÝfully respected, according to ‚Äãstatements from his ministry.
Wang made ‚Äãthe remarks in a phone call with his Kuwaiti counterpart Jarrah Jaber ‚ÄãAl-Ahmad Al-Sabah on Monday ‚Äãas Iran answered a U.S.-Israeli bombing ‚Äåcampaign ‚ÅÝwith strikes around the Gulf region.
In a separate call, Wang told Bahraini Foreign Minister ‚ÄãAbdullatif bin ‚ÄãRashid ‚ÅÝAl Zayani that China was deeply concerned about ‚Äãthe rapidly escalating situation ‚Äãand ‚ÅÝwould play a constructive role in maintaining peace and ‚ÅÝstability ‚Äãin the region.

item.title: Exclusive: Trump reviews options to curb energy prices as Iran strikes roil markets
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump is expected to review as early as Monday a set of options to tame oil prices, ‚Äãwhich have spiked to more than $100 a barrel due to the Iran war, according to two people familiar ‚Äåwith the matter.
The effort reflects White House worries that the surge in oil prices will hurt U.S. businesses and consumers ahead of the November midterm elections when Trump's fellow Republicans are hoping to retain control of Congress.
U.S. officials in Washington have been discussing with counterparts from the Group of Seven major ‚Äãeconomies a possible joint release of crude oil from strategic reserves as one of several measures currently under discussion, the ‚Äãsources said.
Other options include restricting U.S. exports, intervening in oil futures markets, waiving some federal taxes and ‚ÅÝlifting requirements under a U.S. law called the Jones Act that domestic fuel move only on U.S.-flagged ships, among others, the ‚Äãsources said, speaking on condition of anonymity.
Analysts have said that U.S. policy options will have little sway over global oil markets as ‚Äãlong as the fighting blocks Middle East oil exports accounting for a fifth of global supply through the Strait of Hormuz.
"The White House is in constant coordination with the relevant agencies on this important issue, as it is a top priority to the president. President Trump and his entire energy ‚Äãteam have had a strong game plan to keep the energy markets stable well before Operation Epic Fury began, and they will ‚Äãcontinue to review all credible options," White House spokesperson Taylor Rogers said in a statement, using the Trump administration's name for the U.S.-Israel military ‚Äåoperations targeting ‚ÅÝIran.
Global crude oil prices have hit levels not seen since mid-2022, briefly touching $119 a barrel, with gasoline and other fuel costs surging as a result since the U.S. and Israeli strikes began on February 28.
The White House last week asked federal agencies to assemble proposals that could help ease pressure on crude and gasoline prices, Reuters previously reported. The deliberations involve top White House officials, including White ‚ÄãHouse Chief of Staff Susie Wiles ‚Äãand top adviser Stephen Miller, ‚ÅÝthe sources said.
Analysts and industry officials have said the White House has few meaningful tools to quickly curb rising oil prices unless authorities can restore the flow of tankers through the Strait ‚Äãof Hormuz, the narrow waterway between Iran and Oman that carries roughly a fifth of the ‚Äãworld's oil supply.
"The ‚ÅÝproblem is options range from marginal through symbolic to deeply unwise," said one of the sources, who is engaged with the White House on the effort.
The turmoil in energy markets comes at a sensitive moment for the president, who has sought to keep fuel prices low ‚ÅÝas a ‚Äãcornerstone of his economic message to voters. A prolonged spike in oil and ‚Äãgasoline costs could ripple through the broader economy, raising transportation and consumer prices.
A White House plan to provide naval escorts and backstop insurance for tankers traveling the Strait ‚Äãof Hormuz so far has failed to significantly boost shipping traffic through the vital waterway.

item.title: Back to the 1970s? Investors brace for a return of stagflation
item.description:
LONDON, March 9 (Reuters) - Investors are now seriously considering the possibility that war in the Middle East could create a stagflationary shock, just as it did 50 years ago, when disruption to global ‚Äãenergy supplies sent inflation surging, and battered growth
‚ÄúThe risk of a 1970s scenario is rising,‚Äù said Kaspar Hense, portfolio manager at RBC BlueBay Asset Management.
‚ÄúIf there is another extended war, ‚Äåwith oil prices going up significantly further, then the safe-haven status of government bonds are at risk, and with that, all assets.‚Äù
Here's how stagflation is a risk and could play out in markets in five charts.
The epicentre of stagflation fears is the surge in oil prices, and the biggest question for world markets now is how long will those prices remain elevated.
Brent crude vaulted above $100 a barrel on Monday and was on track for its biggest daily jump since the 2020 COVID ‚Äãcrisis .
It is up 70% since the start of the year, while European wholesale gas prices are at their highest level in over three years .
That's bad news for inflation.
"A useful rule of thumb is ‚Äãthat a 5% rise in oil prices adds around 0.1 percentage points to developed market inflation," Capital Economics said.
And high oil prices also could dampen economic growth.
The ‚ÅÝInternational Monetary Fund estimates for every persistent 10% rise in oil prices, a 0.1‚Äì0.2 percent fall in global economic output follows.
Oil price surges contributed to U.S. recessions in 1973, 1980, 1990 and 2008.
This ‚Äãputs central banks in a bind, as rate hikes to restrain inflation could further undermine economic growth.
Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee told the Wall Street Journal on Friday a "stagflationary environment that's as uncomfortable as any" could be looming.
Markets now ‚Äãsee at least one European Central Bank hike this year, compared to a 40% chance of a cut before the war.
They also see a chance of a rate hike this year from the Bank of England, having earlier priced at least two cuts.
‚ÄúIt seems only retreating oil prices could reverse rate hike fears, even with dovish minds at the ECB also stressing downside growth risks,‚Äù said Commerzbank rates strategist Rainer Guntermann.
That has battered global bond markets as investors ditch fixed-income assets, ‚Äãwhere inflation erodes future returns.
Short-dated bonds are the most sensitive. In Britain, two-year gilt yields have shot up by nearly 50 basis points in the last week, their worst sell-off since the budget crisis of 2022, in ‚Äãlight of the UK's sticky inflation and flat-lining growth .
German and Australian two-year yields have risen more than 30 bps in that time, while U.S. two-year yields are up a comparatively modest 13 bps. ,
That's got investors looking at inflation-linked ‚Äådebt, where both ‚ÅÝthe principal and the interest are linked to the rate of inflation.
This has pushed British five-year breakeven inflation rates - the difference between inflation-linked and nominal yields - up 28 basis points since the end of February. On Monday, they hit almost 3.5%, their highest since last April .
Meanwhile, five-year inflation-linked Treasury yields have risen by 4.2 bps in the last week, compared with a 15-bp rise in nominal yields .
Those wondering whether the market and economic hit will cause U.S. President Donald Trump to change course have to remember the stagflationary hit from the war will likely hurt the U.S. less than Europe or Asia.
"The U.S., with the Americas, ‚Äãis self-sufficient in many (of the) commodities being choked off ‚Äãdirectly or indirectly via (Strait of) Hormuz," said Rabobank ‚ÅÝsenior global strategist Michael Every in a note.
As well as oil he flags fertiliser and helium, important for semiconductor manufacturing.
Not surprisingly, U.S. markets have held up better in relative terms. The S&P 500 dropped 2% last week compared to a 5.5% fall in Europe and 6.3% drop for MSCI's Asia Pacific ex-Japan index. (.SPX) , opens new tab , (.STOXX) , opens new tab , (.MIAPJ0000PUS) , opens new tab
U.S. bonds also ‚Äãoutperformed German last week. ,
However, the U.S. is by no means immune to stagflation and was looking a bit vulnerable even before the energy price spike. ‚ÄãThe economy unexpectedly shed jobs in ‚ÅÝFebruary, and data this week is expected to show a tick higher in U.S. inflation.
Investors don't like stagflation because it hurts stocks, bonds - that aren't inflation linked - and even potentially gold, given it does not have a yield.
The precious metal dropped 2% last week, and was down again on Monday, though analysts said this was in part selling to make up for losses elsewhere.
The only safe haven that has really held up since the ‚ÅÝwar begun has ‚Äãbeen the dollar, which has gained on nearly every other developed market currency.
"The U.S. is a major oil producer and ‚Äãcan withstand an oil shock - though there will be political fallout," said Kit Juckes, head of FX strategy at Societe Generale.
"The same simply isn‚Äôt true of Europe, and the UK in particular."

item.title: Swiss parliament approves compensation for Crans-Montana bar fire victims
item.description:
GENEVA, March 9 (Reuters) - The Swiss lower house of parliament on Monday approved plans to make a ‚Äãone-off payment of 50,000 Swiss francs ($64,000) ‚Äåto survivors and bereaved families of a New Year bar fire at a ski resort that killed 41 ‚Äãpeople and injured 115.
The so-called solidarity contribution, ‚Äãwhich aims to provide swift financial support ‚ÅÝto victims of the blaze at the ‚ÄãLe Constellation bar in the town of Crans-Montana, ‚Äãhad already been voted through by the upper house of parliament last week.
The payment is meant to apply ‚Äãto each individual who lost their life ‚Äãin the fire and everyone who was hospitalised. Most ‚Äåof ‚ÅÝthose who died were teenagers and many of the victims were foreigners, including several from France and Italy.
According to witnesses and prosecutors, the ‚Äãfire appeared ‚Äãto have ‚ÅÝbeen started by the use of sparkling candles that ignited foam ‚Äãsoundproofing on the bar's basement ceiling.
Earlier, a document ‚Äãshowed ‚ÅÝthat Swiss prosecutors have widened their investigation into the fire, adding the mayor of Crans-Montana ‚ÅÝand ‚Äãfour other current and former ‚Äãlocal officials to the list of suspects.
($1 = 0.7785 Swiss francs)

item.title: Venture Global pledges on-time LNG deliveries amid Middle East conflict
item.description:
LONDON/HOUSTON, March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. liquefied natural gas developer Venture ‚ÄåGlobal LNG (VG.N) , opens new tab has told customers of its Plaquemines export plant under development in Louisiana that it will start delivering contracted cargoes on schedule and at the already-agreed-upon prices, according to a letter seen by Reuters.
The Virginia-based company wrote on Friday to buyers of Plaquemines Phase 1 - ‚Äãwhich include British major Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab and Poland‚Äôs Orlen (PKN.WA) , opens new tab - and said it remains committed to beginning long‚Äëterm deliveries from ‚ÄãOctober 31 despite a surge in global gas prices because of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran .
‚ÄúAs global ‚ÅÝenergy markets react to the critical developments surrounding Iran and the Middle East, we wanted to assure you that, ‚Äãas of today, Phase 1 remains on schedule,‚Äù the letter said.
Companies including BP (BP.L) , opens new tab , Shell, Unipec, Edison (EDNn.MI) , opens new tab , Galp (GALP.LS) , opens new tab , Repsol (REP.MC) , opens new tab and Orlen took ‚ÄãVenture Global to arbitration in one of the biggest disputes in the history of the LNG industry. The companies accused Venture Global of withholding contracted cargoes during commissioning of its nearby Calcasieu Pass plant to take advantage of higher prices on the spot market during the ‚Äã2021‚Äì2023 energy crisis.
While Venture Global - which blamed technical issues for the delays in formal commissioning at Calcasieu - prevailed over Shell, ‚ÄãBP is seeking at least $3.7 billion after winning its case, a Venture Global filing showed. Orlen‚Äôs case remains pending.
‚ÄúTo be clear, fluctuating market ‚Äåconditions have ‚ÅÝno impact on our previously communicated schedule,‚Äù a Venture Global spokesperson said. ‚ÄúAs we reaffirmed on our earnings call last week, nearly 70% of the 2026 cargoes at Plaquemines are already contracted for and we reaffirm that Phase One remains on target to declare COD in Q4 2026.‚Äù
The U.S.-Israeli war on Iran and Tehran's attacks on Gulf neighbors have disrupted oil and ‚Äãnatural gas exports from the ‚ÄãMiddle East and forced production ‚ÅÝstoppages, including in Qatar, which stopped operations at its LNG facilities, affecting some of the world's largest plants and a source that supplies about 20% of global LNG.
Benchmark gas prices ‚Äãhave jumped, with Dutch Title Transfer Facility futures trading at a three-year high of ‚Äãaround $21 per million ‚ÅÝBritish thermal units, and the Japan-Korea Marker near a two-year high around $16.
Venture Global is the second-largest U.S. LNG exporter. Plaquemines is its second export terminal and it shipped 2 million metric tons of LNG last month from Plaquemines, according to LSEG data. ‚ÅÝThe plant ‚Äãis sending out early commission cargoes even though it is still under ‚Äãdevelopment.
The company told customers it will begin developing its annual delivery program for the first contract year in early May, followed by the second contract ‚Äãyear in July.

item.title: Oil and gas industry urge EU to pause methane emissions law
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 9 (Reuters) - Oil and gas companies urged the European Union on Monday to pause its methane emissions law, warning it could disrupt Europe's fuel ‚Äãimports when tougher provisions kick in next year.
The call from industry ‚Äåcomes after the U.S. government demanded the EU exempt U.S. oil and gas from the rules. Brussels has since offered companies more flexible compliance options but declined to roll back the policy, a central ‚Äãpillar of its climate strategy.
An industry-backed study by Wood Mackenzie, published on ‚ÄãMonday, said up to 43% of EU gas imports and 87% ‚ÅÝof EU crude oil imports could struggle to meet the EU rules ‚Äãfrom 2027, exposing importers to heavy fines and risking supply gaps if producers are ‚Äãdeterred from shipping fuel to Europe.
IOGP and fuel manufacturing group FuelsEurope called on Brussels to pause and amend the law.
"The EU cannot afford a self-made regulatory supply shock, even more so ‚Äãin the current geopolitical context," said Francois-Regis Mouton de Lostalot, managing director of ‚ÄãIOGP Europe, whose members include ExxonMobil, Chevron, BP and TotalEnergies.
Oil and gas prices have surged ‚Äåthis ‚ÅÝmonth in response to the U.S. and Israeli war on Iran , adding short-term pressure on governments already navigating Europe's phase-out of Russian energy imports. Most EU oil and gas now comes from Norway and the U.S.
The EU methane law requires that, from January ‚Äã2027, imported gas ‚Äãmust comply with ‚ÅÝmonitoring and verification rules equivalent to Europe's, or meet a voluntary industry standard known as "Oil and Gas Methane Partnership 2.0 ‚Äãlevel 5". The aforementioned companies are all part of the ‚ÄãOGMP initiative.
Climate ‚ÅÝgroups disputed the study's findings. The Environmental Defense Fund said its own analysis indicated that, following methane-reduction commitments by many oil and gas majors, the global volume of ‚ÅÝOGMP ‚Äã2.0 level 5-compliant gas in 2027 would be ‚Äãmore than double EU demand, including supplies from Norway and the U.S.
A European Commission spokesperson did not ‚Äãimmediately respond to a request for comment.

item.title: Much of Iran's near-bomb-grade uranium likely to be in Isfahan, IAEA's Grossi says
item.description:
PARIS, March 9 (Reuters) - Almost half of ‚ÄåIran's uranium enriched to up to 60% purity, a short step from weapons-grade, was stored in a tunnel complex at Isfahan and is probably still there, U.N. nuclear watchdog chief Rafael Grossi said on Monday.
The tunnel complex is the only ‚Äãtarget that appears not to have been badly damaged in attacks last June by Israel ‚Äãand the U.S. on Iran's nuclear facilities.
Diplomats have long said Isfahan has been ‚ÅÝused to store 60% uranium, which the International Atomic Energy Agency confirmed in a report to member ‚Äãstates last month, without saying how much was there.
The IAEA estimates ‚Äãthat when Israel launched its first attacks in June, Iran had 440.9 kg of 60% uranium. If enriched further, that would provide the explosive needed for 10 nuclear weapons, according to an IAEA yardstick.
"What we believe is that Isfahan had ‚Äãuntil our last inspection a bit more than 200 kg, maybe a little bit more than that, ‚Äãof 60% uranium," IAEA chief Rafael Grossi told reporters in Paris.
He said the stock was "mainly" at Isfahan, and some held elsewhere ‚Äåmay ‚ÅÝhave been destroyed.
"The widespread assumption is that the material is still there. So we haven't seen - and not only us, I think in general all those observing the facility through satellite imagery and other means to see what's going on there - movement indicating that the material could have been transferred," Grossi ‚Äãsaid.
Iran has not informed the ‚ÄãIAEA of the ‚ÅÝstatus or whereabouts of its highly enriched uranium since the June attacks, nor has it let IAEA inspectors return to its bombed facilities.
Iran's nuclear programme is ‚Äãone reason Israel and the U.S. have given for their current attacks on ‚ÄãIran , arguing ‚ÅÝthat it was getting too close to being able to produce a bomb, despite Trump saying in June that U.S. strikes had obliterated the programme. The IAEA has said it has no credible indication of a coordinated nuclear ‚ÅÝweapons ‚Äãprogramme.
All three Iranian uranium-enrichment plants known to have been operating - two at ‚ÄãNatanz and one at Fordow - were destroyed or badly damaged in June.
"There is an amount (of 60% uranium) in Natanz also, which ‚Äãwe believe is still there," Grossi said.

item.title: Airline shares battered, airfares surge as Iran war pushes oil above $100
item.description:
HONG KONG/FRANKFURT, March 9 (Reuters) - Airline stocks were hammered on ‚ÄãMonday, while airfares soared as the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran sent oil prices surging, sparking fears of a deep travel slump and the potential for the widespread grounding ‚Äåof planes.
Oil prices jumped 15% to above $105 a barrel, hitting levels not seen since 2022 as some major producers cut supplies and fears of prolonged shipping disruptions gripped the market. At one point, Brent crude futures jumped as much as 29%.
Some jet fuel prices have doubled since the start of the conflict, piling pressure on carriers already navigating tight airspace as pilots reroute to avoid the Middle East conflict and thousands of stranded passengers try to leave the ‚Äãregion.
"Absent near-term relief, airlines around the world could be forced to ground thousands of aircraft while some of the industry's financially weakest carriers could halt operations," Deutsche analysts said ‚Äãin a note to clients.
In Asia, airline shares tumbled, with the worst-hit, including Korean Air Lines (003490.KS) , opens new tab , which slid 8.6%, Air ‚ÅÝNew Zealand (AIR.NZ) , opens new tab down 7.8% and Hong Kong's Cathay Pacific (0293.HK) , opens new tab which dropped 5%.
In Europe, Air France KLM (AIRF.PA) , opens new tab , British Airways-owner IAG (ICAG.L) , opens new tab , Wizz Air (WIZZ.L) , opens new tab and Lufthansa (LHAG.DE) , opens new tab fell between 2.5% and 6% in morning trade.
Major U.S. ‚Äãairlines shares were down about 1% to 5% in afternoon trading with JetBlue Airways (JBLU.O) , opens new tab down 5.35% followed by American Airlines (AAL.O) , opens new tab down 3.44%.
Underscoring that pain on the consumer side were jumps in ticket prices. Direct ‚Äãflights from Seoul to London on March 11 with Korean Air Lines, for example, leapt to $4,359, from $564 seven days earlier, according to Google Flights data. Flights from Los Angeles to Lima on LATAM Airlines (LTM.SN) , opens new tab rose to $2,125 compared to $499 in the same period.
"The issue for the airlines now is that travel demand may be curtailed as costs become prohibitive for leisure travellers and as some companies start to limit business travel due to the uncertain outlook," said ‚ÄãLorraine Tan, director of equity research, Asia at Morningstar.
The impact of high airfares could limit travel demand for much of 2026, Tan added.
Fuel is the second-largest expense for air carriers after labour, typically ‚Äãaccounting for a fifth to a quarter of operating expenses. Some major Asian and European airlines have oil hedging in place, but U.S. airlines largely stopped the practice over the last two decades.
"We assume the airlines are ‚Äåable to recapture ‚ÅÝa portion of the spike in fuel prices, but it's hard to envision margin expansion this year barring a rapid decline in energy prices," said Tom Fitzgerald, TD Cowen vice president of equity research on six major U.S. airlines and Air Canada.
A one-way trip to Quebec City from Newark on March 11 aboard Air Canada nearly tripled to $1,499 compared to a week ago, according to data from Google Flights.
High prices could have severe implications for the industry.
"If crude is rising 20%, jet fuel is rising several times more as it is even more scarce, adding significant cost ‚Äãto operations together with crew resources, which are ‚Äãstretched due to longer flying times when ‚ÅÝairspace is closed," said Subhas Menon, head of the Association of Asia Pacific Airlines.
The Deutsche analysts noted that a sharp spike in jet fuel costs in 2005 in the aftermath of hurricanes Katrina and Rita resulted in widespread and significant damage to the industry, including major airlines Delta and Northwest ‚Äãfiling for Chapter 11 bankruptcy that year.
Since February 28, when the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran started, through March 9, more than 40,000 flights to ‚Äãand from the Middle ‚ÅÝEast have been cancelled, according to data from Cirium.
With airspace severely constrained, airlines have been forced to reroute flights, carry extra fuel or make additional refuelling stops to guard against sudden diversions or longer flight paths through safer corridors.
Combined, Emirates, Qatar Airways and Etihad normally fly about one-third of the passengers from Europe to Asia and more than half of all passengers from Europe to Australia, New Zealand and nearby Pacific ‚ÅÝIslands, according to ‚ÄãCirium.
Flights to Iraq, Syria, Lebanon and Jordan by Turkish Airlines (THYAO.IS) , opens new tab , AJet, Pegasus (PGSUS.IS) , opens new tab and SunExpress have been cancelled until March ‚Äã13, Turkish Transport Minister Abdulkadir Uraloglu said on Sunday.

item.title: Exclusive: Canadian oil tycoon backs South Bow pipeline to boost US exports
item.description:
CALGARY, March 9 (Reuters) - The Canadian tycoon who heads one of North America's fastest growing oil companies told Reuters he supports ‚Äãa proposal to potentially revive parts of the former Keystone XL pipeline and wants to see Canada use the project as ‚Äåleverage in upcoming trade negotiations with the United States.
Still, Adam Waterous, CEO of Strathcona Resources (SCR.TO) , opens new tab , said he would prefer a new West Coast pipeline to increase access to Asian markets.
The comments by Waterous, one of Canada's most aggressive oil industry dealmakers, are the first public statements by a Canadian oil shipper in favour of a new pipeline proposal led by Canadian company ‚ÄãSouth Bow (SOBO.TO) , opens new tab that could increase the country's crude exports to the U.S. by more than 12%.
They are an early indication that the project ‚Äî which would require both ‚Äãa green light from U.S. President Donald Trump and the construction of additional links to U.S. refining hubs ‚Äî has at ‚ÅÝleast some level of commercial support from Canadian oil sands producers at a time when the U.S.-Israel war on Iran has disrupted international supply and sent global ‚Äãcrude prices soaring.
The plan involves a different route through the U.S. than the previous Keystone XL pipeline, which was canceled by former U.S. President Joe Biden ‚Äãin 2021 after years of Indigenous and environmental opposition, but would use some of that project's already built pipe in Alberta.
South Bow launched a competitive bidding process last week offering 450,000 barrels per day of contracted pipeline capacity from Hardisty, Alberta to multiple U.S. delivery points including Cushing, Oklahoma, and destinations on the U.S. Gulf Coast, to gauge potential commercial use.
Waterous ‚Äãdeclined to say if Strathcona will commit barrels to the South Bow project, citing ongoing commercial negotiations. But he pointed to his company's stated goal to expand ‚Äãits own oil production from 125,000 barrels per day in 2026 to up to 300,000 bpd by 2035.
"I've been a very early advocate of this project," Waterous said.
"And we ‚Äåare looking ‚ÅÝfor egress now," he said, referring to oil shippers' ability to transport their product to market.
But Waterous said the South Bow proposal is not the Canadian oil industry's first choice for bolstering crude exports. Currently more than 90% of Canada's oil exports go to the United States, and Waterous said Canadian oil shippers have long wanted greater market diversification.
Canadian oil companies would prefer another pipeline to the Pacific, to supplement the existing Trans Mountain pipeline which runs from Alberta to the British Columbia coast ‚Äãand offers an outlet to Asian ‚Äãmarkets, Waterous said.
Four other large Canadian ‚ÅÝoil sands producers did not respond to a Reuters request for comment on whether they support the South Bow proposal.
The Alberta government has said it will submit an application for a new crude oil pipeline to the west ‚Äãcoast for fast-track approval by the federal government, but no private company has said it will build the project.
The ‚Äãmain benefit to ‚ÅÝthe South Bow proposal, Waterous said, is that it can likely be built faster and more cheaply than a new West Coast pipeline, while also carrying potential political benefits for Canada.
He said Canada should try to leverage Trump's previously stated comments that he would like to see the Keystone XL project built.
Prime Minister Mark Carney has also raised with ‚ÅÝTrump the prospect of ‚Äãreviving the Keystone XL oil pipeline from Alberta to the United States as part of ‚Äãhis efforts to ease trade tensions between the two countries.
"The utility of a pipeline to the south is that it is a tool to achieve a tariff agreement to protect principally Canada's steel, ‚Äãauto and aluminum sectors, while also allowing the Canadian oil sector to grow," Waterous said.

item.title: Peru's pipeline disruption tests industrial resilience; businesses blanch at remote work order
item.description:
LIMA, March 9 (Reuters) - Peru's business groups are clashing with the new interim government over sweeping emergency measures imposed after a rupture in the country's ‚Äãmain natural‚Äëgas pipeline triggered the worst energy crisis in two decades, an early test of President Jose Balcazar‚Äôs ‚Äåinterim mandate weeks before national elections.
The breakdown of Transportadora de Gas del Peru's pipeline, the backbone of the system that supplies nearly half of Peru‚Äôs electricity and most of its LPG, has forced widespread gas rationing, pushing up energy prices and exposing long‚Äëstanding vulnerabilities in the Andean nation's energy ‚Äãsystem.
Balcazar, appointed on February 18 after Congress ousted his predecessor, now faces a high‚Äëstakes leadership test. More than ‚Äãa thousand firms have struggled with halted gas production, and business associations have called for the rollback ‚ÅÝof emergency directives.
Authorities say gas flows may not resume until mid‚ÄëMarch.
The outage could stoke inflation, which has remained among the ‚Äãlowest in the world despite years of political turmoil.
"It can hit inflation, but in a heterogeneous way depending on how energy ‚Äãaffects each activity,‚Äù said former Economy Minister Alonso Segura.
Inflation reached 0.69% in February and could exceed the 0.81% recorded in March last year, Segura said.
Natural gas is used to produce 70% of the LPG consumed domestically. Some 2.1 million households rely on piped gas and about one million vehicles ‚Äãrun on natural gas or LPG.
Peru is the world‚Äôs third-largest copper producer.
Former Energy and Mines Minister Carlos Herrera said the rupture ‚Äãunderscored Peru‚Äôs vulnerability after an alternative pipeline project was canceled in 2017 when the Brazilian construction company Odebrecht was unable to secure financing ‚Äåwhile being ‚ÅÝinvestigated in a case involving bribes paid to secure public works contracts in Peru.
"This crisis highlights the need to diversify the country‚Äôs energy matrix," Herrera said. "The serious problem we have is that we haven‚Äôt made exploratory efforts for natural gas over time either."
Industry groups said more than 1,000 companies have been affected, with some halting production. Felipe James, head of the National Society of Industry (SNI), ‚Äãsaid emergency rules allowing firms ‚Äãto switch temporarily to alternative ‚ÅÝfuels will be costly and not all companies are equipped to make the change.
"Not all can switch... they are not prepared," James said.
On Friday, the government ordered public employees in Lima to ‚Äãwork remotely for a week and encouraged private firms to do the same. Schools and ‚Äãuniversities will shift ‚ÅÝto virtual classes during the contingency.
Business associations criticized the measures, saying they amount to a partial shutdown of the economy.
"Stopping the country can never be the solution. We demand this decision be reversed," said COMEX, the foreign trade guild.
Peru's mining and energy chamber said it ‚ÅÝwas working "intensely" ‚Äãto mitigate the crisis and seeking to import LPG in "record time."
Spokespeople for ‚Äãthe presidency and the Ministry of Energy and Mines did not respond to a request for comment.
The TGP gas pipeline began operating in 2004 and also ‚Äãenables the export of natural gas to Japan, South Korea and China.

item.title: Two men charged with terrorism after homemade bomb thrown at anti-Islam protesters in New York
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters) - Two men accused of igniting homemade bombs at a chaotic anti-Islam protest outside the New York City mayor's mansion told police they were inspired by Islamic State, the Islamist militant group, and were charged on Monday with ‚Äãfederal terrorism-related crimes, according to court records.
Emir Balat, 18, and Ibrahim Kayumi, 19, are accused of traveling from Pennsylvania with ‚Äådangerous improvised explosive devices to Manhattan on Saturday, where a small group of far-right activists had planned the anti-Muslim rally outside Gracie Mansion against Zohran Mamdani, New York City's first Muslim mayor.
Balat was captured in photographs and video lighting a small IED and throwing it towards the anti-Muslim protesters before lighting a second device ‚Äãhanded to him by Kayumi and dropping it, according to the criminal complaint. Police were able to intercept the smoking devices, ‚Äãduct-taped jars containing screws and bolts and the explosive substance TATP, and no one was hurt.
After police arrested ‚ÅÝthem, they said they were inspired by Islamic State, and Balat told police that he wanted to carry out an attack "even bigger" than ‚Äãthe Boston Marathon bombing in 2013 that killed three people, according to the criminal complaint filed in the U.S. district court in Manhattan on ‚ÄãMonday.
Mehdi Essmidi, who represents Balat, did not immediately reply to a request for comment. Kayumi's lawyer Michael Arthus declined to comment.
Speaking alongside police outside Gracie Mansion on Monday, Mamdani condemned the violence. He and his wife were not home at the time, because they had known about the "Stop the Muslim takeover" rally organized by ‚Äãfar-right activist Jake Lang more than a week in advance. More than 100 counter-protesters gathered nearby, the vast majority peaceful, saying they were there ‚Äãto "run Nazis out" of New York.
"New York City will never tolerate violence whether from protests or counter-protests," Mamdani told reporters. He said while he thought the ‚Äåfar-right anti-Muslim ‚ÅÝrally was "vile", he defended the "sacred right" of protest, even for objectionable speech.
Police said it was the first time an improvised bomb was deployed in New York City in nearly a decade. In 2017, Akayed Ullah, who also cited Islamic State as inspiration, ignited a homemade bomb strapped to his body in a passage near the Times Square subway station, wounding only himself.
Balat and Kayumi are each charged with five federal crimes, ‚Äãaccording to a complaint filed by ‚Äãthe U.S. attorney's office in Manhattan, ‚ÅÝincluding use of a weapon of mass destruction, attempting to provide material support to a designated foreign terrorist organization, and three charges related to the illegal possession or transportation of explosives.
During their separate police interviews, ‚ÄãBalat asked for a piece of paper and wrote on it he had pledged allegiance to Islamic ‚ÄãState, while Kayumi ‚ÅÝtold police he watched Islamic State videos on his phone, according to the criminal complaint.
A third man, Ian McGinnis, was arrested and appeared in a New York state court on Sunday accused of squirting pepper spray or a similar chemical irritant into the faces of two of the counter-protesters opposing ‚ÅÝthe anti-Islam ‚Äãrally, according to the Manhattan district attorney's office and police. McGinnis faces six charges, including ‚Äãthird-degree assault.
Police in New York have been on alert since the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran, but did not say there was a sign of Saturday's attack being linked ‚Äãto the war.

item.title: Trump says war against Iran is 'very complete,' CBS News reports
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump ‚Äãthinks the war against Iran "is ‚Äåvery complete" and that Washington was "very far ahead" of his initial four ‚Äãto five week estimated ‚Äãtime frame, according to a CBS News ‚ÅÝreporter on X, who cited an ‚Äãinterview with him.
"I think the ‚Äãwar is very complete, pretty much. They have no navy, no communications, they‚Äôve ‚Äãgot no Air Force," Trump ‚Äãwas quoted as saying by CBS News' ‚ÄåWhite ‚ÅÝHouse correspondent Weijia Jiang.
Trump also said the U.S. is "very far" ahead of his initial 4-5 ‚Äãweek estimated ‚Äãtime frame ‚ÅÝfor the war.
On Iran's new Supreme leader Mojtaba ‚ÄãKhamenei, Trump told CBS ‚ÄãNews: "I ‚ÅÝhave no message for him." Trump said he has someone in ‚ÅÝmind ‚Äãto replace Khamenei, ‚Äãbut did not elaborate.

item.title: First Brands row hints at banks‚Äô shadow exposure
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Cockroach troubles tend to extend further than they first appear. JPMorgan boss Jamie Dimon likened high-profile credit ‚Äãblowups to the sneaky insects, implying that deeper problems lurk. And now, six months after heavily indebted auto-parts retailer ‚ÄåFirst Brands toppled, regional lender Western Alliance (WAL.N) , opens new tab has disclosed , opens new tab a $126 million hit from the mess. It never lent directly to First Brands, but instead to a fund run by Wall Street financiers at Jefferies (JEF.N) , opens new tab . It‚Äôs just one of many smaller banks piling into the murky world of shadow finance.
First Brands‚Äô surprise bankruptcy last ‚Äãyear sent a chill through credit markets. Federal prosecutors alleged , opens new tab that it pledged assets multiple times over to secure billions of ‚Äãborrowings, largely from non‚Äëbank lenders. It was not an isolated case, with the government also indicting , opens new tab the ‚ÅÝfounder of sub-prime auto lender Tricolor on similar claims. Jefferies, meanwhile, also lent to now-teetering real-estate lender Market Financial Solutions.
Western Alliance claims a ‚Äãdeliberate breach of contract, saying that Jefferies has ceased to pay $126 million outstanding on loans to special-purpose vehicles that held First Brands‚Äô ‚Äãaccounts receivable. Jefferies said , opens new tab on Monday that these are non-recourse loans, with no guarantee of payment from the SPVs‚Äô parents, and that the suit lacks merit.
Whatever the case, shares of Western Alliance and Jefferies have tanked 11% and 14%, respectively, since Thursday. Morgan Stanley analysts downgraded their recommendation on Jefferies‚Äô shares, citing ‚Äãthe litigation.
Western Alliance is a relative pipsqueak, at roughly $8 billion in market value. Yet many similarly sized institutions have been swept up ‚Äãin the flood of lending to lightly regulated firms that the Federal Reserve labels ‚Äúnon-depository financial institutions.‚Äù Lending to these shadow banks has climbed to $1.9 trillion, ‚Äåup 180% ‚ÅÝfrom $680 billion in 2021, according to Fed data. This growth tracks the vertiginous rise of private credit and asset-backed lending , which have increasingly displaced traditional banks since the 2008 financial crisis. Providing leverage is a way to get a cut of the action back.
The question is how much risk this poses. Banks‚Äô equity is a loss-absorbing buffer, taking the hit if loans go awry en masse. Yet 40 ‚ÄãU.S. lenders with a combined $4.7 ‚Äãtrillion in assets now have ‚ÅÝlent the equivalent of more than 100% of their total equity capital to shadow banks, up from 29 banks holding $1.1 trillion two years ago, according to data from KBRA Financial Intelligence. Nearly all borrowers ‚Äãare current on their payments, and much of the activity relates to the mortgage market, which ‚Äãenjoys effective government ‚ÅÝsupport. Yet the territory in which cockroaches can breed is growing.
Follow Stephen Gandel on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .

item.title: Iran war boosts oil price, but oil major shares are stuck on the sidelines
item.description:
LONDON/HOUSTON, March 9 (Reuters) - Oil prices on Monday climbed to the highest level since 2022, but shares of major producers like Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab ‚Äãand Exxon Mobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab have so far seen limited gains since the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran broke out late last month, ‚Äåsuggesting that traders think the pain in the market may be short term.
While crude oil futures have risen more than 40% since Israel and the U.S. launched joint airstrikes on February 28, the iShares Global Energy ETF - which tracks global energy companies - is up around 2%. That suggests that any immediate gains from increased prices will be temporary, ‚Äãor offset by reduced production.
"The market is anticipating a swift end to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz and a subsequent ‚Äãcollapse in oil prices back to normalized levels," said James West, head of energy and power research at Melius ‚ÅÝResearch. "The rally in oil prices is primarily contained to near-term spot prices rather than longer-dated crude oil futures."
Brent crude futures and U.S. West Texas Intermediate ‚Äãcrude futures rose by around 30% to start the week, as the war on Iran has disrupted the passage of traffic in the Strait of Hormuz, ‚Äãoil and natural gas exports from the Middle East and has forced production stoppages.
At the same time, Brent's front-month contract hit an all-time high at around $36 per barrel over its 6-month delivery contract, indicating near-term supply tightness.
The rally conjured oil futures' 2008 all-time highs at around $147 per barrel, when tension between the West and Iran over its nuclear program, ‚Äãa weak U.S. dollar and inflationary factors pushed prices higher. Continued growth in China and other emerging economies was also expected to offset the ‚Äãimpact of any fall in consumption in developed countries.
However, just months after the all-time high, the 2008 financial crisis and concerns around an overbought market sunk oil to ‚Äåbelow $40 per ‚ÅÝbarrel, at the time the lowest since 2004.
"Go back to 2008," said David Hewitt, senior consultant at Hewitt Energy Perspectives. "Oil stocks followed crude's rally until about $100 then almost completely disconnected as it went to $147."
"The market was right then - $147 per barrel rapidly became $30," he added. "It's a bit of the same now."
Shell shares rose as much as 4.9% on Monday from their close on Feb. 27, while Chevron (CVX.N) , opens new tab gained 2.6% and Exxon gained 0.9% over the same period. ‚ÄãBP (BP.L) , opens new tab gained 7.8%.
Deferred oil contracts are ‚Äãnot as high as the ‚ÅÝspot price, signaling that investors do not expect the supply disruption to last, said Simon Lack, portfolio manager of the Catalyst Energy Infrastructure Fund.
U.S. shale producers, which have seen a bigger boost in share prices than the global ‚Äãoil majors, may be benefitting because U.S. energy supply and infrastructure are safe and insulated from the risk, ‚Äãprompting investors to place ‚ÅÝa higher value on those companies, he added.
Diamondback (FANG.O) , opens new tab , the largest independent producer in the Permian basin, has seen its shares gain as much as 7% on Monday from their close on February 27.
Simon Wong, a portfolio manager at Gabelli Funds, said share prices of Diamondback, APA Corp (APA.O) , opens new tab and Occidental (OXY.N) , opens new tab had risen less ‚ÅÝyear-to-date compared with ‚ÄãExxon and Chevron and are now beginning to catch up.
He also believes that the ‚Äãhigher crude prices are temporary and will likely moderate when the disruptions are over.

item.title: Surging oil drives worries for US stock investors
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters) - A stunning surge in oil prices above $100 a barrel is rattling U.S. stock investors, as they brace for an even sharper rise in energy prices that could stagger the economy and further damage equities.
The 50% jump in U.S. crude to its highest ‚Äãlevel in more than three years is among the most significant financial consequences of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, as investors continue to calibrate economic and market fallout following the ‚Äåattacks launched just over a week ago.
"It's a shock," said Michael Reynolds, vice president of investment strategy at Glenmede. "It's out of left field, and people and investors are having to figure out what it means in real time as it's unfolding."
Higher oil and gas prices stand to drive up costs for energy-intensive companies, erode the discretionary spending budgets of consumers and lead to worries about inflation that could stop the Federal Reserve from lowering interest rates.
Reynolds and other investors were scrambling to ‚Äãgame out scenarios should oil reach heights that were not on the horizon just days earlier.
U.S. and Brent crude on Monday both broke above $100 a barrel, which investors have pointed to as a ‚Äãlevel that could create more stock turbulence. The commodities at one point on Monday neared $120. U.S. crude settled at $67.02 on February 27, the last session before the ‚ÅÝU.S.-Israeli strikes.
Meanwhile, stock volatility has spiked. The Cboe Volatility Index (.VIX) , opens new tab on Monday topped 30 for the first time in nearly a year, up from below 20 in late February.
While the declines for U.S. stocks have been moderate ‚Äãin comparison to other global regions, the benchmark S&P 500 (.SPX) , opens new tab was last down nearly 4% from its late January all-time closing high, after paring steeper declines earlier in Monday's session.
Strategists at Yardeni Research said last Tuesday they ‚Äãexpected a 10% stock market correction.
"Now we can't rule out a bear market and even a recession," the firm said in a note on Sunday.
OIL AND STOCKS: MORE TIGHTLY LINKED
As oil prices have surged, the moves have become more tightly linked to the stock market.
The 20-day correlation between the S&P 500 and U.S. crude stood at -0.813 as of Monday morning, according to LSEG data, a strong inverse relationship that shows they have been tending to move in opposite directions.
Deutsche Bank strategists, watching whether the ‚ÄãIran situation could prompt a larger risk-off move, said in a note on Monday that the oil price shock ranks "among the more serious of history," but that investors are pricing in "a short rather than protracted conflict."
While stocks ‚Äãand oil are typically thought of as separate markets, stock investors have watched oil's trajectory quite closely at times historically, especially after extreme price moves.
In early 2022, a jump in oil over $120 a barrel following the start of the conflict ‚Äåin Ukraine coincided ‚ÅÝwith declines in stock prices. In 2015-2016, stock investors worried that low oil prices, with U.S. crude dropping below $30 a barrel, represented a sign of broad economic weakness.
Focus is again on the economic ramifications of rising oil prices.
Each 10% increase in the oil price stands to translate to roughly a 15 to 20 basis-point drag on GDP growth, according to JPMorgan economists.
"Effects also could be non-linear, with larger oil price spikes producing an even larger hit to growth," the JPMorgan economists said in a note.
The JPMorgan economists and other analysts cautioned the economic fallout likely hinges on how long crude prices stay elevated.
For now, the jump in ‚Äãcrude is driving up prices at the gas pump. ‚ÄãThe national average for gasoline rose to $3.478 a ‚ÅÝgallon on Monday, up from $2.902 a month ago, according to data from the motorists' group AAA. That is the highest level since the summer of 2024, the group said.
"From an average consumer perspective, oil prices are about ... as visceral as it gets just because of filling up your gas tank," said Kevin Gordon, head of macro ‚Äãresearch and strategy at Charles Schwab.
Indeed, shares of companies most reliant on discretionary spending are among the potentially most vulnerable to higher oil prices.
Airlines, for which ‚Äãfuel accounts for 20% to ‚ÅÝ25% of unit costs, according to Morningstar, have seen their shares hammered with the S&P 1500 passenger airlines index (.SPCOMAIR) , opens new tab down 15% since the conflict began.
Of course, higher oil prices could benefit some corners of the market. The S&P 500 energy sector (.SPNY) , opens new tab has gained 1% since late February, while the broader S&P 500 has fallen over 2% in that time.
And some investors are wary that the situation could change at any moment.
In a note ‚ÅÝon Monday, Raymond ‚ÄãJames Chief Investment Officer Larry Adam said the wealth management firm expects the conflict to be "relatively short-lived." The firm maintained a year-end ‚Äãprice target for U.S. crude at $55-$60 a barrel.
Investors are also mindful of how U.S. President Donald Trump has changed course on market-sensitive policies during his term. In particular, there was his softening of his blanket "Liberation Day" tariff policies last April that caused a sharp rebound in ‚Äãinitially battered asset prices.
"We're one ceasefire agreement headline away from all of this just reversing in a very aggressive fashion," Gordon said.

item.title: Australia to grant visa to five Iranian women soccer players after Trump appeal
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 10 (Reuters) - Australia on Tuesday agreed to provide ‚Äãvisas to five Iranian ‚Äåwomen's soccer players to stay in the country, after ‚ÄãU.S. President Donald Trump ‚Äãurged Canberra not to ‚ÅÝsend them back to ‚ÄãTehran due to concerns ‚Äãover their safety.
Home Affairs Minister Tony Burke told reporters the other ‚Äãmembers of the ‚Äãteam were welcome to stay back ‚Äåin ‚ÅÝAustralia. The team is in Australia for the Asian Cup tournament.
Trump said ‚Äãon Monday ‚Äãhe ‚ÅÝhad spoken to Australian Prime Minister ‚ÄãAnthony Albanese about the ‚ÄãIran ‚ÅÝwomen's soccer team after reports that five players ‚ÅÝhad ‚Äãsought asylum ‚Äãin Australia.

item.title: US weighs easing Russian oil sanctions to cool global price surge, sources say
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump 's administration is considering reducing oil sanctions on Russia to help cool ‚Äãa surge in global energy prices triggered by ‚Äåthe U.S. and Israeli war on Iran , with an announcement possible as soon as Monday, according to three sources familiar with the planning.
The ‚Äãmove would be intended to boost world supplies ‚Äãof oil amid massive disruptions to Middle East shipments ‚ÅÝfrom the expanding conflict, but could also complicate U.S. ‚Äãefforts to deprive Russia of revenue for its war in ‚ÄãUkraine.
The discussions could include broad sanctions relief as well as more targeted options that would allow certain countries, such as India, to ‚Äãbuy Russian oil without fear of U.S. penalties, including ‚Äãtariffs, the sources told Reuters, speaking on condition of anonymity.
The United States ‚Äålast ‚ÅÝweek allowed India to temporarily purchase Russian crude oil already on tankers at sea to help it cope with the cuts to Middle East supply.
The new moves could be ‚Äãannounced as soon ‚Äãas Monday, ‚ÅÝthe sources said.
"President Trump and his entire energy team have had a strong game ‚Äãplan to keep the energy markets stable well ‚Äãbefore ‚ÅÝOperation Epic Fury began, and they will continue to review all credible options," said Taylor Rogers, a White House spokesperson, ‚ÅÝusing ‚Äãthe administration's term for the war.
"Any ‚Äãpolicy announcement will come directly from the President or his team," Rogers said.

item.title: Kremlin: Putin, in a phone call with Trump, shares¬Ýproposals to end Iran war quickly
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 9 (Reuters) - Russian President Vladimir Putin, in a telephone conversation on Monday with ‚ÄãU.S. President Donald Trump , put forward proposals for a quick ‚Äåsettlement of the conflict around Iran , Kremlin foreign policy aide Yuri Ushakov said.
Ushakov, giving an account of the conversation to reporters, said the two ‚Äãleaders also discussed the conflict in Ukraine and noted that ‚ÄãRussian gains should prompt Kyiv's negotiators to move ‚ÅÝtowards a settlement.
He described the entire discussion as "very substantial" and ‚Äãsaid it would "likely have practical significance for further work between ‚Äãthe two countries".
Ushakov said Putin "expressed several thoughts aimed at a quick political and diplomatic end to the Iranian conflict, including contacts that have taken ‚Äãplace with leaders of Gulf states, the president of ‚ÄãIran and leaders of other countries".
He said Trump "offered his assessment of developments ‚Äåin ‚ÅÝthe U.S.-Israeli operation. Let me say that a very substantial and, without doubt, useful exchange of ideas took place."
Ushakov said Trump believed it was in the U.S. interest to see a "rapid end ‚Äãto the ‚Äãconflict in Ukraine ‚ÅÝwith a ceasefire and a long-term settlement".
Developments in Ukraine, he said, were characterised by "Russian troops ‚Äãadvancing quite successfully. This, as was noted, is ‚Äãa ‚ÅÝfactor that should prompt the regime in Kyiv to finally go down the path of negotiating a settlement to the conflict".
Ushakov ‚ÅÝalso ‚Äãsaid the two presidents also discussed ‚Äãthe situation in Venezuela in the context of global oil markets.

item.title: Iranian women's soccer players given asylum after Trump speaks with Australian PM
item.description:
WASHINGTON/SYDNEY, March 10 (Reuters) - Australia on Tuesday agreed to provide visas to five of Iran's women's soccer team players to ‚Äãstay in the country after they sought asylum fearing persecution in their home nation.
The announcement came after U.S. President Donald Trump said he had ‚Äåspoken to Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese about the Iran women's soccer team in Australia following reports that the players had requested asylum.
The remaining players are in a hotel on the Gold Coast, Home Affairs Minister Tony Burke told a news conference, adding that he had also offered the other team members the chance to stay in Australia.
"I was able to tell ‚Äãfive members of the Iranian women's soccer team that they are welcome to stay in Australia, that they are safe here, and they should ‚Äãfeel at home here," Burke told reporters.
The Iranian women's soccer team are in Australia for the Asian Cup tournament.
Iran's semi-official ‚ÅÝFars news agency said five players in Australia for the Asian Cup secretly left the team hotel with Australian police, and media reports said they were ‚Äãseeking Australian government assistance after "breaking free".
Having initially posted on social media that Australia was "making a terrible humanitarian mistake" by allowing the team to be sent back home, Trump said in a ‚Äãlater post that he had spoken to Albanese and that the Australian leader was "doing a very good job having to do with this rather delicate situation".
Trump said five members of the Iran squad "have already been taken care of, and the rest are on their way.
"Some, however, feel they must go back because they are worried about the safety of their families, ‚Äãincluding threats to those family members if they don‚Äôt return," he said.
The Iranian team's campaign in the Australian-hosted Asian Cup tournament started just as the U.S. and ‚ÄãIsrael launched air strikes on Iran , killing the Islamic Republic's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei. They were eliminated from the tournament on Sunday after losing 2-0 to the Philippines .
In his earlier post Trump said members of ‚Äåthe team ‚ÅÝwould "likely be killed" if forced to return to Iran. "The U.S. will take them if you won‚Äôt, " he added.
Soccer's global players' union FIFPRO said on Monday there were serious concerns for the welfare of the Iran team after they were labelled for refusing to sing their national anthem before a game.
The players' decision to stand in silence during Iran's anthem before their first match against South Korea was labelled by a commentator on Islamic Republic of Iran Broadcasting as the "pinnacle of dishonour".
Iranian ‚Äãmedia quoted Farideh Shojaei, vice president for ‚Äãwomen's affairs at the Iranian Football ‚ÅÝFederation, as saying the team had left the hotel through the back door with the police.
"We have contacted the embassy, the football federation, the foreign ministry and anywhere possible to see what will happen," she said. "We have even spoken with ‚Äãthe families of these five players.‚Äù
Iranian media named the players as Zahra Sarbali, Mona Hamoudi, Zahra Ghanbari, Fatemeh Pasandideh ‚Äãand Atefeh Ramazani-Zadeh.
It said ‚ÅÝother team members were still in Australia and quoted Shojaei as saying the squad had planned to return to Iran via Dubai, but the United Arab Emirates had not allowed them to do so.
It said efforts were now expected to be made for the team to return via Malaysia and Turkey.
The Iranian team sang their national anthem and saluted ‚ÅÝbefore their second ‚Äãmatch against Australia , sparking fears among human rights campaigners that the women had been coerced by ‚Äãgovernment minders.
Australia granted emergency humanitarian visas to over 20 members of the Afghanistan women's cricket team after the Taliban returned to power in 2021.

item.title: Cuban university students stage rare protest amid energy, internet shortages
item.description:
HAVANA, March 9 (Reuters) - A group of just over 20 Cuban university students staged a rare, hours-long protest at ‚Äãthe University of Havana on Monday, angry over class disruptions amid a U.S. oil blockade that has contributed to ‚Äåa near collapse of Cuba's electrical grid.
Students sat under a hot mid-morning sun, shading themselves with umbrellas while trading barbs with several of the university's professors and administration staff, who arrived shortly after the "sit-in" began.
"None of us wanted to get up early today in the dark, make a thermos of coffee, and sit ‚Äãhere in the sun," said Anabel Oliva, a 20-year-old university student who spoke before the group. "But there was no other ‚Äãway."
Students said officials had failed to take adequate measures to prevent major disruptions to classes as the ‚ÅÝUnited States has ratcheted up sanctions and pressure on the Caribbean island nation.
The United States in January cut off oil shipments from ‚ÄãVenezuela, which further decimated an already failing electrical grid, starving it of fuel and forcing ripple effects on transportation, food supply and internet ‚Äãcommunications.
Cuba announced a series of measures in January, including distance learning as during the COVID-19 pandemic, that officials said would assure continuity for students of the island's once-robust higher-education system.
Students said those measures were not enough, and opted to protest to ensure their voices were heard.
Jose Julian Diaz, a university professor and ‚Äãformer student representative, told Reuters the extraordinary circumstances required dialogue.
"These kinds of scenarios are really rare," he said in an interview. "I think we ‚Äãhave to learn to have these discussions with students."
University security initially prohibited the protesting students from sitting on the stone steps at the main entrance ‚Äåof the ‚ÅÝUniversity of Havana, blocking the entrance with a long rope and a sign that read: "Do not pass, do not sit."
The students eventually walked up the steps and past the rope, however, prompting a rush of security guards who were eventually called off by Higher Education Vice Minister Modesto Ricardo Gomez.
The vice minister told reporters the students had raised legitimate concerns, and blamed the ‚ÄãTrump administration's tightening sanctions for ‚Äãthe problems. He said there ‚ÅÝwould be no repercussions for those who protested.
"There are no consequences whatsoever. They have a concern, and we are addressing it. It will always be like this," Gomez said.
While the minister spoke to reporters, ‚Äãthe students left the steps and entered the university with administration officials and teachers, declining requests for ‚Äãinterviews.
The minister said ‚ÅÝthe dialogue would continue inside the university.
One student, who did not give his name to a reporter but spoke before cameras, said there would be more protests if the issues were not resolved.
"We are willing to engage in dialogue if it is to represent our demands, but if ‚ÅÝdialogue, as ‚Äãhas already happened, does not work, we will sit down here again.‚Äù
