Kharkiv, ‚Äãlocated ‚Äã30 km (18 ‚ÅÝmiles) from the Russian border, withstood early ‚Äãadvances by Russian forces ‚Äãafter ‚ÅÝtheir full-scale invasion in February 2022 and has since been ‚ÅÝa ‚Äãfrequent target of Russian ‚Äãair attacks.

item.title: Trump says white South Africans are persecuted; some are returning to a better life
item.description:
JOHANNESBURG, March 11 (Reuters) - Andrew Veitch left South Africa after being held up at gunpoint in his car. But now he feels there are greater threats in the United States, he said, citing mass shootings in public ‚Äãplaces as well as violence by U.S. immigration officers.
"People are being shot in broad daylight. American citizens are being shot and killed," said the 53-year-old, who moved to California in ‚Äå2003. "I don't want to live in a place like this."
President Donald Trump's officials have said Immigration and Customs Enforcement officers were justified in firing the shots that killed two U.S. citizens in January, although video evidence has contradicted their accounts.
Veitch plans to return to South Africa this year, one of thousands of white South Africans coming back, despite Trump's statements that the white minority is being persecuted by the country's Black majority government.
Pretoria says there is no evidence of discrimination or persecution against whites. Many have left since the ‚Äãend of white minority rule in 1994, some citing crime and difficulty getting jobs, but many are also returning.
Veitch is among 12,000 people who have checked their citizenship status in an online portal ‚Äãlaunched by the government in November after the overturning of a 1995 law that stripped citizenship from some South Africans who left.
They represent a fraction of South Africans ‚ÅÝabroad. The latest official statistics on returnees, from 2022, show that almost 15,000 white South Africans returned that year.
Home Affairs Minister Leon Schreiber said 1,000 people had reclaimed their ‚Äãcitizenship, a number he expected to grow significantly as the programme takes off.
"There is definitely a sense of optimism for South Africans abroad," said Schreiber, part of the white-led Democratic Alliance party that has ruled in coalition with ‚Äãthe African National Congress since 2024. He is a returnee himself, having spent time in the U.S. and Germany before coming home in 2019.
Two recruitment agencies that help expats relocate said the number of inquiries had jumped, and Reuters spoke to 10 South Africans who had either returned or were planning to, seven of them from across Europe and three from the United States.
Their reasons, echoed in a 25,000-strong "Return to South Africa" Facebook group some belong to, included being closer to family, lower living costs and ‚Äãpolitical turmoil abroad.
The Trump administration is ramping up its new refugee programme for white South Africans, focusing on Afrikaners, the descendants of Dutch settlers. About 3,500 South Africans have entered the United States as refugees since the programme ‚Äãstarted in May 2025.
Applicants interviewed by Reuters complained of being victims of racially motivated crime and employment equity laws that favour non-white candidates in order to redress decades of white minority rule.
Other Afrikaners, like Naomi Saphire, take a different view.
She had been ‚Äåsettled in ‚ÅÝthe United States for two decades when she came back for a holiday and realized how much she missed home.
Last year, she left North Carolina for a seaside town in South Africa's Western Cape, where she said her three children spend more time outdoors, health insurance is affordable and she prefers the schools.
"My heart is just full of gratefulness to be here," the 46-year-old said from her home in Plettenberg Bay. "The U.S. has been really good to me (but) I just felt like I was depriving my kids of this life."
Saphire said she knows many people who are returning home.
Crime and joblessness are major issues in South Africa, but ‚Äãthe unemployment rate is 35% for Black people compared ‚Äãwith 8% for whites, according to the latest ‚ÅÝfigures from the national statistics agency Stats SA.
Police statistics released last year showed that even farm murders, which Trump has focused on, killed more Black people than whites. Reuters has found that photos and videos Trump has presented on the matter were taken out of context or misrepresented.
Still, Stats SA estimated a net outward flow of half a million ‚Äãwhites since 2001, including 95,000 from 2021 to 2026. There is no regular data on returnees, but a Stats SA analysis showed that 28,000 South Africans ‚Äãreturned in 2022, 52.9% - or some ‚ÅÝ14,800 - of whom were white.
Anton van Heerden, CEO of employment agency DNA Employer of Record, said inquiries from white South Africans seeking to return had jumped 70% in the past six months. Angel Jones, CEO of Johannesburg-based recruitment firm HomecomingEx, reported a roughly 30% rise in inquiries since 2024.
A boom in remote working since the COVID-19 pandemic has also helped; three of the returnees interviewed by Reuters kept their jobs abroad.
Many South African professionals have extensive private security at home ‚ÅÝwhich minimizes ‚Äãcrime risks, Van Heerden said.
"If you can afford to live in a safe environment, you can have a much better life than I ‚Äãthink in most places in the northern hemisphere," he said.
Several returnees also said they felt life in South Africa had improved since they left. Power cuts, which used to be daily, for example, have largely stopped.
Thirty-eight-year-old engineer Eugene Jansen, who returned from the Netherlands in December ‚Äãwith his wife and two children, said the returnees he knows feel things are getting better.
"The opinion is that the country is improving," he said.

item.title: Australia's competition regulator to heighten fuel market scrutiny amid Mideast war
item.description:
March 11 (Reuters) - Australia's competition regulator ‚Äãwill seek explanations from fuel retailers ‚Äåon pricing during the current Middle East crisis, the regulator said on Wednesday, owing to ‚Äãconsumer concerns about price spikes and ‚Äãdistribution issues in regional and rural ‚ÅÝparts of the country.
Global oil prices ‚Äãhad surged to their highest since 2022 earlier ‚Äãin the week as Saudi Arabia and other OPEC members cut supplies amid the U.S.-Israeli war ‚Äãon Iran.
The Australian Competition and Consumer ‚ÄãCommission said it will also commence weekly market updates ‚Äåto ‚ÅÝprovide increased transparency to consumers and enhanced scrutiny of retailers‚Äô behaviour.
The regulator, which wrote to petrol retailers last week seeking ‚Äãinformation about ‚Äãrecent ‚ÅÝprice increases, will take action against them if consumers are misled ‚Äãon rapid increase in prices ‚Äãor ‚ÅÝfor breaches of the competition provisions, Commissioner Anna Brakey said.
The regulator is also prioritising ‚ÅÝmeasures ‚Äãto assist with diesel ‚Äãdistribution issues in regional and rural areas.

item.title: Australia expects Iran war to intensify, shuts some Middle East embassies
item.description: SYDNEY, March 11 (Reuters) - Australia has closed its embassies in Abu Dhabi and Tel Aviv as well ‚Äãas its consulate in Dubai, the ‚Äåforeign minister said on Wednesday, amid fears the conflict in the Middle East could intensify.

item.title: Iran war shatters Trump's case for fossil fuels
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 (Reuters) - The economic risks of the world‚Äôs heavy dependence on oil and gas have been laid bare twice in four years - first with Russia‚Äôs invasion of Ukraine and now with the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran - undermining U.S. President Donald Trump‚Äôs flagship push to double down on fossil fuels.
The dramatic supply disruptions and price spikes unleashed by the latest Middle East conflict are ‚Äãlikely to harden the resolve of many governments to accelerate the drive for greater energy self‚Äësufficiency ‚Äì and that will include clean energy.
The ongoing Middle East conflict has seen the closure ‚Äåof the vital Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly 20% of global oil and gas shipments flow, sending shockwaves through energy markets.
While Brent crude prices have dropped from $119 per barrel on Monday - their highest level since June 2022 - to around $90, the global benchmark is still more than 24% higher since the start of the war.
In any case, the physical shortages that are rapidly developing are heaping pressure on economies worldwide, reviving fears of higher inflation, weaker industrial output and slower growth globally. The pain has been particularly acute in Asia, ‚Äãwhich relies on the Middle East for nearly 60% of its crude oil imports.
The latest crisis is a reminder that fossil fuels, like renewables, can be unreliable and volatile ‚Äì and also that securing ‚Äãdomestic sources of energy is critical.
This, in turn, is altering the debate about the global energy system‚Äôs two supposedly competing objectives: tackling climate change and ensuring access ‚ÅÝto cheap, reliable energy.
It turns out the latter may end up helping with the former.
THE ERA OF ENERGY SHOCKS
In the debate about whether to prioritize cutting fossil fuels or keeping energy cheap, the pendulum swung decisively ‚Äãtoward the energy transition after the signing of the 2015 United Nations‚Äëbacked Paris climate agreement . This triggered a wave of investment in renewable power and a sharp pullback in spending on oil, gas and coal ‚Äì the main sources ‚Äãof greenhouse gas emissions.
However, Russia‚Äôs full‚Äëscale invasion of Ukraine in 2022 delivered a brutal wake‚Äëup call.
Europe‚Äôs heavy reliance on imported Russian gas was exposed as a significant vulnerability when supplies collapsed, sending prices soaring and compounding global inflation just as economies were emerging from the COVID‚Äë19 pandemic.
That extreme volatility forced governments from Europe to Asia into rapid action . Some fast‚Äëtracked solar and wind projects to insulate their economies from future shocks. But many also ramped up domestic production of gas and coal, and others - notably China and India - ‚Äãembraced an unapologetic ‚Äúall-of-the-above‚Äù approach.
The crisis highlighted a stubborn reality: fossil fuels remain deeply embedded in the global economy. Despite record growth in renewables last year, oil and gas still account for almost 60% of global energy demand, ‚Äãaccording to the Energy Institute, underscoring how far the world remains from any rapid exit - and how exposed it remains to future shocks.
NO NET ZERO?
The Russia-Ukraine war is not the only force that has reshaped energy geopolitics over the ‚Äåpast decade. Another ‚ÅÝtectonic shift is the rise of the U.S. as the world‚Äôs top oil and gas producer, powered by the shale revolution.
Since taking office more than a year ago, Trump has championed U.S. ‚Äúenergy dominance,‚Äù seeking to unleash fossil fuel development while rolling back support for renewable energy.
The Trump administration, alongside a growing chorus of politicians globally, has attacked net‚Äëzero ambitions, which refer to the point where greenhouse gas emissions are balanced by the amount removed from the atmosphere.
U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright slammed net-zero as a ‚Äúdestructive illusion‚Äù during an International Energy Agency (IEA) meeting last month, arguing that the shift to renewables imposes unnecessary costs on consumers already strained by inflation.
The price tag attached to building new renewable ‚Äãpower systems is certainly daunting.
Global investment in renewables would ‚Äãneed to climb to about $1.4 trillion per year ‚ÅÝby 2030, from roughly $624 billion in 2024, to meet emissions‚Äëreduction targets, according to the International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA). The IEA estimates that spending on electricity grids alone must rise by around 50% by 2030 from today‚Äôs roughly $400 billion a year.
Even if these enormous sums were met, the notion that the world can quickly switch from fossil fuels to ‚Äãrenewables is clearly unrealistic.
But when viewing the situation through the lens of energy security, it makes a lot of sense for governments to look beyond ‚Äãfossil fuels. Indeed, it appears ‚ÅÝnegligent not to.
Consider that in the past week, a single country has been able to upend the global energy system by blocking one waterway that is a mere 39 kilometers (24 miles) wide at its narrowest point. If that were to persist, it could bring significant segments of the global economy to a veritable standstill.
Governments will take notice, especially the most vulnerable economies, and will likely seek to develop more domestic sources of energy, whether they be fossil ‚ÅÝfuels or renewables.
The ‚Äãpush for energy security may thus end up doing what climate policy alone could not: pushing countries to move faster toward a ‚Äãmore diversified and resilient energy system.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Ron Bousso , a columnist for Reuters.)

And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters ‚Äãapp , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Mideast conflict may hasten Europe‚Äôs gas divorce from Russia
item.description:
SOFIA, March 11 (Reuters) - The natural gas price shock Europe is experiencing from the Iran war could accelerate the region‚Äôs decoupling from Russian energy, while the continent is pushed further into the arms of the U.S.
The Iranian attack that forced QatarEnergy, the world's second-largest liquefied natural gas exporter , to halt production last week sent European benchmark gas prices surging ‚Äãby nearly 50%. This underscored how exposed the continent remains to geopolitical shocks beyond its borders and the necessity of prioritizing energy security above all else.
Qatar supplied only about 4% of European Union gas ‚Äåimports in 2025, according to the European Council , opens new tab , but with these volumes now unavailable, the marginal gas molecule will increasingly come from the U.S., the world's top gas producer and LNG exporter.
This, in turn, could give the U.S. even more leverage to push Europe to accelerate its full decoupling from Russian gas. That is something Western leaders have sought to achieve through sanctions since Moscow's invasion of Ukraine four years ago.
Any temporary easing of U.S. sanctions on Russian oil during the war shouldn‚Äôt alter this trajectory for gas. And Russian President Vladimir Putin‚Äôs threat last week to halt ‚ÄãRussia‚Äôs remaining gas exports to Europe will provide further impetus for reducing this dependency.
Russian gas still accounts for roughly 10% of EU imports. The EU has mandated that all Russian gas imports must end by September 2027, but ‚Äãlegal ambiguities and loopholes could prolong dependence on Russian gas beyond 2028.
RUSSIA'S LINGERING GRIP ON EASTERN EUROPE
While the U.S. now supplies the majority of Europe‚Äôs LNG, Russia‚Äôs state-controlled ‚ÅÝgiant Gazprom (GAZP.MM) , opens new tab remains the dominant supplier of the super-chilled fuel in Central and Eastern Europe and Southeastern Europe. These regions are also the destination for almost all of the Russian pipeline gas flowing into Europe, with the remainder ‚Äãentering EU markets via the TurkStream pipeline system linking Russia and Turkey.
Unlike Northern Europe, which has rapidly diversified its energy operations in recent years through new LNG terminals and pipeline interconnections, Central and Southern countries remain structurally exposed. Limited storage, fragmented ‚Äãtransmission tariffs and poorly integrated markets have kept Russian pipeline gas commercially more attractive.
This is why, at the Transatlantic Gas Security Summit , opens new tab held in Washington in late February, just days before the conflict began, the Americans had a clear message: accelerate the flow of U.S. LNG into Europe's most vulnerable markets.
NEW ENERGY FRONTIER
To achieve this, senior U.S. officials and European energy ministers are focused on a new flagship project: the Vertical Gas Corridor linking Greek LNG terminals with Bulgaria, Romania, Moldova and Ukraine.
The Vertical Gas Corridor could fundamentally reorient Central and Southern ‚ÄãEuropean gas trade flows toward Atlantic supply chains, locking in long-term LNG imports and making the Balkans the next frontier market for exporters.
As part of this effort, major U.S. exporters including Cheniere (LNG.N) , opens new tab and Venture Global (VG.N) , opens new tab have agreed to supply around 8 billion ‚Äãcubic metres (bcm) per year under 20‚Äëyear contracts with traders and governments in Central and Eastern Europe. That is equivalent to roughly 10% of U.S. LNG exports to the EU in 2025.
Those buyers have in turn signed non-binding import deals with European intermediaries to support ‚Äåthe flow of ‚ÅÝLNG through these regions.
European leaders‚Äô willingness to lock themselves into long-term gas contracts after spending over a decade speaking about the need to reduce fossil fuel consumption highlights Europe‚Äôs new reality: energy security concerns outweigh decarbonisation needs.
OBSTACLES TO A U.S. LNG SURGE
The success of the Vertical Gas Corridor is far from guaranteed. Cross-border tariffs between Central and Southern European countries remain prohibitively high. However, regional operators are proposing a new ‚Äúsuper-bundled‚Äù agreement that would allow suppliers to reserve the entire corridor in a single contract.
Infrastructure bottlenecks also persist. LNG terminals lack sufficient storage, meaning underground facilities in Bulgaria and Ukraine would need to be expanded to enable large-scale flows.
Additionally, without EU financial support to cover the extra costs needed for transmission system ‚Äãoperators to upgrade the grid, Russian gas delivered via existing ‚Äãpipelines will remain cheaper, undermining diversification efforts.
Finally, for ‚ÅÝthe Vertical Gas Corridor to function at scale, LNG must flow north from Turkey. The country hosts substantial underutilised regasification capacity, totalling 58 bcm annually, based on my calculations using data from Argus , opens new tab , enough to increase U.S. LNG supply to Europe by an estimated 70%. Around half of that capacity could potentially be used to ship volumes along the Corridor ‚Äãto Ukraine and Central Europe.
In practice, however, Russian gas still occupies a significant share of Turkish transit capacity through TurkStream. And in an increasingly fractured geopolitical world, Turkey ‚Äãmay be unwilling to align ‚ÅÝwith the interests of Europe and the U.S., especially if the financial incentives still make the alternative more attractive.
The U.S. has framed its LNG exports as essential to Europe‚Äôs economic security, industrial growth, and even its emerging infrastructure demand ‚Äì and the conflict in Iran has shown that, under current circumstances, that is probably correct.
Some may question whether deepening this dependence is prudent, especially given America‚Äôs decision to pursue the war in Iran despite knowing the potential disruption it could cause to the global energy ‚ÅÝsystem.
But Europe has ‚Äãfew other options, and reducing Moscow‚Äôs influence over those EU economies most exposed to political and economic pressure may be worth the risk.
(The ‚Äãviews expressed here are those of Martin Vladimirov , Director of the Geoeconomics Program of the Center for the Study of Democracy (CSD))

item.title: The dangerous politics of US oil-driven redistribution
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 (Reuters) - U.S. stock market indexes have weathered this month's oil shock well so far because investors expect only a glancing overall GDP hit from higher energy costs. But that resilience masks a potentially toxic shift of income from households to "Big Energy" - and packs big risks in an election year.
With uncertainty still rife about the extent and length of the Iran war , sharp ‚Äãand volatile spikes in energy prices have left oil and natural gas costs looking structurally higher over the coming year. Worries about food prices are creeping in too, as fertilizer ‚Äåshipments are also being snagged in the Middle East Gulf region.
As inflation is spurred once again, the chances of U.S. interest rate cuts this year have been reduced or pushed back.
None of that sounds like good news for the U.S. economy or consumers overall, at least not until you make calculations about America's new-found position as a net exporter of energy.
For the producers and exporters of all that domestic U.S. oil and natural gas, this is a ‚Äãbig windfall. And unlike their competitors in the Middle East or elsewhere, there's no security threat or blockage in getting their product to market.
Carlyle strategist Jason Thomas points out ‚Äãthat the U.S. ran an annualized energy trade surplus of nearly $100 billion in the last 12 months and the surge in oil and gas ‚ÅÝprices will only increase that substantially.
"Higher gasoline prices still could be conceived of as a 'tax' on U.S. consumers, but that's offset today by an increase in domestic revenues that didn't ‚Äãexist when the energy price shock of 2007-08 shaved over 1% from U.S. GDP," he wrote, pointing to the period in which oil and gas prices roughly trebled in 18 months.
But ‚Äãwith the U.S. now a net exporter of petroleum products for the first time since the 1950s, assumptions of a 2007‚Äì08‚Äëstyle GDP hit - one that could halve U.S. growth on a comparable oil price surge today - now look wide of the mark.
GLANCING BLOW
Apollo Global (APO.N) , opens new tab Chief Economist Torsten Slok's "ready reckoner" - a quick rule‚Äëof‚Äëthumb guide built from Federal Reserve models - shows that even if crude oil stays above $100 a barrel through 2027, the shock's impact fades over time and leaves only minor aggregate impacts on the ‚Äãmacro economy.
While the net impact on headline inflation could be as high as 0.7 percentage point, the effect on real GDP, unemployment and core inflation is just 0.1 point, he ‚Äãshowed.
"The U.S. is a net oil exporter and energy efficiency has improved significantly over the years - meaning the economy burns less oil per unit of GDP compared to historical levels (and) helps dampen the overall negative impact ‚Äåof price spikes."
Not ‚ÅÝeveryone thinks it would be such plain sailing if the shock persists.
Morgan Stanley estimates that if a 25% oil price jump driven by a supply shock lasts four quarters, real GDP would be about 1.5% lower, with most of the damage occurring in the first three.
But the issue is that GDP can hold quite well even though everyone seems to acknowledge that higher energy prices, and inflation more broadly, act as a tax on households.
ELECTION YEAR PRESSURES
If the impact of a sustained oil shock were to have the minimal GDP impacts some suggest, then all that's really happening is that ‚Äãmoney is being redistributed to domestic energy ‚Äãcompanies from already stretched households.
Government could step ‚ÅÝin to subsidize and cushion households, but that simply shifts the burden onto an already stretched Treasury - and ultimately back onto the public.
The political toxicity of high U.S. headline inflation - even if the Fed focuses on core rates that strip out energy and food - has been well documented since the COVID-19 pandemic.
In ‚Äãa midterm election year , sensitivities in Washington will be even higher, which helps explain why Wall Street seems convinced the conflict will be limited and that ‚Äãit should buy the ‚ÅÝdip as GDP sails on regardless.
But a raid on household wallets that just ends up in domestic oil and gas firms' coffers might not go down well at the polls, especially if voters believe the oil surge was triggered by U.S. military action in the first place.
(The opinions expressed here are those of the author, a columnist for Reuters.)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source ‚ÅÝfor global ‚Äãfinancial commentary.
Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear ‚ÄãReuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Carlyle to sell Colombian oil firm SierraCol to Philippine's Prime Infrastructure
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 (Reuters) - Private equity group Carlyle (CG.O) , opens new tab has agreed to sell its Colombian oil producer SierraCol to Prime Infrastructure Capital, the infrastructure unit ‚Äãof Filipino businessman Enrique K. Razon Jr., for an undisclosed ‚Äåsum, the U.S. company said on Wednesday.
Carlyle, which set up SierraCol in 2020 after buying assets from Occidental Petroleum (OXY.N) , opens new tab , had sought around $1.5 billion for the Colombian ‚Äãfirm, sources had told Reuters in 2025.
Elsewhere in the oil ‚Äãand gas sector, Carlyle in January reached a non-binding, initial ‚ÅÝagreement to buy most international assets from sanctioned Russian firm Lukoil (LKOH.MM) , opens new tab ‚Äãand merge its European refining vehicle Moeve with Portuguese energy firm Galp's (GALP.LS) , opens new tab downstream ‚Äãbusiness .
"This is where our track record is strong and I expect to continue that. We have a clear playbook for executing complex carve-outs and strengthening these businesses," ‚Äãsaid co-head of Carlyle International Energy Partners (CIEP), Bob Maguire.
He said CIEP ‚Äãhad no fixed views on how much investment to allocate to downstream or upstream ‚Äåacquisitions.
CIEP ‚ÅÝmanaging director Parminder Singh told Reuters that it has been tough to extract assets from the bigger players in the current market as majors are keen to boost their own oil and gas reserves ‚Äãwhile retrenching on ‚Äãlow-carbon projects.
Carlyle said ‚ÅÝit has invested around $1 billion in SierraCol since 2020, mainly spending on the firm's existing assets to ‚Äãstabilise its net production at around 45,000 barrels of ‚Äãoil equivalent ‚ÅÝper day and reduce operational emissions.
SierraCol's gross output of 77,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day makes up around 10% of Colombia's overall production.
SierraCol ‚ÅÝhad $205 ‚Äãmillion in free cashflow for the 12 ‚Äãmonths to October 2025 and net debt of $618 million, according to its website.
Prime Infrastructure runs ‚Äãenergy, waste and water infrastructure.

item.title: Israeli strike hits Beirut apartment block, Lebanese media reports
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 11 (Reuters) - An Israeli strike hit an apartment block in central Beirut on Wednesday, Lebanon's state news ‚Äãagency reported, further widening Israeli attacks in the ‚Äåcapital beyond the Hezbollah-controlled southern suburbs where heavy strikes continued.
Footage showed extensive damage to two floors of the apartment ‚Äãblock in the Aicha Bakkar neighbourhood, and smoke ‚Äãrising from the building.
There was no immediate comment ‚ÅÝfrom the Israeli military and no immediate reports ‚Äãof casualties.
It would mark the second Israeli strike in ‚Äãthe heart of Beirut in four days. On Sunday, an Israeli strike hit a hotel in the seafront Raouche neighbourhood. The Israeli ‚Äãmilitary said that strike targeted five senior members ‚Äãof Iran's Revolutionary Guards Quds Force.
Lebanese authorities say nearly 570 people ‚Äåhave ‚ÅÝbeen killed by Israeli strikes since Iran-backed Hezbollah opened fire on March 2 to avenge the killing of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei at the start ‚Äãof the U.S.-Israeli war ‚Äãon Iran.
Israel ‚ÅÝkept up heavy strikes on the southern suburbs, known as Dahiyeh, overnight. The ‚ÄãIsraeli military has ordered residents of the ‚Äãpredominantly Shi'ite ‚ÅÝMuslim Dahiyeh to leave, along with residents of a swathe of southern Lebanon and parts of the east.
Some ‚ÅÝ700,000 ‚Äãpeople have been displaced in ‚ÄãLebanon, or more than a tenth of the population, the United Nations ‚Äãhas reported.

item.title: UK police ban pro-Iranian London march due to 'extreme tensions'
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 (Reuters) - British police said they had banned a pro-Iranian march due to take place in London on Sunday, citing the possibility of "extreme tensions" with ‚Äãcounter-protesters and the risk posed by Tehran during the conflict in the ‚ÄåMiddle East.
The Al Quds march in central London is organised annually by the Islamic Human Rights Commission, which the police said was supportive of the Iranian regime.
The threshold to ban a protest ‚Äãis high in Britain, with the police saying this was the first ‚Äãtime the power had been invoked in 14 years, but the ‚ÅÝrisks of public disorder were "so severe" it was right to block it. The ban ‚Äãalso applies to any counter-protest marches.
The Islamic Human Rights Commission has previously said on ‚ÄãX that the march was being held in support of Palestinian liberation.
Police said that previous Al Quds marches have resulted in arrests for supporting terrorist organisations and anti-Semitic hate crimes, and said ‚Äãthat even with the ban in place they were facing a "challenging, potentially violent weekend".
"We ‚Äãhave taken into consideration the likely impact on protests of the volatile situation in the Middle ‚ÄåEast, ‚ÅÝwith the Iranian regime attacking British allies and military bases overseas," the police statement published late on Tuesday said.
"We must also consider that the security services have been publicly clear about the threats we are facing on UK soil from the Iranian ‚Äãregime."
British police have faced ‚Äãheavy scrutiny of ‚ÅÝtheir handling of regular, large pro-Palestinian protests in London since the October 7, 2023 attacks by Hamas on Israel, with supporters ‚Äãand opponents at odds over the right of free speech, the ‚Äãsupport shown ‚ÅÝfor a proscribed group and the impact on the Jewish community.
Last week, British police arrested four men on suspicion of helping Iran's intelligence services carry out surveillance of people and ‚ÅÝlocations ‚Äãlinked to the Jewish community in London.
Should the Islamic ‚ÄãHuman Rights Commission go ahead with a static assembly, which there is no law to ban, police said ‚Äãit would be placed under strict conditions.

item.title: Two drones fall in vicinity of Dubai airport as Iran crisis shows no sign of easing
item.description:
DUBAI, March 11 (Reuters) - Two drones came down in the vicinity of Dubai ‚ÄãInternational Airport (DXB) on Wednesday, injuring four people, Dubai's media ‚Äåoffice said, as attacks on infrastructure across the Gulf continued on the 12th day of the Iran crisis , which has caused widespread ‚Äãdisruption to global air traffic.
"Authorities confirm that two ‚Äãdrones fell in the vicinity of Dubai International ‚ÅÝAirport (DXB) a short while ago," the media office posted ‚Äãon X, adding that air traffic is operating as ‚Äãnormal.
The attack resulted in minor injuries to two Ghanaian nationals and one Bangladeshi national, as well as moderate injuries to one Indian ‚Äãnational, it added.
The outbreak of the U.S.-Israel war against ‚ÄãIran has led to flight cancellations, rescheduling and re-routing for airlines ‚Äåacross ‚ÅÝthe globe, as most airspaces in the Middle East, including Qatar, remain shut over missile and drone concerns. It has also led to an energy crisis that has ‚Äãled to ‚Äãa spike in fuel ‚ÅÝprices.
UAE airlines like Dubai's Emirates and Abu Dhabi's Etihad have resumed some flights since ‚Äãthe beginning of the crisis on February ‚Äã28 ‚ÅÝbut they are still operating below their capacity, with the attack on Wednesday marking a renewed hit to DXB, ‚ÅÝthe world's ‚Äãbusiest airport for international passengers, ‚Äãwhich last year handled nearly 100 million passengers.

item.title: Opposition candidates in Congo Republic's presidential election
item.description:
DAKAR, March 11 (Reuters) - Denis Sassou Nguesso , the 82-year-old president of Republic of Congo, is seeking another five-year term in an election on March 15, aiming to extend his more than four decades in office in ‚Äãthe oil-rich Central African nation.
A former paratrooper, Sassou seized power in 1979 and has ‚Äågoverned almost continuously since, except for a five-year hiatus in the 1990s.
He faces six opposition candidates in a race he is widely expected to win. The opposition remains fragmented, with established parties boycotting the vote and some prominent potential ‚Äãcandidates either in prison or in exile.
Following are details on his challengers: JOSEPH KIGNOUMBI KIA MBOUNGOU
Joseph ‚ÄãKignoumbi Kia Mboungou, 73, a veteran lawmaker from the southwestern Lekoumou department and leader of the political ‚ÅÝparty La Cha√Æne, is running for president for the fifth time since 2002. He earned 0.62% ‚Äãin the 2021 election.
His campaign has focused on what he describes as the need for political change ‚Äãand economic diversification, highlighting his goals of reducing Congo's heavy reliance on oil and tackling poverty.
Uphrem Dave Mafoula, 43, an economist, is making his second consecutive presidential bid after running in 2021, when he was the youngest candidate and ‚Äãsecured 0.52% of the vote.
His platform centres on governance reforms, job creation and reducing territorial inequalities.
Melaine Destin Gavet Elengo, 35, is the youngest contender in the race and a first-time presidential candidate.
An engineer ‚Äåin ‚ÅÝthe oil sector, he presents himself as part of a new generation seeking political renewal. His campaign emphasizes transparency, an independent justice system and inclusive development.
Mabio Mavoungou Zinga, 69, a retired customs inspector and former member of parliament, is running under the banner of the Alliance party, a coalition of opposition ‚Äãgroups.
He has pledged to ‚Äãtackle corruption and free ‚ÅÝjailed opposition leaders Jean-Marie Michel Mokoko and Andr√© Okombi Salissa, who were arrested after the disputed 2016 election and later sentenced to 20 years in ‚Äãprison on charges of undermining state security. This is his first presidential race.
Vivien ‚ÅÝRomain Manangou, 43, an independent candidate and public law university lecturer, is also making his first presidential bid.
His campaign has centered on institutional reforms, improving public finances and promoting national unity.
Anguios Nganguia Engambe, around 60, president ‚ÅÝof the ‚ÄãParty for Action of the Republic, is running for president ‚Äãfor the fourth consecutive time, having stood in 2009, 2016 and 2021, when he won just 0.18% of the vote.
His platform ‚Äãfocuses on bridging political divisions and increasing political participation.

item.title: South Korea Presidential aide says AI cooperation with UAE could accelerate after conflict
item.description: SEOUL, March 11 (Reuters) - South Korea's senior presidential secretary for AI, Ha Jung-woo, said on Wednesday that artificial intelligence cooperation with the ‚ÄãUnited Arab Emirates could accelerate after the Middle East ‚Äåconflict ends, given the UAE's need to strengthen its defence capabilities.

item.title: Israel believes Iran's new leader was lightly wounded in attacks, senior official says
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 11 (Reuters) - Israel's ‚Äãintelligence assessment is that Iran's ‚Äånew supreme leader, Mojtaba Khamenei , was lightly wounded in the Israeli-U.S. joint air war ‚Äãagainst Iran and that is ‚Äãwhy he has not been ‚ÅÝseen in public, a senior ‚ÄãIsraeli official told Reuters.
Iran's Revolutionary ‚ÄãGuards forced through the choice of Mojtaba Khamenei as the new supreme leader, ‚Äãseeing him as a more ‚Äãpliant version of his father who would back ‚Äåtheir ‚ÅÝhardline policies.
Khamenei's selection may add up to a more aggressive stance abroad and sterner internal ‚Äãrepression, said ‚Äãthree ‚ÅÝsenior Iranian sources, a reformist former official and ‚Äãanother insider.
Israeli Defense Minister ‚ÄãIsrael ‚ÅÝKatz said last week that any leader appointed by the current Iranian leadership ‚ÅÝwould "be ‚Äãan unequivocal target ‚Äãfor elimination."

item.title: Romanian to review US request to use local air base for Iran operations
item.description:
BUCHAREST, March 11 (Reuters) - Romanian President Nicusor Dan has convened the EU ‚Äåand NATO nation's top defence council on Wednesday to discuss whether to allow U.S. aircraft access to its military bases for support linked ‚Äãto its Tehran operations , political sources said.
The council will meet ‚Äãfor the first time this year to discuss the ‚ÅÝsecurity fallout from the conflict in the Middle East, ‚Äãits impact on Romania's energy market and "the temporary deployment of military ‚Äãcapability on Romanian territory."
That deployment, political sources said without elaborating, referred to a U.S. request to use the Mihail Kogalniceanu air base.
While some EU ‚Äãcountries--such as France, Greece and Italy-- have sent warships to Cyprus ‚Äãafter Iranian-made drones struck a British base on the island, others allow use ‚Äåof ‚ÅÝtheir military bases.
Most EU top officials have condemned Iranian strikes in the region and urged an end and diplomatic solution to the conflict.
The U.S. withdrew about ‚Äã1,000 troops from ‚ÄãRomania's Mihail ‚ÅÝKogalniceanu air base last year, as the U.S. focused on its own borders and the Indo-Pacific ‚Äãregion. Another 1,000 U.S. troops remain in ‚ÄãRomania.
The permanent ‚ÅÝallied presence in Romania stands at around 3,500 NATO troops, including U.S. soldiers.
Romania shares a 650 km (400 mile) land border with ‚ÅÝUkraine, ‚Äãover which Russian drones have flown ‚Äãtowards Kyiv, while mines in the Black Sea from the conflict impact key ‚Äãtrade and energy routes.

item.title: Russia says it shot down two British-supplied Storm Shadow missiles
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 11 (Reuters) - Russian Defence Ministry said on ‚ÄãWednesday that its forces ‚Äåshot down two British-supplied Storm Shadow missiles over ‚Äãthe past 24 ‚Äãhours, according to the ‚ÅÝRIA news agency.
Earlier ‚ÄãUkraine said it had ‚Äãused Storm Shadow missiles to strike a key plant ‚Äãproducing missile components ‚Äãin the Russian city of Bryansk ‚Äåon ‚ÅÝTuesday.
The governor of Bryansk region, Alexander Bogomaz, said that six ‚Äãcivilians were ‚Äãkilled ‚ÅÝand 42 injured in the ‚Äãattack.
Separately, the Russian ‚Äãdefence ‚ÅÝministry said its forces had captured the ‚ÅÝsettlement ‚Äãof Chervona Zoria ‚Äãin Ukraine's Sumy region.

item.title: Gasunie urges Dutch government to build strategic gas reserve
item.description:
AMSTERDAM, March 11 (Reuters) - Dutch natural gas grid operator Gasunie on Wednesday urged ‚Äãthe government to build a strategic gas ‚Äåreserve to ensure the Netherlands can withstand a prolonged disruption of supplies.
Gasunie said the Netherlands was protected against short-term ‚Äãdisruptions due to cold weather, but not for ‚Äãlong-term problems caused by, for instance, geopolitical tensions ‚ÅÝor sabotage.
"The current geopolitical climate demands realism and ‚Äãpreparations for the unexpected to happen on the ‚Äãlonger term," Gasunie director Hans Coenen said.
The gas company called for research on the use of the so-called cushion gas that ‚Äãis available in the country's large gas storages ‚Äãas a reserve.
Cushion gas is permanently stored in natural gas ‚Äåstorages ‚ÅÝto maintain adequate pressure.
The Netherlands was once one of Europe's major gas suppliers, but has turned into an importer after it shut down the large Groningen ‚Äãfield due to ‚Äãthe earthquakes ‚ÅÝthat gas extraction had caused there.
Most of the gas used in the Netherlands ‚Äãnow comes from Norway, but an ‚Äãincreasing part ‚ÅÝis supplied by the U.S. in the form of liquefied natural gas.
The country still has large underground ‚ÅÝgas ‚Äãstorages, of which about 85% of capacity ‚Äãwould be needed to meet yearly gas demand under regular circumstances, ‚ÄãGasunie said.

item.title: Dutch court scraps cap on number of flights at Amsterdam airport
item.description:
AMSTERDAM, March 11 (Reuters) - The Dutch Supreme ‚ÄãCourt on ‚ÄåWednesday scrapped a government decision ‚Äãto limit ‚Äãthe number of flights ‚ÅÝat Amsterdam's ‚ÄãSchiphol airport, ‚Äãone of Europe busiest hubs.
The court ‚Äãsaid the ‚Äã2024 decision to limit ‚Äåthe ‚ÅÝnumber of flights to 478,000 per ‚Äãyear, ‚Äãmade ‚ÅÝin an effort ‚Äãto cut ‚Äãnoise ‚ÅÝpollution, had not been ‚ÅÝproperly ‚Äãmotivated.

item.title: South Korea and Ghana to expand cooperation on climate, tech and maritime security
item.description: SEOUL, March 11 (Reuters) - South Korean President Lee Jae Myung held summit talks on ‚ÄãWednesday with Ghanaian President John ‚ÄåMahama, agreeing to deepen cooperation in climate, digital innovation and maritime security.

item.title: Exclusive: Foreign hacker in 2023 compromised Epstein files held by FBI
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 11 (Reuters) - A foreign hacker compromised files relating to the FBI‚Äôs investigation of the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein during a break-in at the bureau‚Äôs New York Field Office three years ago, according to ‚Äãa source familiar with the matter and recently published Justice Department documents reviewed by Reuters.
The details of who accessed a server at the FBI‚Äôs New York Field Office, ‚Äåincluding the allegation that a foreign hacker was involved, are being reported here for the first time.
In a statement, the FBI said what it described as a "cyber incident" was "an isolated one."
"The FBI restricted access to the malicious actor and rectified the network. The investigation remains ongoing, so we do not have further comments to provide at this time."
Although the source said the intrusion appeared to have been carried out by a cybercriminal rather than a foreign government, ‚Äãthe incident underscores the files' potential intelligence value, one academic said. The legally mandated publication of U.S. Justice Department documents has exposed the dead financier's ties to prominent people in politics, finance, academia and business, ‚Äãtriggering investigations in numerous countries around the world.
‚ÄúWho wouldn‚Äôt be going after the Epstein files if you‚Äôre the Russians or somebody interested in kompromat?‚Äù said ‚ÅÝJon Lindsay, who researches the role of emerging technology in global security at the Georgia Institute of Technology. ‚ÄúIf foreign intelligence agencies are not thinking seriously about the Epstein files as a target, then ‚ÄãI would be shocked.‚Äù
The breach was reported contemporaneously by CNN and Reuters on February 17; the connection to Epstein materials was made by the French magazine Marianne.
Epstein, a longtime associate of President Donald Trump, ‚Äãpleaded guilty in 2008 to prostitution charges, including soliciting an underage girl. He was found hanged in his jail cell in 2019, in what was ruled a suicide, after being arrested again on federal charges of sex trafficking of minors.
The hack occurred after a server at the Child Exploitation Forensic Lab in the FBI‚Äôs New York Field Office was inadvertently left vulnerable by Special Agent Aaron Spivack, who was trying to navigate the bureau‚Äôs complex ‚Äãprocedures for handling digital evidence, according to the source and the documents.
A timeline written by Spivack and included in the large cache of Epstein documents released earlier this year said the break-in happened ‚Äãon February 12, 2023. It was discovered the following day when Spivack turned on his computer and discovered a text file warning him that his network had been compromised, according to that document.
Further investigation turned up traces ‚Äåof unusual activity ‚ÅÝon the server, the document said, adding that the activity "included combing through certain files pertaining to the Epstein investigation.‚Äù
The timeline does not say which specific files were accessed, whether the hacker downloaded the data, or who the hacker was. Reuters could not establish what, if any, overlap the affected data had with the Epstein documents published earlier this year or the files that remain under wraps.
Spivack, whose name appears elsewhere , opens new tab in the documents , opens new tab in connection with the Epstein investigation, did not return repeated messages seeking comment. Reuters was unable to reach the man identified in the documents as Spivack‚Äôs lawyer, Richard J. Roberson, Jr. Seven FBI ‚Äãagents identified in the documents as being involved ‚Äãin the investigation into the incident did ‚ÅÝnot return messages.
In his statement to FBI investigators examining whether he was responsible for the breach, Spivack said he was being made "a scapegoat for the intrusion" and that conflicting bureau policies and faulty guidance around information technology were to blame. Reuters could not establish the result ‚Äãof the bureau's internal investigation.
The person familiar with the breach said the intrusion was carried out by a foreign hacker who did not appear ‚Äãto realize they had penetrated ‚ÅÝa law enforcement server. The hacker expressed disgust at the presence of child abuse images on the device and left a message threatening to turn its owner over to the FBI, the person said.
The source said bureau officials defused the situation by convincing the hacker that they actually were the FBI, in part by having the hacker join a video chat where they flashed their law enforcement credentials in front ‚ÅÝof a web ‚Äãcamera.
Reuters could not determine - and the source said they did not know - who the hacker was, what country they ‚Äãwere operating from, what they did with the material accessed, or whether any effort was made to identify or punish them for breaking into the FBI‚Äôs server.
Many of the Justice Department's documents have been heavily redacted and others have been kept ‚Äãsecret altogether despite a law mandating their full release last year . The Trump administration says it is withholding material that could compromise victims‚Äô identities or jeopardize ongoing investigations.

item.title: As Iran war rages, Trump pivots to cost of living in Kentucky trip
item.description:
HEBRON, Kentucky, March 11 (Reuters) - Twelve days into the Iran war, U.S. President Donald Trump is refocusing on domestic issues, traveling ‚Äãto Kentucky on Wednesday to promote his economic plans as Republicans urge him to address rising concern over the ‚Äåcost of living.
The president's campaign trip is his first since the start of the U.S.-Israeli military operation in Iran . It offers Trump a chance to sharpen his economic message ahead of the November midterm elections, when Republicans will be defending narrow majorities in both chambers of the U.S. Congress.
Trump is due to ‚Äãdeliver remarks at two events in northern Kentucky and nearby Cincinnati, Ohio. White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt could not ‚Äãsay why Trump had chosen to go to those places in particular, but the Kentucky district ‚ÅÝis also home to Republican U.S. Representative Thomas Massie, a Trump critic who has frequently broken with his party.
Massie led the high-profile ‚Äãpush for more transparency from Trump's Justice Department's handling of files connected to the late convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.
Trump has endorsed the ‚ÄãRepublican candidate who will face off against Massie in the party's primary in May.
In his public remarks, Trump is expected to focus on his efforts to lower some prescription drug costs, a key selling point for Republicans trying to address voters' economic concerns.
It is the latest in a series of speeches Trump ‚Äãhas delivered around the country to sell the American public on his economic policies, but they have often been overshadowed by his ‚Äãpolicies abroad, most recently the war in Iran, which has caused gas prices to soar.
Gas prices are up 61 cents on average in both Kentucky ‚Äåand nationwide ‚ÅÝcompared to last month, according to travel organization AAA.
"Short term oil prices, which will drop rapidly when the destruction of the Iran nuclear threat is over, is a very small price to pay," Trump posted earlier this week, "ONLY FOOLS WOULD THINK DIFFERENTLY!"
Bill Kunkel, a 67-year-old retired welder in Union, Kentucky, told Reuters he is concerned about how the Iran war and Trump's tariff program will continue to affect an ‚Äãarea known for logistics and ‚Äãmanufacturing.
"I voted for Trump because ‚ÅÝhe was going to get us out of all of these wars. That is one of the reasons why I agree with Massie - it can't just be a forever war," Kunkel added, referring to ‚ÄãMassie's criticism of Trump's decision to go to war against Iran.
Massie said in an interview that ‚Äãhe welcomes Trump ‚ÅÝto his district as they share a lot of the same supporters.
‚ÄúI‚Äôve taken care to never insult the president, to never call him names. If I have a policy disagreement with him, I articulate it and keep it to that," he said.
Trump has endorsed Ed Gallrein, a ‚ÅÝformer Navy ‚ÄãSEAL officer and farmer Trump calls "high-quality," to take on Massie, a man Trump ‚Äãcalls "unreliable." Gallrein‚Äôs campaign said he will attend the president‚Äôs event.
"There is growing dissatisfaction with where Massie is, and part of it is not supporting the president," said ‚ÄãLarry Mazzuckelli, a retired federal government employee and political commentator from Union, Kentucky.

item.title: Kremlin says Britain was involved in Ukraine's missile strike on Russia
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 11 (Reuters) - The Kremlin on Wednesday accused British specialists of involvement in a deadly ‚ÄãUkrainian strike on the Russian city of Bryansk that ‚Äåused British Storm Shadow missiles and said it would take Britain's role "into account".
The Bryansk regional governor said Tuesday's strike killed at least six civilians and injured ‚Äã37 in what he called a "terrorist missile attack", without saying ‚Äãwhat was hit. Ukraine said it had struck a ‚ÅÝkey plant producing missile components. Moscow accused it of deliberately targeting ‚Äãcivilians.
Asked by Reuters if there would be a military response to ‚Äãthe use of British missiles against sovereign Russian territory, Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov said Russia would take into account the British involvement.
"It is obvious that the launch ‚Äãof these missiles was impossible without British specialists," Peskov told ‚Äãreporters. "We are aware of this, we know it well, and we naturally take it ‚Äåinto ‚ÅÝaccount."
"In order to prevent such barbaric actions by the Kyiv regime from continuing, the special military operation is being conducted," he said, adding that one of the goals of the operation was to demilitarise ‚ÄãUkraine.
There was no ‚Äãimmediate comment ‚ÅÝfrom London, though Russia has repeatedly said that Ukraine needs Western expertise and Western-supplied targeting data and ‚Äãsatellite imagery to fire advanced missiles deep into Russia.
Ukrainian ‚ÄãPresident ‚ÅÝVolodymyr Zelenskiy said that Kyiv had struck one of the most important military factories in Bryansk that produced electronics for Russian missiles.
Russian Foreign Ministry ‚ÅÝspokeswoman ‚ÄãMaria Zakharova said the strike was premeditated ‚Äãand directed against civilians, and challenged the U.N. to assess what had occurred.

item.title: China 'does not agree' with attacks on Gulf countries, says foreign ministry
item.description: BEIJING, March 11 (Reuters) - China "does ‚Äãnot agree" ‚Äåwith attacks on ‚ÄãGulf states ‚Äãand condemns all ‚ÅÝindiscriminate ‚Äãattacks on ‚Äãcivilians and non-military targets, ‚Äãsaid ‚ÄãGuo Jiakun, spokesperson ‚Äåat ‚ÅÝthe Chinese foreign ministry, at ‚Äãa ‚Äãregular ‚ÅÝpress conference ‚Äãon ‚ÄãWednesday.

item.title: Thailand says 3 crew missing after ship hit by projectiles in Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
BANGKOK, March 11 (Reuters) - Twenty crew have been rescued ‚Äãfrom a Thai ‚Äåship hit by unknown projectiles in the ‚ÄãStrait of Hormuz ‚Äãbut three crew remain ‚ÅÝmissing, Thailand's Transport ‚ÄãMinistry said on Wednesday.
The ‚Äãministry said the crew had abandoned the vessel ‚Äãin a lifeboat ‚Äãand were rescued by the ‚ÄåOmani ‚ÅÝnavy. It said an explosion occurred in the stern of ‚Äãthe ‚Äãship ‚ÅÝand caused a fire in the ‚Äãengine compartment, ‚Äãwhere ‚ÅÝthe remaining three crew members had been ‚ÅÝworking.

item.title: Pope Leo laments death of civilians, children in Iran war
item.description:
VATICAN CITY, March 11 (Reuters) - Pope Leo on Wednesday ‚Äãlamented the death ‚Äåof numerous civilians in the ongoing Iran war ‚Äãand also expressed ‚Äãcloseness to people in ‚ÅÝLebanon, saying the ‚Äãcountry, targeted by Israeli ‚Äãstrikes, was going through a "great trial."
Leo, the first ‚ÄãU.S. pope, called ‚Äãon pilgrims in his weekly ‚Äåaudience ‚ÅÝin St. Peter's Square to pray for peace.
"Let us continue ‚Äãto pray ‚Äãfor ‚ÅÝpeace in Iran, and throughout ‚Äãthe Middle East, ‚Äãespecially ‚ÅÝfor the many civilian victims, including many ‚ÅÝinnocent ‚Äãchildren," said ‚Äãthe pontiff.

item.title: Thailand's Phuket airport closes runway after Air India Express plane malfunction
item.description:
BANGKOK, March 11 (Reuters) - Thailand's Phuket airport said on Wednesday it had temporarily closed its runway to remove a disabled Air India Express plane ‚Äãafter it experienced a malfunction upon landing.
Air India Express Flight AXB938 ‚Äåarriving from Hyderabad suffered damage to its landing gear as it hit the runway, Phuket airport said in a statement.
The plane "experienced an issue with the nose wheel at ‚ÄãPhuket Airport," a spokesperson for Air India Express said, adding: "The ‚Äãcrew followed all standard protocols, and guests were deplaned.
There were no ‚ÅÝcasualties and the runway was expected to reopen at 6 p.m. ‚Äãlocal time (1100 GMT), the airport said.
An image posted on X by Thai state ‚Äãbroadcaster MCOT showed part of the aircraft landing gear, with a wheel missing, in direct contact with the runway, with a long, swerving skid mark on the tarmac.
Air India Express, the ‚Äãbudget arm of Air India, has a fleet of over 100 Boeing ‚Äãand Airbus aircraft.
India's aviation watchdog reprimanded Air India Express in March last year for ‚Äånot ‚ÅÝchanging the engine parts of an Airbus A320, as directed by the European Union's aviation safety agency, Reuters has reported . It was also criticised for falsifying records to show compliance.
Air India Express reported 95 technical defects from January to November last ‚Äãyear, India's civil ‚Äãaviation ministry said ‚ÅÝin December, the second highest among India's airlines over the period. The ministry did not provide details on the ‚Äãnature of the faults.
Parent Air India, a full-service carrier, ‚Äãhas come ‚ÅÝunder intense scrutiny in the last few months after a series of safety lapses and a deadly crash in in Ahmedabad last June that killed ‚ÅÝ260 ‚Äãpeople.
Phuket is one of Thailand's busiest gateways, with ‚Äãhundreds of flights daily, including from the Middle East, Europe and Asia, as well as ‚Äãdomestic routes.

item.title: Kremlin says it is in constant touch with Iranian leaders
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 11 (Reuters) - Russia is constantly in touch ‚Äãwith the Iranian leadership and ‚Äåwilling to contribute to efforts to stabilise the region, ‚Äãthe Kremlin said on ‚ÄãWednesday.
"Here I can only say ‚ÅÝthat we are in ‚Äãconstant contact with the Iranian ‚Äãside and with the Iranian leadership," Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov ‚Äãtold reporters.
"As President (Vladimir) Putin ‚Äãhas said, Russia is always ready ‚Äåto ‚ÅÝdo what it can to restore peace and stability in the region."
Putin and ‚ÄãForeign Minister ‚ÄãSergei ‚ÅÝLavrov have spoken with their Iranian counterparts ‚Äãin recent days, ‚Äãwhile ‚ÅÝPutin also held a phone call with U.S. President ‚ÅÝDonald ‚ÄãTrump on Monday.

item.title: Hungary's opposition Tisza party maintains lead as election nears, poll shows
item.description:
BUDAPEST, March 11 (Reuters) - The main Hungarian opposition party Tisza's lead over long-time Prime Minister Viktor Orban's Fidesz shrank slightly in a poll published on ‚ÄãWednesday, as campaigning enters its final month before an April ‚Äå12 election.
Nationalist Orban faces the biggest challenge to his rule in 16 years, although the outcome of the vote - coming against the backdrop of the Ukraine-Russia war ‚Äãand building economic challenges - remains highly uncertain, with opinion polls showing ‚Äãthat many voters are still undecided.
A poll by 21 Research ‚ÅÝCentre (21 Kutatokozpont), conducted between March 2 and 6 and released on ‚ÄãWednesday, showed Tisza leads by 14 percentage points ahead of Fidesz among ‚Äãdecided voters, down from a 16-point lead in the agency's last poll in January.
Centre-right Tisza, led by former government insider Peter Magyar , had the support of 53% of decided ‚Äãvoters, unchanged from January, according to 21 Research Centre, while 39% backed ‚ÄãFidesz, up from 37% in the previous poll.
The poll, published by news site 24.hu, ‚Äåshowed ‚ÅÝ38% support for Tisza among all voters, with Fidesz backed by 30%.
Based on the poll, Tisza could win 115 seats in Hungary's 199-seat parliament, and Fidesz could expect 78 seats. The far-right Our Homeland (Mi Hazank) party ‚Äãwould be the ‚Äãonly other party ‚ÅÝcrossing the 5% threshold to win seats.
Magyar has said his party would curb corruption, unlock billions of euros ‚Äãin frozen European Union funds to boost the economy, ‚Äãand firmly ‚ÅÝanchor Hungary in the EU and NATO.
Fidesz has pointed to other surveys that still show it on course to victory, though opponents - who criticise Orban ‚ÅÝfor ‚Äãharming the rule of law and keeping ‚Äãwarm ties with Russia - say these have mainly been conducted by institutes with financial or personal ‚Äãties to the ruling party.

item.title: Sweden's Stegra seeks to raise over $2.3 billion, daily DI reports
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, March 11 (Reuters) - Swedish startup Stegra must raise more than 2 billion euros ($2.3 billion) to ‚Äãcomplete construction of its green steel plant, ‚Äåmore than double its previous estimate, business daily Dagens Industri reported on Wednesday, citing unnamed sources.
The ‚Äãcompany has appointed a new CFO and ‚Äãshaken up its board , as it scrambles to ‚ÅÝraise more cash to finish its hydrogen-based ‚Äãsteel plant in northern Sweden.
($1 = 0.8602 euros)

item.title: US ignites Iran war, but Gulf Arab states pay the price, Gulf sources say
item.description:
DUBAI, March 11 (Reuters) - The U.S. may have pulled the trigger on the Iran war , but it is the oil-producing Gulf that will pay the price, Gulf sources and analysts say, signalling unease in ties between a region under Iranian attack and the superpower it relies on for protection.
Behind the scenes, resentment is mounting ‚Äãin Gulf Arab capitals at being drawn into a war they neither initiated nor endorsed but are now paying for economically and militarily, with airports, hotels, ports and military and oil installations hit by Iranian strikes, said three ‚Äåregional sources, who declined to be identified as they were not authorised to speak publicly.
"It is not our war. We did not want this conflict, yet we are paying the price in our security and our economy," Ebtesam Al-Ketbi, President of the Emirates Policy Center, told Reuters.
That doesn't mean Iran is "innocent", she said. Gulf governments had assured Tehran they would not allow their territories or airspace to be used by Washington in the war. Yet Iran has unleashed waves of drone and missile strikes across the region despite those assurances, denting business confidence in the process.
While ‚Äãdisquiet about U.S. President Donald Trump is growing over a conflict many believe he launched without consultation, some regional sources argue that having started the war, Washington should now see it through to eliminate what they see as a persistent ‚ÄãIranian threat on their doorstep.
‚ÄúIf America leaves the war now without achieving victory, it will be like abandoning an injured lion,‚Äù Ketbi said. ‚ÄúIran will remain a threat to the region, capable of ‚ÅÝstriking again. And if the regime collapses, leaving a power vacuum, neighbouring states will suffer the consequences.‚Äù
Asked for comment, the White House said U.S. and Israeli strikes had reduced Iran‚Äôs retaliatory missile attacks by 90 percent, "crushing their ability to shoot these weapons or ‚Äãproduce more".
White House spokeswoman Anna Kelly added that Trump was in close contact with Middle East partners and that Iran‚Äôs attacks on its neighbors underscore why the threat had to be eliminated.
There was no immediate response from Gulf states to requests for comment.
Iran's Supreme ‚ÄãLeader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, was killed on the first day of the war. Tehran responded by hitting Israel and Gulf states hosting U.S. military installations, effectively halting oil and gas shipments through the Strait of Hormuz -- a conduit for roughly a fifth of the world‚Äôs petroleum and LNG.
Across the region, airspace closures have resulted in airlines cancelling some 40,000 flights, the largest disruption to global air travel since the COVID-19 pandemic. Gulf tourism meanwhile is also taking a hit , putting at risk the region's carefully crafted image as a safe and high-end vacation hotspot.
Amid the turmoil, Gulf states have ‚Äãsought to project calm and resolve. The president of the United Arab Emirates, Sheikh Mohammed bin Zayed Al Nahyan, said on Friday his nation was in a time of war but was well and told his enemies it was no easy prey , in his ‚Äãfirst public comments since Iran fired missiles at the UAE.
At the same time, analysts say the war has left Gulf states reassessing both their security dependence on Washington and the prospect of eventually engaging Tehran on new regional security arrangements -- even as trust in Iran has collapsed.
For decades, ‚Äårelations between Washington ‚ÅÝand the Gulf states rested on an implicit trade-off: Gulf energy and capital -- including hundreds of billions of dollars spent on U.S. arms, advanced technology and goods and services -- in return for U.S. protection, said Fawaz Gerges of the London School of Economics.
Gerges said the war had shaken those assumptions. Now, he said, Gulf states would accelerate efforts to diversify their foreign and security partnerships, realising "they cannot really rely on the United States to protect their energy, oil, gas, their people and their sovereignty.‚Äù
The Gulf's latest misgivings about Washington echo disquiet felt in Saudi Arabia at the U.S. failure to retaliate against Iran after drone and missile attack in 2019 on the kingdom's Abqaiq and Khurais oil facilities. Washington and Riyadh blamed the attacks on Tehran, which denied responsibility.
Abdulaziz Sager, Chairman of the Saudi-based ‚ÄãGulf Research Center, said Washington had failed to prepare safeguards ‚Äãfor its regional allies or ensure oil and gas flows ‚ÅÝduring wartime, saying the cost on the Gulf states' economies is "horrendous".
Sager said the war had demonstrated the limits of relying solely on external security guarantees, particularly from the United States, adding that Gulf countries need to strengthen their own defense capabilities and prepare for future crises.
"External powers also tend to make decisions based on their own strategic interests rather than ‚Äãthose of the Gulf. As a result, Gulf states will likely pursue a more cautious and balanced approach in their relations with both Iran and major international partners," added Sager.
In a ‚Äãrare public rebuke, prominent Emirati businessman ‚ÅÝKhalaf Al Habtoor questioned the objectives of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.
‚ÄúIf the strikes were aimed at containing Iran, did they take into account the regional consequences -- or was the cost of dragging the Gulf into a conflict it was not party to simply ignored?‚Äù said Al Habtoor, founder of the Al Habtoor Group.
Saudi Aramco CEO Amin Nasser warned on Tuesday that continued disruption to shipping through Hormuz would have ‚Äúcatastrophic consequences‚Äù for oil markets .
Iran‚Äôs Revolutionary Guards, however, said they would not allow ‚Äúone litre of oil‚Äù to be shipped from the Middle East if U.S. ‚ÅÝand Israeli attacks ‚Äãcontinued. Trump has warned Washington would strike Iran harder if it blocked oil exports.
Sources close to Gulf Arab government circles say frustration with Trump privately runs ‚Äãdeep across the region. Many believe he dragged the Gulf into a war shaped heavily by Israel, without sharing a plan and acting hastily and without fully weighing the political and economic fallout for allies.
One Gulf source familiar with U.S. policymaking said key decisions were crafted by a small inner circle around Trump operating ‚Äãlargely outside traditional U.S. policy channels.
‚ÄúThey are businessmen and dealmakers, not career policymakers,‚Äù the source said, adding this approach left Gulf partners exposed to the consequences of this circle's decisions.

item.title: Meloni bets on a lottery to curb Italy's judicial clans
item.description:
ROME, March 11 (Reuters) - Italy has tried random selection before.
Ancient Romans used to cast lots to decide who governed in distant provinces while Renaissance Florence pulled names from bags to avoid powerful families gaining a stranglehold on power.
Now Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni is looking to revive the centuries-old ‚Äãtradition of "sortition" as a way to stop what she says are political factions lording it over Italy's long-troubled judiciary.
The plan to select magistrate members of judicial governing councils by lottery ‚Äålies at the heart of a reform being put to referendum on March 22-23, pitting Meloni's conservative government against most of the nation's judges and prosecutors.
Recent opinion polls show the "no" camp, backed by the main opposition centre-left parties, has nudged ahead, threatening one of Meloni's signature initiatives which was championed by her predecessor Silvio Berlusconi before his death in 2023.
Berlusconi, who faced dozens of trials largely tied to his media empire after entering politics, accused the judiciary of being in the ‚Äãhands of the left. Magistrates denied this, but Meloni vowed to take up his crusade after she won power in 2022.
"I think change is needed because justice is one of the ‚Äãthree fundamental powers that keep Italy afloat, but it's also the one that we haven't managed to substantially reform since the 1980s," Meloni told RTL ‚ÅÝradio last week.
"If we don't succeed this time, we won't have another chance," she added, saying future governments would be unlikely to take on this sort of constitutional reform that almost always requires ‚Äãa referendum that is hard to win in a divided country.
The referendum campaign is unfolding amid growing distrust between the judiciary and the ruling coalition, with "no" supporters arguing that Meloni's plan would undermine the justice ‚Äãsystem and boost political interference.
They seized on remarks by Giusi Bartolozzi, the justice ministry's chief of staff, who urged voters in a TV interview this week to back the reform, saying it would allow Italians to "get rid of a judiciary that acts like a firing squad."
The government's reform has three main pillars.
First, it would separate the career paths of judges and public prosecutors, who currently enter through the same exam, can swap ‚Äãjob roles and are governed by a single council (the CSM), which oversees appointments and promotions.
Second, it would split the CSM into two councils - one for judges and one for prosecutors. Third, it would create ‚Äãa new disciplinary court to handle misconduct cases.
Under the proposal for the new-look CSMs, two thirds of seats would be reserved for working judges or prosecutors, and one third for lay members, such as law professors or ‚Äålawyers, whose ‚ÅÝnames would be put forward by parliament.
All CSM members will ultimately be drawn by lot.
Meloni says this will prevent the sort of wheeler-dealing between various internal factions that recent scandals have revealed, and create a system in which council members do not have to "say thank you to anyone" after an election.
Critics counter that in institutions designed to safeguard independence, legitimacy comes from having leaders chosen on purpose, not picked at random.
"It is one thing to punish individuals who make mistakes; it is another to humiliate the entire judiciary by saying that you are not capable of electing your own representatives," said David Ermini, a ‚Äãformer vice president of the CSM and an ‚Äãex-lawmaker with the centre-left Democratic Party.
No one looms over this debate quite like Luca Palamara, a former CSM member whose spectacular downfall spurred the reform.
Wiretaps in 2019 exposed late-night meetings involving Palamara, senior judicial figures and politicians, with discussions focused on steering appointments ‚Äî especially to coveted and sensitive prosecutor posts.
Careers were clearly being fixed by cliques ‚Äãand Palamara was expelled from the magistracy.
Now, in a twist that infuriates many of his former colleagues, Palamara is campaigning for a "Yes" vote.
"The introduction ‚Äãof random selection would dismantle ‚ÅÝthe possibility of pre-selecting who sits on the CSM, thereby eliminating factional ties," he said, calling the reform "the one most feared" by those within the factions.
Some opponents say the proposed new selection rules risk promoting people who are ill-prepared or under-qualified, sweeping away any notion of merit and leaving everything up to chance. But Palamara said these concerns were overblown.
"We are talking about people who have passed a highly competitive public examination ‚ÅÝto enter the ‚Äãprofession and who make extremely serious decisions every day - life sentences, delicate family situations," he said.
But even if the reform ‚Äãpasses the referendum hurdle, history suggests that sortition may not achieve the desired result.
In both ancient Rome and Renaissance Florence, lotteries promised fair rotation in theory, but in practice elites learned to game the system, steering outcomes so that the biggest ‚Äãprizes still tended to land in familiar hands.
"The mechanism of factionism would not end with drawing lots. Everyone selected might all come from the same faction," said Ermini.

item.title: Ukrainian trainers will help German army get ready to defend against Russia by 2029, chief says
item.description:
BERLIN, March 11 (Reuters) - Ukrainian military trainers will help ‚ÄãGermany's military meet a target of being ready to defend against any Russian attack on ‚ÄåNATO by 2029, the German army chief said, in a role reversal after years of Western troops training Ukrainian forces.
Berlin and Kyiv agreed last month that Ukraine would send military instructors to German army schools to teach lessons they ‚Äãhave learned from fighting against Russia's invasion.
"We have high expectations," Lieutenant General Christian Freuding, the head ‚Äãof the German army, told Reuters in an interview. "The Ukrainian military is currently ‚ÅÝthe only one in the world with frontline experience against Russia."
Germany is not the only Western country ‚Äãdrawing on Kyiv's experience: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy on Thursday pledged to provide assistance to the U.S. in ‚Äãdealing with Iranian drones in the Middle East.
Freuding said Germany was the first country to reach an arrangement with Ukraine to provide military instructors but predicted others would follow suit.
Asked what the Ukrainian trainers would contribute, he referred to assessments ‚Äãby German and other Western intelligence agencies that Russia could be ready for a large-scale attack on ‚Äãthe NATO military alliance by 2029.
"That's almost the day after tomorrow. We have no time - the enemy doesn't wait for ‚Äåus ‚ÅÝto declare we're ready. So we have to use every possibility to prepare," Freuding said.
Russia has insisted it does not intend to attack NATO, but Western officials say such assurances cannot be taken at face value as Moscow made similar statements before invading Ukraine.
Freuding, who became army chief ‚Äãlast October after previously coordinating ‚ÄãGermany's arms supplies to ‚ÅÝKyiv , said the initial contingent of Ukrainian trainers would likely number in the "middle double-digits" and stay for a few weeks at a time.
The Ukrainian instructors are ‚Äãexpected to contribute expertise in artillery, engineering, armoured operations, drone usage and command ‚Äãand control.
Since ‚ÅÝRussia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022, Germany has trained Ukrainian forces on weapons such as Marder infantry fighting vehicles, Leopard tanks, howitzers and air defence systems.
Freuding said Ukraine had built on these skills in combat, developing ‚ÅÝtactics ‚Äãand capabilities in data-centric warfare - the military term for highly ‚Äãdigitalised and interconnected operations - that cannot be found in textbooks.
"The fact that they are now coming to us as instructors reflects a ‚Äãsecurity partnership on an equal footing," Freuding said.

item.title: Italy‚Äôs Meloni criticises US war on Iran as part of dangerous trend
item.description:
ROME, March 11 (Reuters) - Italy's Prime Minister ‚ÄåGiorgia Meloni on Wednesday delivered her strongest criticism yet of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, describing it as part of a growing and dangerous trend of interventions "outside the scope of international law".
Her ‚Äãremarks to parliament came after repeated accusations from the opposition that her ‚Äãright-wing government had been too soft toward its allies. Most other ‚ÅÝEuropean nations, with the notable exception of Spain , have withheld direct critique of the U.S. ‚Äãand Israeli attacks, largely calling for restraint.
Meloni - who has close ties with U.S. President ‚ÄãDonald Trump - also said Iran must not be allowed to develop nuclear weapons, as that would end the international non-proliferation framework with "dramatic repercussions for global security", leaving Italy and Europe exposed to a ‚Äãpotential nuclear threat from Tehran.
The U.S. and Israel traded air strikes with Iran ‚Äãacross the Middle East as the war stretched into its 12th day, a conflict that has halted ‚Äåthe ‚ÅÝflow of one-fifth of the world's oil and gas supplies.
Addressing parliament on the crisis, Meloni drew parallels between the Middle East conflict and Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine, which she said triggered a broader global destabilisation.
"It is in this context of ‚Äãstructural crisis in the ‚Äãinternational system, in ‚ÅÝwhich threats are becoming increasingly frightening and unilateral interventions outside the scope of international law are multiplying, that we must also ‚Äãplace the American and Israeli intervention against the Iranian regime," she ‚Äãtold the ‚ÅÝSenate.
Meloni said Rome was providing air-defense assets to Gulf countries hit by strikes from Tehran.
"This is not only because these are friendly nations and strategic partners of Italy, but also ‚ÅÝbecause ‚Äãthere are tens of thousands of Italian citizens in ‚Äãthat area whom we must protect, not to mention the fact that there are about 2,000 Italian ‚Äãsoldiers stationed in the Gulf."

item.title: US navy aircraft transits Taiwan Strait ahead of Trump's planned China visit
item.description:
TAIPEI, March 11 (Reuters) - A U.S. Navy P-8A Poseidon aircraft transited the Taiwan Strait in international ‚Äãairspace on Wednesday, in a move the ‚ÄåU.S. 7th Fleet said "demonstrates the United States' commitment to a free and open Indo-Pacific."
The transit comes ahead of ‚ÄãU.S. President Donald Trump's planned visit to China ‚Äãthis month as Washington and Beijing look ‚ÅÝto stabilise ties marred by issues including Taiwan, ‚Äãtrade disputes and technology curbs.
"By operating within the ‚ÄãTaiwan Strait in accordance with international law, the United States upholds the navigational rights and freedoms of all nations," the ‚Äã7th Fleet said in a statement.
U.S. military ‚Äãships or aircraft routinely pass through or above the waterway ‚Äåthat ‚ÅÝseparates democratically governed Taiwan from China - missions that always anger Beijing.
China claims democratically governed Taiwan as its own territory and says it has jurisdiction over ‚Äãthe strait. ‚ÄãTaiwan and ‚ÅÝthe U.S. dispute that, saying the strait is an international waterway.
China has repeatedly ‚Äãsaid the Taiwan issue is its ‚Äãinternal affair ‚ÅÝand at the core of Beijing's interests - a red line that must not be crossed or ‚ÅÝtrampled ‚Äãon.
Taiwan's government rejects China's sovereignty claims, ‚Äãsaying that only Taiwan's people can decide their future.

item.title: A 'corporate welfare' claim vs Walmart and Amazon over worker benefits
item.description:
March 11 (Reuters) - (The opinions expressed here are those of the author, a correspondent for Reuters. This column is part of the Reuters Sustainable Finance Newsletter, which you can sign up for here )
Providing low-paid workers with government benefits like health insurance and food assistance is usually considered to be socially minded policy. But another way to look at the payments is as subsidies giving big businesses access to a large pool of labor able to work for less-than-living wages.
That's the critique of Sarah Anderson, Global Economy ‚ÄãProject Director at the Institute for Policy Studies, a progressive-leaning think tank in Washington, D.C. In a new report , opens new tab she reviewed pay practices of companies she dubbed "The Low-Wage 20," or the 20 largest employers of low-wage U.S. ‚Äåworkers.
Among her top findings: more than half of these firms reported 2024 median pay that would make a family of three eligible for food benefits under the federal Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, also known as SNAP, and for government-subsidized Medicaid health insurance benefits in most states. All the while, most of their CEOs earned hundreds of times as much per year.
The following transcript of our conversation has been edited for length and clarity. At the end I've published responses from the two top U.S. employers, Walmart and Amazon. (AMZN.O) , opens new tab
Question: Sarah, could you talk about how you were able to develop the calculations?
Answer: I have worked on executive compensation issues for a long, ‚Äãlong time. And this is a report that really connects those high CEO pay levels to low-wage worker issues. These are all companies that are in the S&P 500 and they're the ones that have the lowest median pay levels ‚Äãthat they've reported to the SEC (U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission) and that also have a majority of their workers in the United States.
Q: You found a lot of these employees were eligible for things ‚ÅÝlike Medicaid and SNAP food assistance, which are social benefits to lower-wage Americans.
A: We think that median pay number that the companies report themselves is a really good indicator of how many of their workers might be on Medicaid and SNAP. We don't have ‚Äãthe exact figures because they're not required to report that, but there were a handful of state governments that have revealed specific data company by company, and Walmart and Amazon were at the top of all of those lists with really clear numbers in terms ‚Äãof how many of their employees are.
These benefits are at risk right now. So it's a good time to be having this conversation because these workers who are already having to stretch to pay their bills are going to be facing even more hardship.
Q: If I were an executive for one of these companies, I'm sure I would respond by saying, well, a lot of these are part-time workers who don't want to be working full time.
