And, the calculations for public assistance are based on household size. Maybe it's not realistic to expect a head of household, a three-person household, to survive ‚Äãon just one income in this day and age.
A: I would say that having a business model that is heavily reliant on part-time workers is a choice. It's a way to get around having to pay full benefits at a lot of these companies under ‚Äãthe Affordable Care Act. Large companies have to provide affordable health insurance, or at least offer it to people working at least 30 hours a week. So if you can keep your workers, you know, at a lower hourly level, then you can get around that. I (also) question ‚Äåwhether people are ‚ÅÝworking part time because they want to work part time. A lot of people are having to work several part-time jobs to try to make ends meet.
You know, a huge share of the low-wage workforce are single parents. And for these companies to just abdicate their obligation to provide a living wage says a lot about their priorities.
Question: Surely mom-and-pop businesses face the same challenge. A lot of them can't pay their workers enough to get them above the thresholds for public assistance as well. What's the difference?
A: These should be the leading among the leading corporations of America. They're in the S&P 500. They set the standards for wages and other things. The other thing that just makes it so outrageous to so many people is to see the CEO pay levels at these companies. There's ‚Äãno owner of a small mom and pop that is making ‚Äãthe $18 million a year, which is the average (for CEOs) ‚ÅÝin our low-wage 20. It's a business model that is based on really extracting from the people often who are the frontline workers.
Q: Something that jumped out at me, among the large employers in Nevada (a state cited in the study) is that a couple of public sector organizations, a school district and a county that have a lot of their employees eligible for public assistance.
A: That's true. I have to ‚Äãsay I was dismayed to see the U.S. Postal Service was on some of these lists as well. It just gets to my broader view on this that having these extreme ‚Äãgaps between CEO pay and worker ‚ÅÝpay, where people don't feel like they're part of a team, it just doesn't bring out the best in people and that does have real costs in terms of lower employee morale and higher turnover rates.
Here is what a Walmart spokesman said about the study - "We offer a ladder of opportunity so people can build a career at Walmart, which includes a small percentage of our workforce that comes to us on public assistance. We hire them, train them and give them the chance to build a better life."
Here is what Amazon spokesperson Eileen Hards told me: ‚ÄúAmazon pay ‚ÅÝis among the ‚Äãbest in the industry ‚Äì well over double the federal minimum wage and significantly more than other retailers. Pointing fingers at Amazon over SNAP and/or Medicaid is a ‚Äãred herring when eligibility is based on total household income and size ‚Äì and not individual wages."
"For example, two employees who work at the same site and earn identical pay in the same state can have completely different SNAP or Medicaid eligibility depending on whether they support children, elderly parents, or are the sole earner in ‚Äãtheir household. As we‚Äôve said for years, what really needs to happen is a significant and large increase in the federal minimum wage‚Äîthat would be a big boost for American families.‚Äù

item.title: Morning Bid: From panic to patience
item.description:
March 11 - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-At-Large, Finance and Markets
Both energy and stock markets appear to be in a holding pattern after the wild swings earlier this week as they look for any evidence that oil and gas supplies might ‚Äãbe freed up in the Middle East.
For now, there are few signs on the ground to back up President Trump‚Äôs view that the war will ‚Äåbe over very soon, with Tuesday seeing intense bombardments , shipping still blocked in the Strait of Hormuz, and Saudi Aramco‚Äôs CEO warning of ‚Äúcatastrophic consequences‚Äù for the oil market if conflict drags on.
I‚Äôll get into that and more below.
But first, check out my latest column on why an Iran oil shock could prove politically toxic in the U.S. even if GDP escapes largely unscathed.
And listen to today's episode of the Morning Bid podcast , where I discuss ‚Äãoil-shock inflation fears and look ahead to today's big macro release - inflation data for the leadup to the crisis.
Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news ‚Äãin markets and finance seven days a week.
Oil prices initially dropped below $90 per barrel on Wednesday on reports that the ‚ÅÝInternational Energy Agency had proposed the largest release of oil reserves in its history - more than double the size of the 182 million barrels released after the Ukraine invasion in 2022.
Oil then staged ‚Äãa rebound , however, as traders digested the proposal. Goldman Sachs estimated a release the size of that in 2022 would offset 12 days of an estimated 15.4 million barrel per day ‚Äãexport disruption in the Gulf.
As prices seesaw, though, it will be hoped that Monday's panic surge in crude markets to nearly $120 per barrel has set the parameters for prices - at least until the duration of the Iran conflict becomes clearer.
Wall Street was mixed on Tuesday as the intense bombardments in the Middle East - and further Iranian threats of a comprehensive oil blockade - dampened earlier hopes for a short-lived conflict. All major ‚Äãindexes closed largely flat.
Global shares steadied somewhat on Wednesday as Japan‚Äôs Nikkei gained 1.7% and South Korea‚Äôs KOSPI rose by 1.75%. European stocks opened lower, however, while U.S. stock futures held ‚Äãsteady ahead of the bell.
Meantime, the dollar rebounded after slipping early on Wednesday, holding onto some of the early-week gains it recently shed as traders looked for signs of a quick conclusion to the war.
Later ‚Äåtoday, traders ‚ÅÝwill get a read on where U.S. CPI was in February, just before the Iran attacks started late in the month. More important, though, will be the release on Friday of February PCE data, the Fed‚Äôs favoured measure of inflation. Core inflation measured by the latter was likely already running above 3% before the oil spike.
Elsewhere, Oracle‚Äôs shares surged 8% in extended trading after its latest results, which saw it predict boosted revenue estimates well into 2027 on the AI data center boom, helping to reassure investors about the massive scale of its capex. ‚ÄãThe results also serve as a reminder of ‚Äãthe enduring AI theme in equities ‚ÅÝbeyond the day-to-day energy market ructions.
Ongoing angst about private credit funds and the quality of the loans contained within them is also rising, however. JPMorgan Chase has marked down the value of certain loans held by private credit groups and is tightening its lending to the sector, ‚Äãthe FT reported on Wednesday
Chart of the day
U.S. consumer prices likely picked up in February as the cost of gasoline increased in ‚Äãanticipation of an escalating ‚ÅÝwar in the Middle East, and with the conflict driving up oil prices, a further rise in inflation is expected in March.
The anticipated increase in the Consumer Price Index last month would also reflect the continued but staggered pass-through from President Donald Trump's sweeping tariffs, which he pursued under a law meant for use in national emergencies, that have since been struck down by the ‚ÅÝU.S. Supreme ‚ÄãCourt.
Today's events to watch
* U.S. February CPI (8:30 AM EDT)
* U.S. 10-year note auction
* Fed's Michelle Bowman speaks
* OPEC ‚ÄãMonthly Oil Market Report
Want to receive the Morning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of ‚Äãthe author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Exclusive: Shell, TotalEnergies and others declare FM to their clients who take LNG from Qatar - sources say
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 - Several companies that buy liquefied natural ‚Äågas (LNG) from QatarEnergy as portfolio players or offtakers ‚Äî including Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab , TotalEnergies (TTEF.PA) , opens new tab and some firms in Asia ‚Äî have declared force majeure ‚Äãto customers they supply, three sources told Reuters ‚Äãon Wednesday.
Qatar, the world's second-largest LNG exporter, announced ‚ÅÝa production halt at its 77 million tons ‚Äãper annum (mtpa) facility last week and declared force majeure ‚Äãon LNG shipments.
Shell , the world's largest LNG trader, declined to comment. TotalEnergies did not immediately respond to Reuters request for comment.
Both ‚Äãcompanies have long-term partnerships with QatarEnergy and are partners ‚Äãin the company's massive North Field expansion project which aims to ‚Äåboost ‚ÅÝcapacity by 2027.
Analysts estimate Shell takes 6.8 million tons per annum (mtpa) of Qatari LNG, while TotalEnergies takes 5.2 mtpa and sells them to their clients worldwide.
Qatari ‚ÄãEnergy Minister Saad ‚Äãal-Kaabi told ‚ÅÝthe Financial Times last week that it would take "weeks to months" to return to ‚Äãnormal deliveries, even if the war ended ‚Äãtoday. ‚ÅÝThe company declared force majeure on LNG shipments on Wednesday.
Sources told Reuters last week that the FM notices ‚ÅÝsent ‚Äãto clients stated that LNG deliveries ‚Äãfor March will not be affected, with the impact being felt as ‚Äãof April.

item.title: Return to Russian fuels would be a strategic blunder, von der Leyen says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 11 (Reuters) - Europe would be making a strategic ‚Äãblunder if it sought to ‚Äåblunt soaring energy prices with a return to Russian ‚Äãfossil fuels, European ‚ÄãCommission president Ursula von der ‚ÅÝLeyen said on Wednesday.
"In ‚Äãthe current crisis, some argue ‚Äãthat we should abandon our long-term strategy and even go ‚Äãback to Russian fossil ‚Äãfuels. This would be a strategic ‚Äåblunder," ‚ÅÝvon der Leyen said in a speech in European Parliament.
Von der Leyen ‚Äãsaid the ‚ÄãEU ‚ÅÝwas preparing options to lower energy prices, ‚Äãincluding the better ‚Äãuse ‚ÅÝof power purchasing agreements, state aid measures and ‚ÅÝgas ‚Äãprice subsidies or ‚Äãcaps.

item.title: Israel sees no certainty Iran‚Äôs government will fall despite war
item.description:
JERUSALEM/DUBAI, March 11 (Reuters) - Israeli officials in closed discussions have acknowledged there is no certainty the war against Iran will lead to a collapse of its clerical government, a senior Israeli official told Reuters, with no sign ‚Äãof an Iranian uprising amid the bombardment.
However, despite commentary by U.S. President Donald Trump that the war may finish soon , Israel's assessment is that Washington is not close to instructing an end ‚Äåto the conflict , two Israeli officials said.
The intense U.S. and Israeli bombing campaign killed the supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, as well as a host of senior military commanders, but it has also killed civilians and smashed homes and public buildings, angering many Iranians.
With missiles striking across Tehran and other cities, and with the Iranian authorities threatening deadly force against any who dares protest, Iranians who might otherwise come to the streets may also fear to do so until the war ends.
Still, Iran's long-term challenges ‚Äãlook worse than ever, with ever harsher sanctions strangling the economy and little prospect of better times ahead for a population whose protests in January were crushed with thousands dead.
The senior Israeli official did not say what had led the country to assess that a collapse of Iran's ruling system was not ‚ÅÝa certainty.
On the day Israel launched its joint air war with the U.S., its Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said: "Our joint action will create the conditions for the brave Iranian people to take their destiny ‚Äãinto their own hands."
He particularly spoke of Iran's main ethnic and linguistic minorities, the Kurds , Baluchis and Arabs, adding to reports that the U.S. or Israel may back uprisings by those groups.
However, in a statement on Tuesday, ‚ÄãNetanyahu repeated that though Israel's aspiration was to help Iranians "cast off the yoke of tyranny," that ultimately "it is up to them" - an apparent acknowledgement that a rising does not look imminent.
Israel and the U.S. have not issued a joint public statement setting out clear unified war aims, or articulating the conditions under which they might decide to end the campaign.
On Monday, Trump described the war as "very complete, pretty much", but on Tuesday the White House said it would only end when Trump determined its ‚Äãobjectives were met and that Iran was in a state of unconditional surrender.
In a closed‚Äëdoor briefing with foreign diplomats on Tuesday, Israeli Foreign Minister Gideon Saar declined to set a timeframe for the military campaign - ‚Äãchiming with the government's assessment that Trump was not close to ending it.
Saar acknowledged in the briefing that Iran‚Äôs government could survive the war but expressed confidence it would collapse later, sources said.
Speaking to reporters on Tuesday, Saar said ‚Äåthe war ‚ÅÝwould proceed until Israel and the U.S. determined the time had come to end hostilities, but that Israel did not seek "endless war".
Assaf Orion, a former head of strategy with the Israeli military and a fellow at the Washington Institute for Near East Policy, said weakening Iranian military capabilities appeared a more direct, tangible and measurable war aim.
"Creating conditions for regime change is indirect and hence more difficult to fathom," he said, adding that while the military campaign appeared to be planned for weeks, any uprising against Iran's ruling system might take months or years.
On Wednesday Iran's police chief Ahmadreza Radan warned ‚Äãthat "anyone taking to the streets at the enemy's ‚Äãrequest will be confronted as an enemy, not ‚ÅÝa protester. All our security forces have their fingers on the trigger".
While many Iranians want change and some openly celebrated the death of Khamenei, whose security forces killed thousands of anti-government protesters just weeks ago, there has been no sign of protest since the war began.
"I hate this regime. I want it to ‚Äãgo, but under bombardment there are no streets left to protest in," said Ali, 26, from Tehran.
Although Tehran's streets are far quieter than usual, banks, petrol ‚Äãstations and shops are all ‚ÅÝoperating as usual, though with reduced hours and tighter rationing of fuel. All government offices are open.
"Every day and every night there is bombing. My apartment building and its windows shake," said a Tehran resident who asked to retain anonymity for fear of reprisals.
"Despite discontent with the regime, the sense of Iranian pride and patriotism is growing and people are expressing hatred for Reza Pahlavi, Trump and Netanyahu," the resident added, naming the son of ‚ÅÝIran's former shah, ‚Äãwho had urged military intervention.
However, the bombing has destroyed airports and ports as well as other civilian infrastructure, adding to the ‚Äãproblems Iran's government may face in reviving a stagnant economy that has angered much of the population.
"Iran prefers the war to continue because if it ends, protests will start due to economic problems and shortcomings that the war has also worsened," said a university ‚Äãteacher in Tehran who also requested anonymity.

item.title: Spain permanently withdraws ambassador as rift with Israel deepens
item.description:
MADRID, March 11 (Reuters) - Spain permanently withdrew its ambassador to Israel on Tuesday as a ‚Äãdiplomatic standoff worsened between the two countries ‚Äåover Spain's opposition to the U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran .
The ambassador was summoned back to Spain last September amid a diplomatic ‚Äãrow over Spanish measures banning aircraft and ships ‚Äãcarrying weapons to Israel from its ports ‚ÅÝor airspace due to Israel's military offensive in ‚ÄãGaza, which Israeli Foreign Minister Gideon Sa'ar denounced ‚Äãas antisemitic.
On Tuesday, Spain published an announcement in its official gazette that the ambassador's position had been terminated. Spain's Foreign ‚ÄãMinistry said its embassy in Tel Aviv ‚Äãwill be led by a charge d'affaires for the foreseeable ‚Äåfuture.
The ‚ÅÝmove marks the latest escalation in diplomatic relations between the two countries, which have been heavily strained since Israel launched its assault on the Gaza ‚ÄãStrip in ‚ÄãOctober of ‚ÅÝ2023.
Israel's embassy in Spain is also run by a charge d'affaires after the ‚Äãcountry summoned its ambassador last May ‚Äãin protest ‚ÅÝat Spain's decision to recognise a Palestinian state.
Tensions have heightened since the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran, ‚ÅÝwith ‚ÄãSa'ar accusing Spain in early March ‚Äãof "standing with tyrants" for opposing the war.

item.title: Iran's new supreme leader 'lightly injured' but still active, Iranian official tells Reuters
item.description:
DUBAI, March 11 (Reuters) - Newly-appointed Supreme ‚ÄãLeader Mojtaba Khamenei ‚Äåwas lightly injured but ‚Äãis continuing ‚Äãto operate, an Iranian ‚ÅÝofficial ‚Äãtold Reuters on ‚ÄãWednesday.
The official did not give details ‚Äãabout ‚Äãwhen he was injured ‚Äåor ‚ÅÝwhy he had not made any ‚Äãstatement ‚Äãto ‚ÅÝthe public since ‚Äãbeing appointed ‚Äãthe ‚ÅÝnew leader.

item.title: Drones hit east Congo's biggest city, killing French aid worker, rebels and UN say
item.description:
March 11 (Reuters) - Drone strikes hit Goma in east Congo ‚Äãon Wednesday, killing at least three people including a French aid worker, the ‚Äåfirst such attack in the city since AFC/M23 rebels seized it last year, according to the group and the U.N.
The AFC/M23 blamed the attack on the Congolese army, saying in a post on X that Kinshasa ‚Äãhad launched drones against a densely populated urban area in the lakeside city.
A spokesperson ‚Äãfor the rebels said in a separate post that three people had died, ‚ÅÝincluding a foreign humanitarian worker.
A senior official for U.N. children's agency UNICEF told Reuters ‚Äãthat one of its employees, a French national, was killed.
French President Emmanuel Macron and Hadja Lahbib, ‚ÄãEuropean Commissioner for equality, preparedness and crisis management, condemned the attack in posts on X, calling for respect for international humanitarian law and saying aid workers must never be targeted.
A spokesperson for Congo's army declined to ‚Äãcomment.
A spokesperson for Congo's government did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
A Reuters ‚Äãjournalist in Goma reported hearing two loud explosions at around 4 a.m. (0200 GMT). The blasts shook windows and ‚Äådoors ‚ÅÝand were followed by the sound of ambulance sirens.
A senior AFC/M23 official told Reuters the house struck by one of the drones had been rented by UNICEF employees and is close to a residence used by former Congolese President Joseph Kabila in a neighbourhood that houses several ‚Äãprominent political and business ‚Äãfigures.
The official said a ‚ÅÝsecond drone had targeted the residence of AFC/M23 political coordinator Corneille Nangaa, but fell into Lake Kivu.
The strikes come after weeks of intensifying ‚Äãdrone operations on both sides of the conflict.
AFC/M23 military spokesperson Willy Ngoma ‚Äãwas killed in ‚ÅÝa drone attack by the Congolese army near the strategic mining town of Rubaya, some 60 km from Goma, on February 24.
The rebels have also claimed a series of drone attacks on the airport ‚ÅÝserving ‚Äãthe northeastern city of Kisangani in recent weeks.
Fighting has ‚Äãcontinued in eastern Congo on several fronts, despite mediation efforts by parties including Qatar and the U.S.

item.title: Israeli settler violence rises in West Bank under Iran war curbs
item.description:
ABU FALAH, West Bank, March 11 (Reuters) - Israeli settlers in the occupied West Bank are taking advantage of curbs on movement imposed during the war on Iran to attack Palestinians, with military roadblocks ‚Äãpreventing ambulances reaching victims quickly, rights groups and medics say.
Settlers have killed at least five Palestinians in the West Bank since the United States and Israel began airstrikes against Iran ‚Äåon February 28, according to the Palestinian health ministry. A sixth man died after inhaling teargas fired during an attack, according to Israeli rights group B'Tselem.
Israel's military blocked many West Bank roads with iron gates and mounds of earth on the first day of the war, and has largely shut crossings with Israel.
The Israeli military says the curbs are preemptive measures while it is carrying out airstrikes on Iran and against Lebanese group Hezbollah, which has fired missiles at Israel in solidarity with ‚ÄãTehran.
Palestinians in remote West Bank villages say the roadblocks have left them increasingly exposed to settler violence.
The Israeli military has also continued to carry out the raids it frequently conducts in Palestinian ‚Äãcities and towns during peacetime to arrest Palestinians, often without charge, they say.
A spokesperson for the Yesha Council, which represents Jewish settlements, did not immediately respond ‚ÅÝto a Reuters request for comment on the attacks.
Malak Beirat's husband, Thaer, was one of two Palestinians who residents and the Palestinian health ministry say were shot dead by settlers before dawn on Sunday ‚Äãin Abu Falah, north of the city of Ramallah.
"Thaer loved life. I never expected he would die," said Beirat, fighting back tears as she sat with her two children.
Witnesses told Reuters that when over 100 settlers ‚Äãgathered on the outskirts of Abu Falah, a local WhatsApp group rallied men to protect the small village. The initial confrontation involved stone throwing, but armed settlers arrived later and began shooting, they said.
Beirat's husband was shot dead while trying to protect a house from attack, a man who helped defend the village said.
Blood could still be seen on Monday in olive groves at the scene of the attack, where villagers have erected two Palestinian flags at the spots where the ‚Äãtwo men were killed - one for each victim.
A third Palestinian died after the attack. B'Tselem said his death was probably caused by the effect of teargas fired by Israeli troops deployed to the village during the ‚Äãattack.
The Israeli military says an investigation has been launched into the incident and that it condemns "violence of any kind".
Medics say the new roadblocks have led to delays in reaching injured Palestinians.
"There are obstacles - and even ‚Äåattacks by settlers ‚ÅÝand the military on the (medical) crews," said Ahmed Jibril, spokesman for the Palestinian Red Crescent ambulance service.
There have been over 109 reports of settler violence against Palestinians in the West Bank since the start of the war with Iran including shootings, physical assaults, property damage, and threats, said Israeli monitoring group Yesh Din.
All the reported killings of Palestinians by settlers this year were in the last week, B'Tselem said.
Settlers shot dead Amir Muhammad Shanaran in a village near the city of Hebron on Saturday, and brothers Muhammad and Fahim ‚ÄòAzem were shot dead in Qaryut southeast of the city of Nablus last Monday, the Palestinian health ministry said.
"Taking advantage ‚Äãof the war, armed settler militias, often operating with ‚Äãsupport from the army, continue to attack and ‚ÅÝharass Palestinian communities across the West Bank in an effort to force them out," B'Tselem said.
In three of the settler shootings, the settlers were wearing Israeli army uniform, Yesh Din said. The Israeli military did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Palestinians accuse the military of protecting settlers rather than villagers. Israel's ‚Äãmilitary denies this.
Israeli indictments of settler violence are rare. At the end of 2025, Yesh Din said that of the hundreds of cases of settler ‚Äãviolence it had documented since ‚ÅÝthe Hamas-led attacks on Israel in October 2023 that led to the Gaza war , only 2% resulted in indictments.
The United Nations says nearly 700 Palestinians were displaced by settler violence from the start of 2025 through early February 2026.
Israel's government has expanded settlements in a construction push that Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich says is aimed at burying the idea of a Palestinian state.
Right-wing Israeli minister Yossi Dagan announced on Wednesday the establishment of a new settlement ‚ÅÝin a strategic ‚Äãposition in the mountain overlooking Nablus, one of 22 new settlements announced by the Israeli government last May.
Palestinians have long sought an ‚Äãindependent state in the West Bank, Gaza and East Jerusalem, territories Israel captured and occupied in the 1967 Middle East war.
Over 700,000 settlers live in East Jerusalem and the West Bank among more than 3 million Palestinians, according to a European Union report in 2024.
Most ‚Äãof the world considers Israel's settlement activity in the West Bank illegal under international law relating to military occupations. Israel disputes this view.

item.title: EU envoys approve sanctions on 19 Iranian officials, entities over rights violations
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 11 (Reuters) - European Union envoys have approved new ‚Äãsanctions targeting 19 ‚ÄåIranian officials and entities responsible for serious human rights ‚Äãviolations, the bloc's ‚Äãforeign policy chief, Kaja ‚ÅÝKallas, said on Wednesday.
"As ‚Äãthe Iran war continues, ‚Äãthe EU will protect its interests and pursue those ‚Äãresponsible for domestic repression," ‚ÄãKallas said on X, adding ‚Äåthat "it ‚ÅÝalso sends a message to Tehran that Iran‚Äôs future cannot be built ‚Äãon ‚Äãrepression".
The ‚ÅÝsanctions, which have been in the ‚Äãmaking since before ‚Äãthe ‚ÅÝbeginning of the war, will require final approval ‚ÅÝfrom ‚Äãthe Council ‚Äãof the EU.

item.title: Israeli dentist returns to identifying war victims in Iran conflict
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 11 (Reuters) - After helping to identify the remains of the last Israeli hostage in Gaza, dentist Esi Sharon-Sagie hoped her work naming the dead killed ‚Äãin more than two years of war was finally over. Weeks later she was called back to duty.
On February 28, a ‚ÄåU.S.-Israel air assault in Iran ignited a war now engulfing the Middle East and Sharon-Sagie is again seeking to give a name to those whose bodies are too badly mutilated for visual identification techniques.
On March 1, she was called to the site of an Iranian missile attack on the town of Beit Shemesh in central Israel.
"We were informed that a missile ‚Äãkilled nine people that weren't able to get into a shelter in time. And the minute we heard that the disaster happened, ‚Äãwe had to go to do the identification," said Sharon-Sagie.
Among the dead were a brother and two sisters, 13, 15 ‚ÅÝand 16 years old.
Although she has been volunteering with police since 2010, Sharon-Sagie says that everything changed on ‚ÄãOctober 7, 2023, when Palestinian militant group Hamas launched a surprise attack on Israel.
Health authorities struggled to cope with the hundreds killed in Israel's single deadliest ‚Äãday. Identification of about 1,200 fatalities, some badly burned, was not completed for many months.
Forensic odontology is used to identify the remains of people whose bodies are in a condition that makes other methods, like visual recognition or fingerprinting, impossible. For instance, a charred or decomposed body can be identified by matching X-rays with past dental records.
Sharon-Sagie, who ‚Äãis on duty day and night, often had to summon the strength to overcome exhaustion. On one occasion, at the end of an hours-long shift, ‚Äãshe was asked to help with one more victim. She looked at the woman's slim hand, and rings.
"I spoke with her in my heart with no sound. I ‚Äålooked at ‚ÅÝher and I said: 'now you're going to help me because I have no strength anymore. You'll help me and I'll help you. I'll bring you back to your family'," said Sharon-Sagie.
A year later she helped to identify one of the masterminds of the October 7 attack.
Yahya Sinwar , then the leader of Hamas, was killed by Israeli fighters in southern Gaza in October 2024.
Sinwar's body was transferred to Israel, which still held his ‚Äãmedical records from his time in ‚Äãan Israeli prison, where he was ‚ÅÝjailed for killing Israeli soldiers and Palestinians whom Hamas suspected were spying for Israel.
"He had brain surgery in Israel many, many years ago. So we had all his information," said Sharon-Sagie.
For many in Israel, the pain of the ‚ÄãOctober 7 attack could only be alleviated with the release of the last remaining hostages from Hamas captivity ‚Äãin Gaza.
That relief finally ‚ÅÝcame on January 26 when the remains of the last of the 251 hostages were found in the Palestinian enclave.
Sharon-Sagie was on the team that checked scores of bodies buried in a cemetery in northern Gaza in the search for police officer, Ran Gvili, who fought Palestinian militants at an Israeli kibbutz.
As a volunteer with the police ‚ÅÝforce, it ‚Äãwas a complex moment for Sharon-Sagie.
"I was surprised it was actually happening. I felt my ‚Äãhands trembling, I was very moved. When we did the identification and finished signing the report that this is him, at first I was happy but seconds after that I understood ‚Äãthat it is over, he is not coming back to Israel alive," she said.

item.title: OPEC confirms big Saudi oil production hike ahead of Iran war, holds forecasts steady
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 (Reuters) - OPEC said on ‚ÄãWednesday that Saudi Arabia sharply increased ‚Äåoil production in February ahead of U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran and ‚Äãkept its forecasts for relatively ‚Äãstrong global oil demand growth this ‚ÅÝyear.
Saudi Arabia increased its oil ‚Äãoutput and exports as part of ‚Äãa contingency plan in case any U.S. strike on Iran disrupts supplies from the Middle ‚ÄãEast, sources familiar with the ‚Äãplan had said in February.
The attack came on ‚ÄåFebruary ‚ÅÝ28 and the resulting conflict has disrupted oil exports, forced production stoppages and sent prices soaring.
OPEC, in a ‚Äãmonthly report ‚Äãon its ‚ÅÝwebsite, said that Saudi Arabia told OPEC its ‚Äãoil supply to the market ‚Äãin ‚ÅÝFebruary was 10.111 million barrels per day while production was 10.882 million ‚ÅÝbpd. ‚ÄãSaudi Arabia reported its ‚Äãproduction at 10.10 million bpd for January.

item.title: EU countries give final approval to 2040 target
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 5 (Reuters) - European ‚ÄåUnion countries gave the final approval on Thursday to a new climate target to slash greenhouse gas emissions 90% by 2040, pressing ahead with the bloc's ambitious climate agenda despite political resistance.
The new climate target ‚Äãis a hard-fought political compromise, struck by governments and EU lawmakers last year. ‚ÄãIt is more ambitious than most major economies' emissions-cutting commitments, including China's.
In ‚ÅÝpractice, the target will require an 85% emissions reduction from European industries against 1990 ‚Äãlevels. The EU will pay developing countries via carbon credits, so they cut emissions on ‚ÄãEurope's behalf to make up the rest, to reach 90%.
The EU agreed the target last year following months of wrangling between countries, such as Spain, which say worsening droughts and wildfires justified more ambitious ‚Äãgoals, and those like Poland and Italy, which sought to soften the emissions cuts, ‚Äãarguing that struggling industries cannot afford the upfront investments.
A reinforced majority of EU countries' ministers gave the ‚Äåfinal ‚ÅÝformal sign-off to the legally binding goal at a meeting in Brussels. The Czech Republic, Slovakia, Poland and Hungary opposed it. The climate target will now pass into EU law.
As part of the agreement, the EU will also consider the option in future to ‚Äãuse international carbon credits ‚Äãto meet a ‚ÅÝfurther 5% of its 2040 emissions reductions - potentially further softening the domestic efforts required.
The deal also delays the launch of a politically ‚Äãsensitive new EU carbon market by one year, to 2028 - a ‚Äãmove designed ‚ÅÝto win over countries sceptical of the climate goal.
The target, which is designed to keep Europe on track to meet its pledge to reach net-zero emissions by 2050, falls short of the ‚ÅÝ90% domestic ‚Äãemissions cut recommended by the EU's climate science ‚Äãadvisers.
It is also weaker than Brussels' original plan for the goal, reflecting disagreement between EU governments over the speed and ‚Äãcost of their green agenda.

item.title: U.S. military tells civilians to avoid port facilities where Iranian navy operating
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 11 (Reuters) - The U.S. military on ‚ÄãWednesday warned civilians ‚Äåto leave port facilities where Iranian ‚Äãnaval forces are ‚Äãoperating, adding that U.S. ‚ÅÝforces could ‚Äãnot guarantee their ‚Äãsafety.
"Iranian dockworkers, administrative personnel, and commercial vessel crews ‚Äãshould avoid ‚ÄãIranian naval vessels and military ‚Äåequipment," ‚ÅÝU.S. Central Command said in a statement. The ‚ÄãUnited States ‚Äãhas ‚ÅÝbeen carrying out strikes ‚Äãagainst Iranian naval ‚Äãvessels-- ‚ÅÝsinking or destroying 60 so ‚ÅÝfar.

item.title: Turkey opposition leader says Imamoglu trial is purely political
item.description:
ISTANBUL, March 11 (Reuters) - The trial of Istanbul's jailed mayor, Ekrem Imamoglu, is a purely political affair ‚Äãaimed at preventing him from challenging President Tayyip Erdogan in the next election, Turkey's main opposition leader told ‚ÄåReuters in an interview on Wednesday.
Imamoglu, 55, appeared in court on Monday in the opening hearing of a sweeping corruption case involving more than 400 defendants linked to the Istanbul municipality.
Prosecutors accuse him of leading a criminal organisation for profit through tender-rigging and bribery, charges he and his Republican People's Party (CHP) deny.
"Let ‚Äãme put it plainly. In Turkey, what is happening is not a trial in the normal sense of the word. ‚ÄãIt is not a legal process but a political one," CHP leader Ozgur Ozel said.
The judges handling ‚ÅÝthe case have been tasked "not with conducting a fair trial but with delivering the decision that Erdogan wants", he said.
Erdogan's government denies ‚Äãinfluencing the judiciary and says Turkey's courts are independent. The investigation into the Istanbul municipality was led by Akin Gurlek, who last month was ‚Äãappointed justice minister.
Imamoglu has been held in pre-trial detention for nearly a year amid an unprecedented judicial crackdown on the CHP that rights groups and opposition figures say has damaged the democratic credentials of NATO member Turkey.
Ozel said he did not expect Imamoglu to be released from detention during ‚Äãhis trial, adding that such an outcome would require greater social pressure within Turkey, international pressure, or a further drop in Erdogan's ‚Äãpoll ratings.
Ozel said Erdogan wanted to keep Imamoglu in prison because, if released, he could campaign across the country ahead of elections that the opposition ‚Äåleader ‚ÅÝbelieves will take place next year, ahead of schedule.
"If Imamoglu gets out, he could travel all over Turkey and launch a major campaign. That is why Erdogan wants to keep him inside," he said.
Opinion polls show Imamoglu performing strongly against Erdogan in any presidential race, while party polling suggests a tight race between the secularist CHP and Erdogan‚Äôs Islamist-rooted AKP, with the CHP holding a narrow lead.
Ozel said ‚ÄãTurkish democracy had sharply deteriorated ‚Äãunder Erdogan, who has led ‚ÅÝTurkey as prime minister or president since 2003.
"Turkey's democracy has probably gone back 50 or 60 years," he said. "We have fallen into what I would call the third division in the world."
Ozel also said ‚Äãhe expected pressure on the opposition to intensify as elections approach.
"Things will get worse, and [Erdogan] will ‚Äãbecome more aggressive. ‚ÅÝWe are open to every kind of attack, both through legal channels and through other means," he said, adding that the opposition was not afraid and believed it would defeat Erdogan and his ruling AK Party at the ballot box.
Ozel said he believed Erdogan was preparing for ‚ÅÝan early ‚Äãelection in September or October 2027 rather than waiting until the scheduled 2028 ‚Äãvote because under the constitution an earlier ballot could allow the president to run again.
"We want an election as soon as possible," Ozel added. "If the election is held ‚Äãin June 2028, Erdogan cannot run. If it is held early, he can."

item.title: Chile's Kast to take office in biggest right-wing shift in decades
item.description: Chile's Kast to take office in biggest right-wing shift in decades

item.title: Iran cannot participate in World Cup, sports minister says
item.description:
March 11 (Reuters) - Iran cannot participate in the 2026 World Cup after co-host ‚Äãthe U.S. launched airstrikes alongside Israel, Sports Minister Ahmad Donyamali said on Wednesday.
The attacks killed the Islamic Republic's Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali ‚ÄåKhamenei and triggered a region-wide conflict .
"Considering that this corrupt regime has assassinated our leader, under no circumstances can we participate in the World Cup," the minister told state television.
The 48-team World Cup will be held in the U.S., Mexico and Canada from June 11 to July 19.
"Our children are not safe and, fundamentally, such conditions for participation do not exist," Donyamali said.
"Given ‚Äãthe malicious actions they have carried out against Iran, they have forced two wars on us over eight or nine months and have ‚Äãkilled and martyred thousands of our people. Therefore, we certainly cannot have such a presence."
More than 1,300 Iranian civilians have ‚ÅÝbeen killed since the U.S. and Israeli airstrikes began on February 28, according to Iran's U.N. ambassador Amir Saeid Iravani.
Iran are grouped with Belgium, Egypt and New Zealand.
All three of their Group G matches are scheduled to take place in the U.S., two in Los ‚ÄãAngeles and one in Seattle.
Iran, who dominated the Asian qualifying rounds to qualify for the tournament in March last year, was the only nation missing from a FIFA planning summit for World Cup participants held last week in Atlanta.
There was no immediate comment from the Iranian Football Federation or world soccer's governing body FIFA.
FIFA regulations state that any team that ‚Äãwithdraws from the tournament "no later than 30 days before the first match" will be fined at least 250,000 Swiss francs ($320,800).
"Disciplinary sanctions may include the expulsion ‚Äãof the participating member association concerned from subsequent FIFA competitions and/or the replacement of the participating member association with another member association," FIFA's regulations say.
"The FIFA Council or the ‚Äårelevant committee ‚ÅÝmay decide, in particular, to replace the member association in question with another association."
Iran had selected Tucson's sprawling Kino Sports Complex as the team's base camp and 18 months of preparation hang in the balance with a potential economic hit in Arizona.
There is also the question of tickets to World Cup games involving Iran. Should Iran boycott the tournament, Iranian fans who bought tickets for eye-watering prices may be tempted to sell them on the huge resale market .
Earlier, FIFA President Gianni Infantino said ‚Äãhe had met U.S. President Donald ‚ÅÝTrump, who told him he welcomed Iran's participation in the World Cup.
Trump had previously said " I really don't care " if Iran participated or not, but Infantino said he had a productive discussion with the president.
"During the discussions, President Trump reiterated that ‚Äãthe Iranian team is, of course, welcome to compete in the tournament in the United States," Infantino said.
A ‚Äãsource in Tehran familiar ‚ÅÝwith the matter said that as well as Iran's decision not to attend the World Cup, warm-up games were not possible because of the war.
Earlier this week, Australia granted humanitarian visas to five Iranian women soccer players after they sought asylum, fearing persecution on their return home for their refusal to sing the national anthem at ‚ÅÝa Women's ‚ÄãAsian Cup match.
Trump had called on Australia to give asylum to members of the Iran ‚Äãwomen's soccer team.
On Wednesday, Australian police helped two more members of the Iranian women's soccer delegation slip their minders to claim asylum, but one has changed her mind and decided to go back to ‚ÄãIran, the country's interior minister said.
($1 = 0.7793 Swiss francs)

item.title: Turkey's Erdogan says Iran war must stop before whole region dragged in
item.description:
ANKARA, March 11 (Reuters) - Turkish President Tayyip Erdogan ‚Äåsaid on Wednesday that the war in Iran must be stopped before the whole Middle East region is "thrown into the fire", adding that diplomacy must be given a chance.
NATO air defences have in the last week intercepted two ‚ÄãIranian missiles headed toward southern Turkey, where there are air and radar bases used ‚Äãby NATO and the United States, and the alliance has since beefed up ‚ÅÝdefences there.
"This war must be stopped before it grows and the region as a whole ‚Äãis thrown into the fire. If diplomacy is given a chance, it is very possible to ‚Äãachieve this," Erdogan told lawmakers in parliament.
"In light of the sensitivity of the period we are in, we are speaking very carefully. We are acting with caution to protect Turkey from the fire that is surrounding ‚Äãit," he said, adding that Ankara was engaged with all sides in seeking an end to ‚Äãthe war.
Turkey had offered to mediate between the sides before the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran began 12 days ‚Äåago. ‚ÅÝSince then, Erdogan has repeatedly called for diplomacy to end the conflict, while also warning that Turkey would respond to any threats to its national security.
Ozgur Ozel, chairman of Turkey's main opposition Republican People's Party (CHP) told Reuters in an interview on Wednesday that Erdogan had avoided criticising U.S. President ‚ÄãDonald Trump by name ‚Äãin order to ‚ÅÝmaintain their good personal rapport, adding that this had only emboldened the U.S. leader.
Ankara's "shy silence regarding Trump's actions, and its failure to take a ‚Äãstance against that disproportionate force, is not neutrality," Ozel said, adding that ‚Äãthe U.S. ‚ÅÝwas not taking Turkish sensitivities into account during the conflict.
Erdogan has criticised U.S., Israeli and Iranian actions and has called the U.S.-Israeli air campaign a "clear violation" of international law.
But in his comments, like many other world ‚ÅÝleaders, he ‚Äãhas refrained from mentioning Trump by name. Erdogan's good ‚Äãpersonal ties with Trump have helped ease bilateral U.S.-Turkish tensions over the last year.

item.title: More than 330 arrested in Europol-led operation against illicit waste trade
item.description:
MADRID, March 11 (Reuters) - More than 330 people have been arrested across 70 countries in a ‚Äãcrackdown on organised crime networks accused ‚Äåof trafficking illicit waste within Europe and exporting it to Africa, Asia and Latin America, Europol said ‚Äãon Wednesday.
Authorities seized 127,149 metric tons ‚Äãof waste, 602 tons of polluting agents ‚ÅÝalong with 75 tons of plant ‚Äãprotection chemicals, 2.3 tons of mercury and nearly ‚Äã10 million euros ($11.6 million) in cash and bank accounts.
The seized waste could have generated illicit profits of ‚Äãat least 31 million euros, officials said.
Illegal ‚Äãwaste trafficking has become a global problem, operating through ‚Äåparallel ‚ÅÝcriminal circuits, Spain's Guardia Civil, which co‚Äëled the operation, said.
"These criminal networks not only fraudulently manage urban and industrial waste, but ‚Äãalso systematically resort ‚Äãto ‚ÅÝdocument forgery and fraud to move hazardous materials, causing negative environmental ‚Äãimpacts and risks to public health," ‚Äãit ‚ÅÝsaid.
Last year, Spanish police said they had arrested a gang suspected of smuggling more than 40,000 ‚ÅÝtons ‚Äãa year of untreated urban ‚Äãwaste from Italy to Spain.
($1 = 0.8627 euros)

item.title: How many ships have been attacked in the Gulf since start of Iran war?
item.description:
DUBAI, March 11 (Reuters) - The U.S.-Israeli war on Iran has threatened Gulf ports and disrupted global trade through the Strait of Hormuz through which roughly a fifth of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas normally passes.
Iran's Revolutionary Guards have warned that ‚Äãany ship passing through the narrow Strait will be targeted.
Below is a list of reported attacks since the ‚Äåwar started on February 28:
A crew member was killed on board Marshall Islands‚Äìflagged crude tanker MKD VYOM after it was hit by a projectile as the vessel sailed off the Omani coast, 50 nautical miles north of the capital Muscat, manager V.Ships said.
Gibraltar-flagged oil bunkering tanker ‚ÄãHercules Star, which supplies fuel to ships, was struck by a projectile 17 nautical miles northwest of UAE's ‚ÄãMina Saqr in Ras Al Khaimah. A fire caused by the attack was extinguished, UKMTO said.
A ‚ÅÝPalau-flagged tanker was attacked in the Strait of Hormuz, two nautical miles north of Oman's Kumzar. The crew of ‚Äãthe U.S.-sanctioned Skylight was evacuated, Oman's maritime security centre said.
U.S.-flagged products tanker Stena Imperative was struck by two projectiles in ‚Äãthe port of Bahrain, which resulted in a fire and the crew evacuating, UKMTO said.
Marshall Islands crude oil tanker Libra Trader and Panama-flagged bulker Gold Oak also sustained minor damage about 7-10 nautical miles off the UAE's port of Fujairah, UKMTO said.
Maltese-flagged container ship Safeen ‚ÄãPrestige was damaged by a projectile as it sailed towards the top of the strait, two nautical miles north of ‚ÄãOman. The attack caused a fire in the engine room and prompted its crew to abandon ship, according to shipping sources.
Crude oil ‚Äåtanker ‚ÅÝSonangol Namibe was hit by a blast while anchored near Iraq's Khor al Zubair port, U.S. representative company Sonangol Marine Services said.
An Iranian remote-controlled boat laden with explosives was used to target and damage the Bahamas-flagged ship, according to initial assessments from two Iraqi port security sources.
A tugboat was hit by projectiles in the Strait of Hormuz, six nautical miles north ‚Äãof Oman, while conducting operations ‚Äãwith vessel Safeen Prestige, which ‚ÅÝwas struck on March 4, UKMTO said.
UKMTO reported - citing a third party it did not name - a possible drone attack 10 nautical miles north of Saudi Arabia's Jubail. It ‚Äãsaid the majority of the crew was evacuated.
Thailand-flagged bulk carrier Mayuree Naree was attacked ‚Äãby a projectile ‚ÅÝin the Strait, 11 nautical miles north of Oman. A fire broke out on board and prompted the crew to evacuate, the vessel's owner Bangkok-headquartered Precious Shipping (PSL.BK) , opens new tab said in a statement.
Japan-flagged container ship One Majesty sustained minor damage after it was hit by ‚ÅÝa projectile ‚Äã25 nautical miles northwest of UAE's Ras Al Khaimah.
The hull of Marshall ‚ÄãIslands-flagged bulk carrier Star Gwyneth, was damaged after being hit by a projectile 50 nautical miles northwest of Dubai, maritime risk management company Vanguard said. ‚ÄãThere was no environmental impact reported and the crew was reported safe, UKMTO said.

item.title: Trump tells Axios war in Iran will end soon, 'practically nothing left' to target
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 11 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump told Axios on Wednesday that ‚Äãthere's "practically nothing ‚Äãleft" to target in Iran ‚ÅÝand that the war ‚Äãthere will end "soon."
"Little this ‚Äãand that... Any time I want it to ‚Äãend, it will ‚Äãend," Trump said during a ‚Äåbrief ‚ÅÝphone interview.
The conflict began nearly two weeks ago with ‚Äãstrikes ‚Äãon ‚ÅÝIran by the U.S. and ‚ÄãIsrael aimed at ‚Äãdismantling ‚ÅÝIran's military capabilities and nuclear program.

item.title: UK releases first files on Mandelson's appointment as US ambassador
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 (Reuters) - The British government published the first documents covering the appointment of ‚ÄãPeter Mandelson as ambassador to Washington on Wednesday, reviving questions about Prime Minister Keir Starmer's judgment in employing a man ‚Äåclose to Jeffrey Epstein.
The publication of the first tranche of documents covering the vetting of Mandelson, under police investigation for allegedly leaking government documents to the late sex offender, does little to reduce the pressure on Starmer, who is under fire over the appointment and a series of policy U-turns.
The more than 100 pages of documents ‚Äãsuggested concerns were raised about the "reputational risks" in appointing Mandelson, particularly over his friendship with Epstein, but also his previous resignations ‚Äãfrom government and his support for closer ties with China.
Starmer sacked Mandelson from what is considered the most prestigious ‚ÅÝposting in Britain's diplomatic service in September, when the depth of his friendship with the convicted sex offender started to become clear.
A document labelled "Advice to the prime minister, checks conducted on 4 December, 2024", said: "After Epstein was first convicted of procuring an underage girl in ‚Äã2008, their relationship continued across 2009-2011, beginning when Lord Mandelson was business minister and continuing after the end of the Labour government. Mandelson reportedly stayed in Epstein's House while he was in jail in June 2009."
In a summary of a fact-finding call between the prime minister's general counsel and the national security adviser, Jonathan Powell, in ‚ÄãSeptember, a document said that Powell found the appointment process "unusual" and "weirdly rushed".
Powell said he had concerns about Mandelson's "reputation‚Äù in conversations with Morgan McSweeney, ‚Äãthen the prime minister's chief of staff. According to the note, Philip Barton, then the most senior government official at the foreign ministry, "also had reservations around the ‚Äåappointment".
Starmer's allies have sought to play down the importance of the first set of documents, saying an ongoing police investigation meant some of the more revealing exchanges were being withheld.
Further documents will be released at a later date, papers which the British prime minister's team say will prove Mandelson lied to Starmer about the extent of his relationship with Epstein before his appointment as ambassador in December 2024.
Darren Jones, Starmer's chief secretary, ‚Äãtold lawmakers last month the first ‚Äãtranche of documents would not ‚ÅÝinclude any correspondence between Starmer's Downing Street office and Mandelson, when follow-up questions were asked, due to the probe.
Mandelson, a government minister when Labour was previously in power more than 15 years ago, quit the House ‚Äãof Lords, the upper house of parliament, in February over his links to Epstein. He was arrested ‚Äãlast month on suspicion of ‚ÅÝmisconduct in a public office, and later released on bail.
Files released by the U.S. Justice Department in January included emails suggesting Mandelson had leaked government documents to Epstein, and that the convicted offender had recorded payments to Mandelson or his then-partner, now husband.
Mandelson has said he does not recall having ‚ÅÝreceived payments. ‚ÄãHe has not commented publicly on allegations he leaked documents, and did not respond to ‚Äãmessages seeking comment.
Starmer has expressed regret over the appointment, saying the Labour veteran had created a "litany of deceit" about his ties to Epstein, an apology that did little to quieten ‚Äãopposition voices saying the prime minister should step down.

item.title: UNESCO fears for fate of historical sites during Iran war
item.description:
PARIS, March 11 (Reuters) - UNESCO said it is deeply concerned about the fate of world heritage sites in Iran and across the region, after Tehran's Golestan palace, often compared to ‚ÄãVersailles, and a historic mosque and palace in Isfahan were damaged in the ‚Äåwar.
The United Nations' cultural agency on Wednesday urged all parties to protect the region's outstanding cultural sites, saying four of Iran's 29 world heritage sites had been damaged since the start of the U.S. and ‚ÄãIsraeli war with Iran.
"UNESCO is deeply concerned by the first impact that the hostilities ‚Äãare already having on many world heritage sites," Lazare Eloundou Assomo, director ‚ÅÝof the World Heritage Centre, told Reuters, adding he was also concerned for sites in ‚ÄãIsrael, Lebanon and across the Middle East.
Tehran's Golestan palace, damaged in U.S.‚ÄìIsraeli strikes, is testimony ‚Äãto the grandeur of Iran's civilization in the 19th century, he said.
The palace was chosen as the Persian royal residence and seat of power by the Qajar family and shows the introduction of European styles in ‚ÄãPersian arts, according to the UNESCO website. The last Shah of Iran, Mohammad Reza Pahlavi, ‚Äãheld a coronation ceremony there in 1969.
"We sometimes even compare it with the Versailles Palace in France, ‚Äåfor instance, ‚ÅÝand it has suffered, unfortunately, some damage. We don't know the extent for the moment. But clearly, with the images that we have been able to receive, we can confirm ... it has been affected," Eloundou Assomo said.
Photos of the interior of the palace have shown piles ‚Äãof smashed glass and ‚Äãshards of wood ‚ÅÝon the floor, and shattered woodwork.
Isfahan was one of Central Asia's most important cities and a key point on the Silk Road trading ‚Äãroute. Its Masjed-e Jame (Jameh Mosque) is more than 1,000 years old and ‚Äãshows the ‚ÅÝdevelopment of Islamic art through 12 centuries.
Buildings close to the buffer zone of the prehistoric sites of the Khorramabad Valley have also been damaged, UNESCO said.
UNESCO has shared coordinates of key cultural ‚ÅÝsites to ‚Äãall parties, Eloundou Assomo said, and was monitoring damage.
"We ‚Äãare calling for the protection of all sites of cultural significance ... everything that tells the history of all the ‚Äãcivilisations of the 18 countries in the region," he said.

item.title: Carney on verge of majority in Canada parliament after opposition MP defects
item.description:
OTTAWA, March 11 (Reuters) - Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney on Wednesday moved closer to a parliamentary majority that could help ‚Äãhim counter U.S. tariffs when an opposition legislator joined the ‚Äåruling Liberals.
The centrist Liberals, governing with a minority after an election last April, need opposition support to pass key legislation such as budgets. That can be a ‚Äãslow process, and Carney has said he needs a majority ‚Äãto react more effectively to U.S. President Donald Trump's trade ‚ÅÝmeasures.
Carney said in a statement that Lori Idlout of the ‚Äãsmall left-leaning New Democratic Party, who represents the Arctic territory of Nunavut, ‚Äãwould sit with the Liberals in the House of Commons, the elected chamber.
Idlout is the fourth opposition legislator to defect to the Liberals since last November and ‚Äãher decision means the party now has 170 seats in the ‚Äã343-seat House, two short of a majority.
Special elections will be held on April ‚Äå13 ‚ÅÝin three vacant constituencies previously held by Liberals, two of which are safe seats. If the Liberals win two of the three, Carney would have control of the chamber.
A majority would open the way to ‚ÄãCarney serving until ‚ÄãOctober 2029. Canadian ‚ÅÝelections are held no later than every four years.
"At this important moment in our history, Canadians are ‚Äãcoming together to build a strong future," said Carney, ‚Äãwho ‚ÅÝhas promised to increase defense spending to better protect the vast and almost totally uninhabited Canadian Arctic.
‚ÄúWith new threats against our sovereignty and pressures ‚ÅÝon ‚Äãthe wellbeing of people throughout the North, ‚Äãwe need a strong and ambitious government that makes decisions with Nunavut ‚Äî not only about ‚ÄãNunavut," Idlout said in a statement.
(This story has been refiled to fix the hyperlink in paragraph 2)

item.title: Poland to seek help from two other countries in Epstein investigation
item.description:
WARSAW, March 11 (Reuters) - Poland will ask two other European countries for information and evidence needed for its investigation into human trafficking related to late U.S. sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, ‚Äãprosecutors said on Wednesday.
In a statement, they said documents from the Epstein ‚Äåfiles indicated a reasonable suspicion that human trafficking had taken place in Poland. They did not name the European countries they would contact but a source familiar with the matter told Reuters ‚Äãthe prosecutors would ask France and Sweden for help.
The U.S. Justice Department's release of ‚Äãmillions of internal documents related to Epstein has revealed the late financier ‚ÅÝand sex offender's ties to many prominent people in politics, finance, academia and ‚Äãbusiness - both before and after he pleaded guilty in 2008 to prostitution charges.
In February, Polish ‚ÄãPrime Minister Donald Tusk announced that Poland would launch an investigation into possible links between Epstein and Russian intelligence, as well as any offences affecting Polish citizens.
The Polish National Prosecutor's Office confirmed in its ‚Äãstatement that it had initiated an investigation into human trafficking committed in the period ‚Äãfrom 2009 to August 2019 on the territory of Poland and other countries.
Prosecutors suspect the trafficking consisted ‚Äåof ‚ÅÝrecruiting women and girls for work abroad under false pretences and of then transporting them outside Poland and handing them over to other people for sexual exploitation, the statement said.
Files reviewed by Reuters show that a man called Daniel Siad had informed Epstein ‚Äãabout his travels through ‚ÄãPoland, Slovakia, the Czech ‚ÅÝRepublic, among other countries, scouting for models.
He also mentioned his cooperation with Jean-Luc Brunel, a key suspect and longtime Epstein associate, ‚Äãwho died in a French prison in 2022.
According to Polish media ‚Äãreports, Siad ‚ÅÝwas born in Algeria and moved to Sweden at the age of 23.
Reuters reached out to him on two phone numbers and an e-mail address found in the files, but ‚ÅÝhas ‚Äãnot yet received answers to questions sent.
In February, Swedish ‚Äãnewspaper Expressen quoted Siad as saying he had never committed a crime and was open to talking to ‚Äãinvestigators in any interested country.

item.title: Higher oil clouds Wall Street's sunny earnings outlook
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 11 (Reuters) - U.S. companies face structurally higher oil prices this year even if the Iran war ends soon, meaning investors may need to rethink those sunny 2026 corporate earnings forecasts.
Heading into the year, the consensus 2026 outlook for oil was fairly bearish, while the earnings forecasts for Wall Street were pretty optimistic.
The latter ‚Äãhasn't changed. As of Friday, full-year 2026 earnings growth estimates, according to LSEG data, were nearly 16%, up from 14% last year and 12% the year before.
However, those ‚Äårosy outlooks assume an average oil price this year close to $60 per barrel ‚Äì an expectation that disappeared into thin air with the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran on February 28 and subsequent supply disruption.
Oil market volatility has been wild. Crude recorded its biggest weekly rise on record last week, and rose further to almost $120 a barrel this week before plunging on hopes for a swift end to the conflict.
While oil will likely drift lower when the war eventually subsides, the damage is done. The finely tuned ‚Äãglobal energy system has been upended, infrastructure has been hit and the expected supply glut has evaporated.
Average oil prices this year will almost certainly be much higher than businesses were budgeting ‚Äãfor on January 1. Companies will absorb part of that increase, and consumers will certainly feel the pinch. Either way, corporate earnings will be squeezed.
In a Reuters poll in December, the consensus average 2026 forecast for Brent crude was $61.27 a barrel, with oversupply expected to offset possible supply disruption from the brewing U.S.-Iran tensions. That would have ‚Äãrepresented a 7% decline from the 2025 average of $68.20.
These forecasts have since been torn up.
Analysts at HSBC on Tuesday raised their average 2026 Brent forecast to $80 from $65 and their U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude outlook to $76 from $61. These ‚Äãare increases of 23% and 25%, respectively.
Also on Tuesday, the U.S. Energy Information Administration upped its average 2026 Brent crude forecast to $79 from $58 last month. That's a 36% increase.
Few sectors of the economy will escape the impact of energy price hikes like that. Gasoline, jet fuel, fertilizer, petrochemicals and plastics will all be more expensive, with pain felt in industries as diverse as transportation, manufacturing, metals, retail and food.
"As prices rise, consumption is affected, and, ultimately, corporate earnings erode," ‚Äãsays Joe Brusuelas, chief economist at RSM US LLP.
Equity strategists at Goldman Sachs reckon the direct impact of "modestly" higher oil prices on S&P 500 earnings should be fairly muted, ‚Äãbut an extended period of supply disruption or uncertainty poses much greater risk to economic activity.
For every one percentage point decline in real U.S. GDP growth, S&P 500 earnings per share could fall 3-4%, they posit.
Other estimates suggest ‚Äåa 30% ‚ÅÝrise in oil prices could knock as much as 4% off S&P 500 earnings, with the pain felt most acutely in transportation, industrial, and consumer discretionary.
Consumer spending accounts for around 70% of U.S. economic activity. Sharply higher energy costs will squeeze household budgets, and spending elsewhere will suffer.
Alarm bells are already ringing. Average U.S. gasoline prices are now over $3.50 a gallon, according to American Automobile Association data. That's up 17% from before the war started.
There's a flip side, of course. Sustained higher oil prices boost energy sector profits, and double-digit earnings growth for the sector is not out of the question. But energy only accounts ‚Äãfor 4‚Äì5% of total S&P 500 earnings and ‚Äãthus is unlikely to offset the hit to ‚ÅÝmargins elsewhere.
Within current 2026 earnings growth forecasts, there is incredibly wide dispersion among the 11 sectors.
As of Friday, the energy sector EPS had sunk to minus 1.2%, by far the gloomiest forecast and the only one pointing to declining profits.
At the other end of the scale, tech's ‚Äã2026 EPS growth estimate was 35.9%, comfortably the highest among all sectors and up from 30.8% on January 1. Tech has contributed the lion's ‚Äãshare of the S&P 500's ‚ÅÝearnings growth in recent years.
But higher energy prices are a blow for the mega-cap firms leading the artificial intelligence arms race. UBS analysts estimate capex spending by the "hyperscalers" this year will hit $770 billion. The construction and running of those data centers could now get a lot more expensive.
Investors were already nervous about the future returns on these outlays. Significantly more expensive energy will only deepen their fears at a time when risks to ‚ÅÝmany other sectors ‚Äãare growing too.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Jamie McGeever , a columnist for Reuters)

item.title: UN warns global aid at risk as Middle East war spreads
item.description:
GENEVA, March 11 (Reuters) - The United Nations aid chief warned on Wednesday that the conflict in the Middle East is straining humanitarian operations worldwide, ‚Äãdisrupting supply chains and slowing the delivery of life‚Äësaving assistance ‚Äåto numerous crisis zones.
‚ÄúWe are in a moment of grave peril for the Middle East and, actually I believe, for the wider world,‚Äù Tom Fletcher, the U.N. aid ‚Äãchief, told Reuters.
The U.S.-Israeli war with Iran, which has expanded to ‚ÄãLebanon and dragged in Gulf countries, has convulsed global markets ‚ÅÝand disrupted supply chains , with airspace closures and the halt of shipping ‚Äãthrough the critical Strait of Hormuz .
Fletcher said aid supplies to Gaza and sub‚ÄëSaharan Africa are ‚Äãbeing affected by the conflict, as humanitarian relief that needs to travel through the Strait of Hormuz or through airspace in the Gulf has been largely blocked or constrained.
Conflict-ridden ‚ÄãSomalia, which is facing a major drought, and Sudan are among ‚Äãa number of countries facing dire humanitarian crises.
"These (constraints) will damage our humanitarian supply chains, reduce ‚Äåthe ‚ÅÝhumanitarian supplies we can get to people who need them, but they'll also drive up energy costs and food costs across the region," Fletcher said.
"This really is a perfect storm of factors right now, and I'm seriously ‚Äãworried," he stated.
There ‚Äãis particular concern ‚ÅÝfor aid supplies to sub‚ÄëSaharan Africa, Flether said, which are being impacted by restricted movement through the Strait ‚Äãof Hormuz, with alternative routes slapped with rising freight costs.
Higher ‚Äãoil ‚ÅÝprices are making the U.N.'s aid deliveries by air more expensive, at a time when UN agencies and NGOs budgets are already facing major donor cuts , Fletcher ‚ÅÝexplained.
"We've ‚Äãjust about put some money together to keep ‚Äãmore of our UN humanitarian flights in the air, but they've suddenly, almost overnight, become ‚Äãmuch more expensive," Fletcher said.

item.title: Iran has laid about a dozen mines in Strait of Hormuz, sources say
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 11 (Reuters) - Iran has deployed about a dozen mines in the Strait of Hormuz, two sources familiar with the matter ‚Äãsaid, in a move likely to complicate the reopening ‚Äåof the narrow waterway, an important route for shipping oil and liquefied natural gas.
Exports of oil and LNG through the strategic chokepoint along ‚ÄãIran's coast have effectively been halted by the war ‚Äãlaunched 12 days ago by the United States and ‚ÅÝIsrael, helping to drive a surge in world energy ‚Äãprices.
Iran‚Äôs military command on Wednesday said the world should be prepared ‚Äãfor oil to hit $200 a barrel.
One source said the locations of most of the mines are known but declined to say how the ‚ÄãU.S. planned to deal with them.
CNN first reported the mining ‚Äãof the strait on Tuesday.
Iran has long threatened to retaliate against any ‚Äåmilitary ‚ÅÝattack by mining the strait. About a fifth of global oil and LNG normally passes through the strait , and Tehran's ability to stop shipping through the channel gives it enormous leverage ‚Äãover the U.S. ‚Äãand its allies.
The ‚ÅÝU.S. military says it has targeted Iranian mine-laying vessels, eliminating 16 of them on Tuesday. But ‚Äãthe U.S. Navy has so far declined to provide ‚Äãprotective ‚ÅÝescorts to commercial ships through the strait.
U.S. President Donald Trump on Tuesday demanded that Iran immediately remove any mines deployed in ‚ÅÝthe ‚Äãstrait and he said that it ‚Äãwould face unspecified military consequences if it failed to do so.

item.title: Trump administration considering looser protections for North Atlantic right whale
item.description:
March 3 (Reuters) - The Trump administration on Tuesday took the first ‚Äãstep toward loosening regulations ‚Äåthat protect the endangered North Atlantic right whale from ‚Äãvessel strikes.
