If climate change kicks off a new era of eruptions in the coming decades, Smellie said, it would hasten the already rapid loss of the continent‚Äôs ice cover due to climate change, adding to rising sea levels and swamping the world‚Äôs big coastal cities. Antarctica is shedding more than 150 billion metric tons¬Ýof ice a year, according to satellite measurements, and scientists believe that as oceans continue to warm, the West Antarctic Ice Sheet is fast approaching a tipping point beyond which the melt cannot be stopped.
‚ÄúIf an eruption adds even a small amount to global sea level, it will exacerbate effects already happening due to climate change,‚Äù Smellie said.
Under the worst-case scenario, rapid melting of the West Antarctic Ice Sheet would ease the pressure on buried volcanoes, triggering eruptions. This in turn would speed up ice loss, awakening even more volcanoes that melt more ice, and so on, leading to global catastrophe. However, Smellie said, that is unlikely to happen because thousands of nearly simultaneous eruptions would be needed to melt even a small proportion of the ice sheet.
In the danger zone
Few people make their home near Askja. The closest village lies about 60 km (37 miles) away. But some 13,000 people visit the volcano during the summer months, when Askja is more accessible, according to data from the national park authority. Often, tourists climb down into Viti crater to swim in the Instagram-worthy lake, despite signs that warn them about acidic water, which can irritate skin, and falling rock.
After a week of field work at and around Askja last August, the volcanologists gathered park rangers and local police and lodging staff in a nearby ranger hut one evening for a safety briefing on the state of the volcano.
Related content
In the Andes, mountain guides bear witness to shrinking glaciers
Climate change thaws world‚Äôs northernmost research station
Iceland builds defences for a future of increased volcanic activity
Iceland volcano erupts near town after weeks of quake activity
Parks hovered over her laptop displaying a series of charts and graphs on her most recent findings that showed Askja was still inflating. Sigmundsson, standing in the doorway, explained to the rangers and police that predicting eruptions is an imprecise science; they may not necessarily have weeks of seismic activity as a warning of an imminent eruption. ‚ÄúThe time scale can be quite short,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúHours, maybe ‚Ä¶ You would want to have a plan if something happens to quickly evacuate the caldera.‚Äù
One of the greatest risks to tourists is a phreatic explosion ‚Äî a blast of hot steam, ash and rocks that comes with little warning. That‚Äôs what formed Viti crater nearly 150 years ago. In 2019, a phreatic explosion at New Zealand‚Äôs White Island volcano killed 22 people who were visiting the island at the time.
At Sigmundsson‚Äôs words, the room fell quiet. One of the police officers then piped up with a plan: ‚ÄúWe just have to hope that it blows up in the middle of the winter.‚Äù
By Gloria Dickie
Graphics: Sam Hart and Anurag Rao
Photography: Stoyan Nenov
Video: Kuba Stƒô≈ºycki
Video production: Lauren Cook, Holly Murtha and M√≠a Womersley
Photo editing: Maye-E Wong and Eve Watling
Art direction: Eve Watling
Visual editing: Feilding Cage
Edited by Katy Daigle and John Blanton
Amid Israel‚Äôs military assault, the world‚Äôs hunger monitor has struggled to access key data to determine whether conditions in Gaza constitute a famine.
OnlyFans is among the world's most successful social media platforms ‚Äì but a secretive one. Reuters traces its journey from an obscure, porn-free site to an adults-only phenomenon turbocharged by erotic performers and influencers.
It‚Äôs a simple but brutal equation: The number of people going hungry or otherwise struggling around the world is rising, while the amount of money the world‚Äôs wealthiest nations are contributing toward helping them is dropping.
Multiple OnlyFans accounts featured suspected child sex abuse, investigator reports.
Reuters, the news and media division of Thomson Reuters, is the world‚Äôs largest multimedia news provider, reaching billions of people worldwide every day. Reuters provides business, financial, national and international news to professionals via desktop terminals, the world's media organizations, industry events and directly to consumers.

item.title: 'Sustainable' logging operations are clear-cutting Canada‚Äôs climate-fighting forests
item.description:
Nonprofit environment watchdogs put their stamps of approval on countless wood products that get touted as responsibly produced. But Reuters found that the timber firms these groups certify are harvesting large swaths of Canada‚Äôs older forests, which are critical to containing global warming.
By CHRIS KIRKHAM , GRANT SMITH and JESSICA DINAPOLI Photographs by CHRIS HELGREN and DAVID JACKSON
Filed Sept. 7, 2024, 10 a.m. GMT
With its vast expanses of forest, Canada has the most ‚Äúcertified‚Äù sustainable timber operations of any nation, according to the nonprofit organizations that attest to the environmental soundness of logging practices.
Such forestry-standards groups were born in the 1990s out of rage¬Ýover tropical rainforest destruction. Today, they put their leafy seals of approval on toilet paper, two-by-fours and other wood and paper goods to assure eco-conscious consumers and investors they were responsibly produced.
Yet research shows Canadian forests have seen some of the world‚Äôs largest declines in ecologically critical primary and old-growth woodlands over the last two decades, even as sustainability-certification programs grew to include nearly all of Canada‚Äôs logging.
To track destruction of older woodlands in these certified zones, Reuters analyzed forestry data in Ontario, a major logging province. The analysis found that about 30% of the certified boreal forests harvested from 2016 to 2020 were at least 100 years old. That resulted in the loss of 377 square miles of these older forests, an area the size¬Ýof New York City and Washington D.C. combined, the analysis found.
Canada‚Äôs forests ‚Äì accounting for 9% of the world‚Äôs total ‚Äì are considered critical to containing global warming. Environmental advocates have long pushed to end logging in primary or old-growth forests, which soak up far more climate-damaging carbon than logged-and-replanted areas. Primary forests are those that show no sign of previous harvesting. They can include old-growth areas ‚Äì some with trees hundreds or thousands of years old ‚Äì but also relatively newer woodlands that, for instance, might have regrown after wildfires.
Forest-certification nonprofits have chosen to allow logging of older forests through a host of concessions to industry. The harvesting of such areas in Ontario came despite the fact that 94% of the province‚Äôs managed forests are certified by one of the two dominant environmental-certification¬Ýorganizations in Canada, the analysis found. Reuters analyzed satellite-derived logging data, government forest-age estimates and forest-certification maps to estimate the harvest of forests at least 100 years old in Ontario‚Äôs certified zones.
‚ÄúWhy the heck are they allowing logging ‚Äì certified logging ‚Äì in primary forests that are over 100 years old?‚Äù asked Dominick DellaSala, a conservation biologist with environmental group Wild Heritage who studies Canadian logging impacts.¬Ý‚ÄúFor Canada to claim that it‚Äôs doing sustainable management, it‚Äôs laughable. To put a certification seal of approval on it is more alarming.‚Äù
The rapid loss of older Canadian forests¬Ýhighlights the flaws of certification programs that have come under heavy influence of the logging and forest-products¬Ýindustries, a Reuters investigation has found. The damage has come under the watch of the Forest Stewardship Council (FSC), the world‚Äôs first such certification organization, founded in 1993 with environmentalist support; and the Sustainable Forestry Initiative (SFI), a rival founded by a timber¬Ýand forest-products¬Ýtrade group the following year.
This account is based on the Reuters analysis of Ontario forests, a review of hundreds of pages of FSC and SFI audits, along with policy and strategy documents, and interviews with 20 current or former FSC employees or members and more than a half-dozen¬Ýresearchers who study the environmental effects of Canadian logging.
In a statement, FSC said it has not wavered from its original commitment to responsible forest¬Ýmanagement and¬Ýthat its certification standards are ‚Äúrobust and credible.‚Äù SFI said its standards are ‚Äústrong and continuously improving‚Äù and that its certification has become a ‚Äúhighly trusted solution‚Äù to the growing demand for products from sustainably managed forests.
Neither organization commented on the Reuters analysis or on whether they considered harvesting large sections of century-old¬Ýforests to be sustainable.
The FSC and SFI certify logging companies‚Äô practices in specific forests and examine consumer-product supply chains. Their seals of approval ‚Äì a leaf insignia¬Ýfor SFI, and a tree with a checkmark for the¬ÝFSC ‚Äì have become essential to timber and forest-products¬Ýfirms amid rising pressure for environmental stewardship.
But these companies hold immense leverage over the big forest-certification nonprofits, which depend heavily on the industry for funding through certification fees, Reuters found. And since its inception, the FSC has watered down its forestry standards in response to the competitive threat posed by SFI and other industry-friendly certifiers, according to environmentalists and more than a dozen current and former FSC staffers and members, who advise the organization on policy and strategy.
Companies are free to choose which certifier to use, allowing them to avoid those with stricter standards and giving them clout to lobby all certifiers for permissive policies, said the FSC staffers and members.
‚ÄúIt‚Äôs easy to pull the wool over people‚Äôs eyes about what is good forestry.‚Äù
Widespread certification of British Columbia timber operations over the past two decades hasn‚Äôt stopped the disappearance of more than half of the province‚Äôs old-growth woodlands over that period. Logging caused the vast majority of the declines in the biggest old-growth trees storing the most carbon, according to one 2021 study in the Canadian Journal of Forest Research and another last year in the journal Frontiers in Forests and Global Change.¬ÝStudies in 2009 and 2017 examined areas of Quebec woodlands and found¬Ýsections¬Ýof forests dominated by trees more than a century old had dwindled to between 13% and 28% of the forest amid heavy logging. Without logging, these older sections would account for between 40% and 68%¬Ýof these woodlands, the researchers estimated.
Herb Hammond, a veteran forest ecologist,¬Ýran a British Columbia nonprofit¬Ýorganization that conducted some of Canada‚Äôs first FSC audits in the late 1990s.¬ÝHe later left the organization, frustrated with what he described as¬Ýtoo many compromises with industry.
‚ÄúIt‚Äôs easy to pull the wool over people‚Äôs eyes about what is good forestry,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúCertification has turned out to be a bit of a dog‚Äôs breakfast. It doesn‚Äôt really mean anything.‚Äù
A ‚Äòchess move‚Äô
Forestry certification has become ubiquitous in the global forest-products¬Ýtrade, helping companies such as Procter & Gamble, Starbucks and Penguin Random House appeal to eco-conscious consumers and investors. Those three companies declined to comment.
The certifying trend started in the 1990s when environmental organizations including Greenpeace, Friends of the Earth and the World Wildlife Fund helped launch the FSC after failing to secure forest-conservation pledges from governments worldwide. They hoped to incentivize companies instead with a market-driven system that branded goods as sustainable, stimulating demand from discerning buyers.¬ÝThe FSC was founded in 1993 with a membership of business, environmental and community representatives.
Still, many companies were wary of aligning with environmentalists. The following year, the American Forest &¬ÝPaper Association, a trade-group, started the SFI as an industry-friendly alternative. The trade association said its discussions about sustainable forestry began earlier, in 1990, and included input from academics and conservation groups.
Competition from the industry-backed SFI forced the FSC to reckon with how to maintain rigorous forestry standards while recruiting companies to certify, 10¬Ýcurrent and former FSC members said. A 2002 FSC management report highlighted the need to ‚Äúrapidly increase the supply of certified timber‚Äù or risk losing out to ‚Äúan ever-increasing number of competing certification schemes.‚Äù
The FSC launched an internal push to boost its market share that led to compromises with industry and weaker harvesting restrictions, according to FSC documents and the FSC members.
Weakening FSC standards didn‚Äôt stop the SFI‚Äôs growth, however. The FSC certified about 46 million hectares of Canadian forests¬Ýat the end of 2023, less than half the SFI‚Äôs 119 million hectares, according to the Forest Products Association of Canada, an industry group. Globally, the FSC certifies 160 million hectares compared to 295 million hectares by the Programme for the Endorsement of Forest Certification¬Ý(PEFC). The PEFC is a global organization that oversees the SFI, which covers North America, and affiliated certifiers in other regions.
Both the FSC and the SFI largely survive on industry-paid fees. FSC International reported in 2022 that such fees accounted for 86% of its $58 million in annual revenue. The SFI derived 77% of its $12 million in revenue from such fees, according to its 2022 tax return.
Some environmental groups and advocates, while acknowledging the FSC‚Äôs shortcomings, continue to view the organization as the best option among imperfect alternatives. Jen Skene, a policy director at the Natural Resources Defense Council, said FSC certification represents a minimum standard.
‚ÄúFSC is the most credible certification system out there,‚Äù she said, while adding that it should be viewed as ‚Äúa floor, not a ceiling‚Äù for sustainability standards.
FSC told Reuters it had not weakened standards in response to SFI competition. Instead, FSC said, the rivalry has prompted it to ‚Äúimprove and refine‚Äù its certification process to ensure it remains ‚Äúthe gold standard for responsible forestry.‚Äù
SFI said competition among certifiers doesn‚Äôt ‚Äúexert a downward pressure on standards‚Äù but rather promotes continuous improvement. The PEFC said it allows regional groups including the SFI to develop their own standards, which the PEFC said¬Ý‚Äúcontributes to long-term commitment to sustainable forest management¬Ýpractices.‚Äù
Though some corporations favor FSC-certified wood, few customers know the difference among certifying groups and their labels.
Peter Wood, a forestry lecturer at the University of British Columbia who has served on FSC-rulemaking committees, called the SFI‚Äôs creation a ‚Äúchess move.‚Äù
The industry ‚Äúwanted to take the power away from FSC, and it worked,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúNow, everything is certified.‚Äù
Race to the bottom
FSC‚Äôs early standards emphasized the need to protect primary and old-growth forests. One pivotal provision read: ‚ÄúPrimary forests ‚Ä¶ shall be conserved. Such areas shall not be replaced by tree plantations or other land uses.‚Äù
But companies complained the policy was too restrictive and difficult to enforce, said Grant Rosoman, a Greenpeace forests advisor and former FSC International board member.
FSC members spent years debating policy changes and in 1999 removed requirements to conserve primary forests. Instead, the FSC adopted a more subjective requirement to protect forests with ‚Äúhigh conservation value,‚Äù based on a complex matrix of environmental, economic¬Ýand cultural characteristics.
That vague language, still in effect, gives companies broad influence over which forests qualify for protection. It has also spawned an industry of consultants ‚Äì hired and paid¬Ýby forest-products firms¬Ý‚Äì to perform studies determining which woodlands¬Ýhave high conservation value, according to FSC audits and six current and former FSC members.
Rosoman of Greenpeace was among the FSC‚Äôs¬Ýmembers¬Ýwho approved the language at the time. He now regrets it, believing its subjectivity enabled destruction of critical forests. ‚ÄúThe continued logging of primary forests and old-growth forests was never resolved,‚Äù he said.
FSC acknowledged that its rules allow certified logging in such areas but said the high conservation value designation aims to ensure such harvesting is ‚Äúconducted with the highest level of scrutiny and responsibility.‚Äù
In another major concession, FSC in 2004 introduced the ‚ÄúFSC Mix‚Äù system, which created a new label for products containing up to 30% wood¬Ýfrom non-certified sources.
The move came after pressure from pulp-and-paper companies including Klabin of Brazil, SCA of Sweden and Mondi of South Africa, as well as book publishers and furniture manufacturers, said Rosoman, who participated in the negotiations. Mondi did not comment. SCA said it could not address its role at the time because the company has since been split into two firms. Klabin did not answer questions on whether the company influenced the FSC Mix rules. But¬Ýit said the label eased the logistical burden of separating wood from certified and non-certified sources, a view echoed by SCA.
FSC Mix has since become the certification group‚Äôs dominant label, accounting for more than three-fourths of the FSC-product trade, according to a 2017 FSC paper. The paper added that FSC Mix was¬Ý‚Äúthe main source of income for the operating costs of FSC.‚Äù
‚ÄúI just wanted to turn away from the whole project, and warn people: ‚ÄòDon‚Äôt trust it.‚Äô‚Äù
The FSC told Reuters it doesn‚Äôt know what percentage of FSC-certified products use the Mix label today. The label, it said, helps companies ‚Äútransition to more sustainable practices.‚Äù
FSC Mix rules give companies wide latitude to use the label.¬ÝSome consumer-products firms¬Ýare allowed to put the Mix label on products that contain no FSC-certified material at all because the FSC gives them credit for certified content in other products they sell.
The SFI also offers a label ‚Äì ‚ÄúSFI Certified Sourcing‚Äù ‚Äì that makes no guarantees that products contain any wood from certified forests, so¬Ýlong as companies meet certain other conditions.
Phil Guillery, a former FSC United States board member and supply chain integrity director, said allowing uncertified wood into the FSC system brought many more timber and forest-products¬Ýcompanies into the organization and gave them more clout.
‚ÄúThey understood and learned about the politics of FSC, and they became very powerful,‚Äù he said.
Wood, the University of British Columbia lecturer, served on two FSC groups that starting in 2011 tried to overhaul what internal critics had called a weak system of company self-assessments to ensure their FSC Mix products did not contain wood from ‚Äúunacceptable sources,‚Äù such as illegally harvested forests. The rules changes took eight years in a process that was heavily influenced by industry, he said.
The FSC told Reuters the process resulted in a ‚Äúsubstantial strengthening‚Äù of rules governing non-certified wood.¬ÝWood had a different take, saying the endless deliberations did little to screen out problematic sources of timber. He called his involvement ‚Äúan awful experience.‚Äù
‚ÄúI just wanted to turn away from the whole project,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúand warn people: ‚ÄòDon‚Äôt trust it.‚Äô‚Äù
Certified forest devastation
Environmentalists criticize the FSC but generally take a harsher view of the SFI, citing its founding by an industry group and weaker forestry standards.
The SFI disputes that it serves only industry interests, telling Reuters its standards reflect input from a diverse group of collaborators including environmentalists on its board.
Environmental groups including the Sierra Club, Stand.earth and the Natural Resources Defense Council say the impact of the SFI‚Äôs industry-friendly approach is clear in British Columbia, where the organization has dominated certification.
The province, a showcase of Canada‚Äôs raw beauty and diverse ecosystems, has seen old-growth forests decline¬Ýby more than 50% over the last two decades, according to the 2021 and 2023 studies. A subset of ‚Äúhighly productive‚Äù old-growth woodlands ‚Äì forests with the largest¬Ýtrees storing the most carbon, and also the most attractive to logging companies ‚Äì has declined by an estimated 85%.
The SFI became the certifier of choice in British Columbia largely because industry perceived the FSC‚Äôs early rules as too onerous, said Karen Tam Wu, an FSC consultant during the 2000s.
The timber industry and Canada‚Äôs government share in the logging wealth. Canada‚Äôs forests are generally on public land, which means provincial governments get a cut of the revenue from every felled tree. In British Columbia, that totaled more than $7.3 billion¬Ýover the decade ending in March of this year, according to the province‚Äôs forest ministry.
British Columbia in 2020 announced a plan to protect its dwindling old-growth forests after years of public pressure. A year later, officials released maps showing at-risk areas where it called for a deferral of logging. But the government never barred logging in those zones, instead leaving it to industry discretion.
Some major firms chose instead to continue harvesting, including Vancouver-based Canfor Corp, a global timber-and-pulp producer.
Canfor in 2022 whacked about 3,700¬Ýacres of old-growth forest the government had recommended¬Ýfor deferral of logging, according to satellite imagery analysis from Stand.earth. The provincial government said earlier this year that more than 50,000 acres of old-growth forest had been harvested in areas it sought to protect.
BC‚Äôs Ministry of Forests said it is not seeking to end all old-growth logging and that harvesting in some areas is ‚Äúpossible and necessary‚Äù to support ‚Äúlocal, sustainable jobs‚Äù while protecting forests.
SFI certified Canfor‚Äôs vast western Canada operations in 2019, 2021, 2022¬Ýand again last year. None of the publicly released audit summaries ever mentioned the cutting of old-growth forests. Major auditing firm KPMG, which conducted the reviews, had no comment.
Nothing in SFI‚Äôs standards would have prevented¬Ýlogging of old-growth forests.
SFI said old-growth-forest harvesting in British Columbia is ‚Äúcontentious,‚Äù involving negotiations among governments, industry and indigenous communities. It said its standards require compliance with ‚Äúall applicable laws.‚Äù
Canfor said it is working with indigenous groups, communities and government to ‚Äúreview old-growth management and seek input into our proposed harvesting.‚Äù
‚ÄòLike printing money‚Äô
Logging companies‚Äô ability to choose their own watchdogs poses the biggest obstacle to promoting high sustainability standards, environmental advocates said.
The auditing structure all but guarantees logging companies can get certified, said Simon Counsell, who was an FSC founding member while with the nonprofit group Friends of the Earth. He‚Äôs now an FSC critic.
‚ÄúThere‚Äôs a clear, vested financial interest‚Äù for the auditor, because granting FSC certifications leads to more auditing opportunities, Counsell said. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs like printing money.‚Äù
The FSC said it avoids conflicts of interest by outsourcing reviews and certification to independent auditors who examine companies‚Äô forestry practices and are paid by the firms being certified. The companies, it said, pay a separate ‚Äúannual administration¬Ýfee‚Äù based on their forest-products revenue that goes to the FSC after being collected by the auditor.
In one example of industry influence over sustainability audits, a major Canadian timber company, Resolute Forest Products, defeated an effort in 2014 to strip its FSC certification in a western Ontario forest by suing and ultimately firing its auditor.
Resolute for years faced charges from scientists and environmentalists that its clear-cuts in the FSC-certified Black Spruce Forest had decimated habitat for threatened woodland caribou. As early as 2012, auditors at the Rainforest Alliance, a nonprofit hired by Resolute, found the timber company failed to meet FSC habitat-protection requirements. Another 2013 Rainforest Alliance audit examined complaints from environmental groups that Resolute‚Äôs logging ‚Äúwill lead to the extirpation of caribou from the Black Spruce Forest.‚Äù
Auditors suspended Resolute‚Äôs certification in January 2014, citing a failure to meet FSC forest-protection requirements.¬ÝIn May 2014, Resolute sued the Rainforest Alliance and its auditors, personally, calling their reviews flawed and biased. The company sought $400,000 in damages.¬ÝIt also asked for an injunction blocking the audit‚Äôs public release, which an Ontario court granted.¬ÝThe lawsuit noted that certification was ‚Äúcritical to Resolute‚Äôs business model.‚Äù
The lawsuit was settled in 2015, with the alliance agreeing to assign new auditors to redo Resolute‚Äôs negative review. The follow-up audit found Resolute met FSC standards and had resolved the issues from the earlier audit.
Chris Wedeles, one of the original auditors Resolute sued, said he was ‚Äúdisappointed‚Äù that the new auditors ‚Äúreviewed the same evidence and came to a different conclusion.‚Äù
The Rainforest Alliance reinstated Resolute‚Äôs certification.¬ÝResolute dumped the alliance anyway, moving its auditing business in 2016 to SAI Global, which has re-certified the company every year since.
After the settlement, Resolute‚Äôs then-CEO Richard Garneau told FSC‚Äôs international director¬Ýgeneral in a 2015 letter that the firm would pull out of FSC unless the certifier addressed the company‚Äôs complaints about burdensome FSC requirements. A top Resolute executive was elected to FSC Canada‚Äôs board in 2021 and continues to serve today.
Resolute did not answer questions about its forestry practices or its lawsuit but said it upholds ‚Äúthe highest standards‚Äù in forestry management.
SAI Global, Garneau and the Rainforest Alliance, which no longer conducts FSC forestry audits, declined to comment.
The FSC said it was not involved in the dispute between Resolute and its auditor and that it wasn‚Äôt influenced to change its standards by Garneau‚Äôs 2015 letter. FSC pointed to recent suspensions of certifications in Quebec as evidence of its commitment to protect caribou.
Meanwhile, problems with caribou in the Black Spruce Forest persist.
In 2020 and 2021, SAI Global auditors found that Resolute could not substantiate the effectiveness of its caribou-conservation plan. The auditors resolved the matter, however, after a Resolute consultant argued that logging would decline to a level that could sustain caribou populations ‚Äì though not until 2039.
Bulldozing forests for oil
One of¬Ýthe world‚Äôs largest stretches of certified forests is in northern Alberta, where the FSC has signed off on the logging practices of Alberta-Pacific Forest Industries Inc.
Over the last two decades, about 878,000 acres of these woodlands, an area more than twice the size of¬ÝLos Angeles, have been set aside to make way for oil companies to operate open-pit mines, drilling sites and pipelines in Canada‚Äôs oil¬Ýsands. The oil exploration involves clear-cutting and bulldozing the forest. Some environmentalists consider it one of the world's most destructive industrial projects.
Alberta-Pacific Forest Industries holds logging rights to the forest, which includes old-growth woodlands, according to company disclosures and environmental studies. A clause in the company‚Äôs agreement with Alberta allows regulators to allocate chunks of the woods for oil-and-gas development.
When that happens, the FSC allows Alberta-Pacific to do a carve-out: removing the FSC certification from the land marked for oil development, while retaining certification for the surrounding forest. The arrangement has allowed Alberta-Pacific to retain certification in the area since 2005 despite the oil-related destruction.
FSC said it encourages certified firms to ‚Äúengage in dialogue‚Äù and use their influence to affect land-use decisions such as oil-and-gas development. But the organization said such decisions are ‚Äúoutside of FSC‚Äôs direct certification scope‚Äù and are governed by provincial and national laws.
Alberta-Pacific said it is proud to have been FSC-certified since 2005 and that it has a goal of ‚Äúmaintaining biodiversity and other forest values.‚Äù It said FSC‚Äôs policies allow carve-outs for oil development because the resulting environmental impacts are ‚Äúbeyond the full control‚Äù of Alberta-Pacific.
Alberta-Pacific makes money from the oil¬Ýdevelopment: Under an agreement with Alberta, it receives compensation from oil-and-gas firms for the destroyed forests. It can also sell wood from forests cleared for oil mining under the ‚ÄúFSC Mix‚Äù label, FSC audits show.
Some of the oil is extracted through surface mining, a process that requires the forest to be bulldozed and stripped of vegetation and soil to make way for pits that can be hundreds of feet deep.
‚ÄúThe mining is totally unsustainable,‚Äù said Barry Robinson, an Alberta environmental attorney who has specialized in oil-and-gas issues. ‚ÄúIt will be generations before it ever grows trees again.‚Äù
To estimate the amount of Ontario primary and old-growth forest harvested in areas with sustainable certification,¬ÝReuters analyzed satellite-derived logging data from the National Terrestrial Ecosystem Monitoring System for Canada , forest-stand attribute data from Ontario‚Äôs Forest Resources Inventory and forest-certification maps from the Forest Products Association of Canada .
The news organization focused on the province‚Äôs productive boreal forest stands within Ontario‚Äôs forest management units, as defined in the Forest Resources Inventory, and calculated ages in 2016 through 2020 based on the last-known age of the stand.
Reuters used forest certification maps from 2016 through 2020 to determine how much of the annual harvest was in these areas. Reuters then combined the certified forests harvest data with boreal forest stands to estimate the harvests by age in those areas.
Reuters consulted with nine experts in either forestry or geographic information systems in developing and reviewing its methodology.
Related content
How a US health agency became a shield for polluters
The next pandemic may come from bats. Reuters investigates where.
The Musk industrial complex
reuters investigates
More Reuters investigations and long-form narratives

A Cut of the Action
By Chris Kirkham, Grant Smith and Jessica DiNapoli
Photography: Chris Helgren and David Jackson
Photo editing: Corinne Perkins
Graphics and art direction: John Emerson and Jillian Kumagai
Edited by Vanessa O‚ÄôConnell and Brian Thevenot
Amid Israel‚Äôs military assault, the world‚Äôs hunger monitor has struggled to access key data to determine whether conditions in Gaza constitute a famine.
OnlyFans is among the world's most successful social media platforms ‚Äì but a secretive one. Reuters traces its journey from an obscure, porn-free site to an adults-only phenomenon turbocharged by erotic performers and influencers.
It‚Äôs a simple but brutal equation: The number of people going hungry or otherwise struggling around the world is rising, while the amount of money the world‚Äôs wealthiest nations are contributing toward helping them is dropping.
Multiple OnlyFans accounts featured suspected child sex abuse, investigator reports.
Reuters, the news and media division of Thomson Reuters, is the world‚Äôs largest multimedia news provider, reaching billions of people worldwide every day. Reuters provides business, financial, national and international news to professionals via desktop terminals, the world's media organizations, industry events and directly to consumers.

item.title: Trump says he is not worried about Iran-backed attacks on US soil
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 11 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump said on Wednesday he was not worried about Iran-backed attacks on U.S. soil, as the ‚ÄãFederal Bureau of Investigation warned of Iranian drones potentially striking the U.S. ‚ÄåWest Coast, ABC News reported.
The U.S. and Israel carried out strikes on Iran nearly two weeks ago, launching the Gulf region into a war . Tehran has carried out retaliatory strikes in ‚Äãresponse to the U.S.-Israeli strikes that killed top Iranian officials, including ‚Äãthe country's supreme leader .
When asked on Wednesday if he was worried ‚ÅÝthat Iran may increase it retaliation to include strikes on U.S. soil, ‚ÄãTrump told reporters, "No, I'm not."
ABC News later reported that the FBI had warned ‚Äãpolice departments in California that Iran could retaliate for U.S. attacks by launching drones at the West Coast.
"We recently acquired information that as of early February 2026, Iran allegedly aspired ‚Äãto conduct a surprise attack using unmanned aerial vehicles from an unidentified ‚Äãvessel off the coast of the United State Homeland, specifically against unspecified targets in California, ‚Äåin ‚ÅÝthe event that the US conducted strikes against Iran,‚Äù the FBI wrote in an alert distributed at the end of February, according to ABC News.
"We have no additional information on the timing, method, target, or perpetrators of this ‚Äãalleged attack."
Spokespeople for ‚Äãthe FBI, Los ‚ÅÝAngeles Police Department, California governor and Los Angeles mayor did not immediately respond to requests for comments.
Reuters reported earlier ‚Äãthis month that Iran and its proxies could target the ‚ÄãU.S. ‚ÅÝwith attacks in response to U.S. strikes.
A threat assessment produced by the Office of Intelligence and Analysis at the Department of Homeland Security said Iran and its proxies "probably" ‚ÅÝpose ‚Äãa threat of targeted attacks on the United ‚ÄãStates, although a large-scale physical attack was unlikely.

item.title: US gasoline prices surpass $3.50 a gallon at the pumps as Iran war rages on
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 11 (Reuters) - The national average retail price of gasoline crossed $3.50 a gallon this week to hit its highest since May 2024, data from ‚Äãprice tracking services AAA and GasBuddy showed, as the Israel-U.S. war with ‚ÄåIran stokes supply concerns.
Disruptions to Middle Eastern oil exports through the Strait of Hormuz have caused a surge in fuel prices across the globe that is threatening to pinch consumers' ‚Äãwallets and derail global economic activity. That could pose the biggest risk ‚Äãfor U.S. President Donald Trump and his Republican Party in the ‚ÅÝMidterm elections in November, especially as a major driver of Trump's re-election in ‚Äã2024 was his vow to lower energy costs.
"Geopolitical shockwaves don't take months to ‚Äãhit your wallet. They take days," said William Stern, chief executive officer at U.S.-based small business lender Cardiff. "You feel the exact squeeze the second you fill up your car just to ‚Äãtake the kids to practice."
U.S. average retail gasoline prices have jumped nearly 60 ‚Äãcents since Trump's decision to join Israel in attacking Iran on February 28 and stood at $3.58 ‚Äåa ‚ÅÝgallon on Wednesday.
Even more increases are likely to follow in the days ahead as more ships were hit in the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday and as the United States undergoes a transition to summer-grade gasoline sales, which burns cleaner but ‚Äãis costlier to produce.
Spot ‚Äãand wholesale gasoline ‚ÅÝprices were registering double-digit increases on Wednesday, said Denton Cinquegrana, chief oil analyst at Oil Price Information Service.
Wholesale price changes ‚Äãare typically reflected at the pumps the next day.
Crude oil ‚Äãprices , , the ‚ÅÝbiggest component in fuel costs, were also on the rise on Wednesday despite a proposal by the Paris-based International Energy Agency to release a record 400 million barrels ‚ÅÝof ‚Äãoil from strategic stockpiles.
The IEA's announcement on the release ‚Äãraised more questions than answers as the group did not announce who would release how much oil ‚Äãand when, Cinquegrana said.

item.title: Canada looks¬Ýfor ways to increase oil production to curb price spikes from Iran war
item.description:
CALGARY, March 11 (Reuters) - Canada will look at ways to increase its crude production to ‚Äãhelp global efforts to stabilize oil ‚Äåprices in the face of the Iran war, Natural Resources Minister Tim Hodgson said Wednesday.
The ‚ÄãCanadian government is talking to the ‚Äãcountry's oil producers about delaying planned ‚ÅÝmaintenance projects at oil sands facilities ‚Äãin order to temporarily increase output, Hodgson ‚Äãtold reporters in Ottawa. It is also asking Canadian refineries that are using imported oil to ‚Äãuse more domestic oil, in order ‚Äãto free up supply in other regions
The International Energy ‚ÄåAgency ‚ÅÝon Wednesday agreed to release 400 million barrels of oil, the largest such move in its history, to try to ‚Äãrein in ‚Äãcrude ‚ÅÝprices that have soared due to supply shocks from the U.S.-Israeli ‚Äãwar with Iran.
Canada ‚Äî the world's ‚Äãfourth ‚ÅÝlargest oil producer ‚Äî is a member of the IEA, but does not have its ‚ÅÝown ‚Äãstrategic petroleum reserve because ‚Äãit is a net exporter of oil.

item.title: India tells consumers not to panic over gas shortages and save energy
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 11 (Reuters) - India on Wednesday asked consumers not to panic about gas supplies and conserve energy wherever possible, as the world's ‚Äãsecond-largest importer of liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) struggles to meet domestic demand.
The federal ‚Äågovernment has invoked emergency powers ordering refiners to maximise production of LPG and cut sales to industry to avoid a shortage for its some 333 million homes with LPG connection.
The government's measures ‚Äãhave increased local LPG output by 25%, said Sujata Sharma, a joint ‚Äãsecretary in the federal oil ministry.
Despite the steps, restaurants, hotels, and ‚ÅÝindustries across sectors are beginning to feel the strain from the tighter supplies of ‚ÄãLPG, mainly used as cooking fuel.
"Panic booking (of LPG cylinders) and hoarding behaviour have been ‚Äãdriven by misinformation," Sharma said.
India's crude oil, LPG, and Liquefied natural gas (LNG) supplies have been disrupted due to global shipping constraints after the U.S. and Israel's war with Iran halted traffic ‚Äãthrough the Gulf and the Strait of Hormuz .
India consumed 33.15 million metric tons of ‚Äãcooking gas last year, with imports accounting for about 60% of demand. About 90% of those ‚Äåimports ‚ÅÝcame from the Middle East.
India also imports about half of its 190 million standard cubic metres per day (mscmd) of gas consumption.
Sharma said LNG imports of 47.4 mmscmd have been hit due to supply constraints and force majeure by its top supplier Qatar .
India ‚Äãhas begun diverting gas supplies ‚Äãfrom non-priority sectors ‚ÅÝto key users.
India is securing crude and LNG from alternative sources with two LNG cargoes already on their way, Sharma said.
Indian ‚Äãrefiners have bought millions of barrels of Russian oil floating on ‚Äãthe high ‚ÅÝseas after Washington granted a 30-day sanctions waiver.
Most Indian refineries are operating at full capacity, she said.
"The Middle East conflict has posed challenges for the world, but India's fuel ‚ÅÝdemand ‚Äãis being met. The Government is continuously monitoring the ‚Äãglobal situation and taking necessary steps to ensure uninterrupted fuel supplies and protect households and priority sectors," ‚Äãshe added.

item.title: Iraq asks KRG to help pipe crude oil to Turkey, sources say
item.description:
BAGHDAD, March 11 (Reuters) - Iraq has asked the Kurdistan Regional Government (KRG) if it would pump at least 100,000 barrels of crude per day from ‚Äãits state-managed Kirkuk oilfields to Turkey's Ceyhan port, two oil officials familiar with ‚Äåthe matter told Reuters.
Iraq's oil ministry sent a letter to the KRG with the request early last week, the officials said.
The Kurdistan Pipeline Company operates Iraqi Kurdistan's main oil pipeline, which transports crude from ‚Äãnorthern Iraq to a border connection point with the Iraq‚ÄìTurkey pipeline.
Shipments could gradually increase ‚Äãdepending on availability, and Baghdad would pay the transit fees, the officials ‚ÅÝsaid.
The officials said the step was taken to mitigate the huge losses to Iraq‚Äôs ‚Äãoil revenues caused by the halt of southern crude exports.
They added that the KRG had not ‚Äãyet responded to the request. However, speaking to Reuters, a senior KRG official said any decision to grant use of the pipeline would come with conditions.
"No drop of oil will pass through the pipeline unless ‚ÄãBaghdad gives the KRG immediate dollar relief for imports and security guarantees for international ‚Äãoil companies to restart production (in Kurdistan)," said the official who asked not to be named due to the ‚Äåsensitivity ‚ÅÝof the matter.
Some energy companies operating in Iraqi Kurdistan shut oil and gas production as a precaution as the U.S. and Israel launched strikes on neighbouring Iran.
Oil production from Iraq's main southern oilfields, where most of its oil is produced and exported, has fallen by 70% to ‚Äãjust 1.3 million from ‚Äã4.3 million bpd, ‚ÅÝsources said on March 8, as the country is unable to export via the Gulf due to the Iran war.
Iraq's northern Kirkuk oilfields, ‚Äãmeanwhile, are currently producing around 350,000 bpd, with all of it ‚Äãdiverted to ‚ÅÝdomestic refineries in the north, including the country's largest, the Baiji refinery.
The oil ministry would send crude from its southern oilfields to Baiji to compensate for the Kirkuk oil being sent to ‚ÅÝCeyhan, ‚Äãone oil ministry source said.
Falling oil production and lower ‚Äãexports are likely to tighten Iraq‚Äôs already fragile fiscal situation, as the government relies on crude income for nearly ‚Äãall of its budget.

item.title: Sea drones target oil tankers in the Middle East as conflict risks widen
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 (Reuters) - Naval drones have been used in at least two attacks on oil ‚Äãtankers in the Gulf region since war erupted between the U.S., Israel and Iran , according to maritime authorities and analysts, demonstrating ‚Äåa dangerous new threat in the key shipping lane.
The emergence of the use of explosive-laden unmanned surface vessels, which Ukraine has used with great effect in its war with Russia, comes as Iran threatens to block oil shipments from transiting the key Strait of Hormuz , the chokepoint for one-fifth of the world's oil.
The first sea-based ‚Äãdrone attack was on March 1 when a crude oil tanker, the MKD VYOM, was hit about 44 nautical miles off ‚ÄãOman, killing one crew member, Britain‚Äôs maritime agency said. The United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO) agency said ‚ÅÝin a public advisory that an unmanned surface vessel struck the Marshall Islands-flagged tanker just above the waterline, triggering an explosion and fire ‚Äãin the engine room.
Days later, a small vessel struck the Bahamas-flagged crude oil tanker Sonangol Namibe while anchored near Iraq‚Äôs Khor al Zubair port, according ‚Äãto U.S. representative company Sonangol Marine Services. The company said its 23 crew members were safe, and did not comment on what kind of vessel struck the vessel, citing an ongoing investigation.
Footage of the attack posted online shows a small speedboat-shaped object racing toward the side of the ship before slamming into it, ‚Äãtriggering an explosion that sent plumes of thick smoke into the air. The vessel in the video appears to be a naval drone ‚Äãthat exploded on impact, according to two UK-based maritime specialists, Sidharth Kaushal, a research fellow at think tank RUSI, and Robert Peters of maritime security ‚Äåfirm Ambrey.
Peters said he believes Iran was responsible for the Sonangol Namibe attack, noting that Tehran has previously showcased its naval drone capabilities at military displays. He pointed to the timing of the attack, which came a day after Iran‚Äôs Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) said it had struck an American oil tanker in the northern Persian Gulf, setting it ablaze, according to Iran‚Äôs semi‚Äëofficial Mehr news agency. The IRGC ‚Äãsaid ‚Äúmissiles‚Äù were used in that attack, ‚ÄãMehr reported.
Iran has said the ‚ÅÝworld should be ready for oil at $200 a barrel as its forces hit merchant ships on Wednesday, and as shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has come to a near-standstill.
Reuters was unable to determine who ‚Äãwas responsible for the two attacks. The Iranian mission to the UN in New York didn‚Äôt ‚Äãimmediately respond to a ‚ÅÝrequest for comment.
If Iran‚Äôs involvement is confirmed, it would be the first known instance of the Islamic Republic directly using naval drones to attack commercial vessels, according to Peters. Iran-aligned Houthi militants from Yemen have previously used drone boats against commercial ships in the Red Sea.
Peters said sea drones can ‚ÅÝcarry more ‚Äãexplosives than aerial ones, potentially rivaling the payload of ballistic missiles. While aerial ‚Äãdrones can kill crew members from above, sea drones can ‚Äúimmobilize ships if they hit the right place,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúIf your ship is immobilized, if they want to follow up ‚Äãyou are highly vulnerable.‚Äù

item.title: US may have struck Iranian girls' school after using outdated targeting data, sources say
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 11 (Reuters) - A strike on an Iranian girls' school that killed scores of children may be the ‚Äåresult of U.S. use of outdated targeting data, two sources familiar with the matter told Reuters on Wednesday, providing new details about what would rank among the worst cases of civilian casualties in decades of U.S. conflicts.
Reuters first reported on Thursday that an ‚Äãongoing, internal U.S. military investigation showed U.S. forces were likely responsible for the strike on the ‚Äãgirls' school in Minab.
Video surfaced that experts say appears to show a U.S. Tomahawk missile striking ‚ÅÝthe area. But exactly how the tragedy unfolded has remained unclear and the Pentagon has declined comment, saying ‚Äãthe investigation remains ongoing.
The strike, during the first day of U.S. and Israeli attacks on Iran, killed 150 ‚Äãstudents, according to Iran's ambassador to the U.N. in Geneva, Ali Bahreini.
According to archived copies of the school's official website, the school is adjacent to a compound operated by the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, the military force that reports to Iran's supreme leader.
One ‚Äãof the sources, who spoke on the condition of anonymity, said officials responsible for creating targeting packages appeared ‚Äãto have used out-of-date intelligence. The second source confirmed that out-of-date intelligence appears to have been used.
In response to a request for ‚Äåcomment, ‚ÅÝthe Pentagon said that "the incident is under investigation." The possible use of outdated targeting data was first reported by the New York Times earlier on Wednesday.
It is unclear how old data ended up being used for the strike and what, if any other factors, might be responsible for the error.
The investigation is still ongoing and it ‚Äãis not clear when a ‚Äãfinal conclusion will be ‚ÅÝreached.
Since Reuters' report on the U.S. likely being responsible for the strike, U.S. President Donald Trump has claimed without evidence that Iran was responsible. But since then, ‚Äãhe has said he does not know enough about the strike, that an investigation ‚Äãis ongoing and ‚ÅÝthat he will accept the results of the inquiry.
U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and other U.S. officials have stressed that the United States would not deliberately target civilians.
Deliberately attacking a school or hospital or any other civilian structure would ‚ÅÝlikely be ‚Äãa war crime under international humanitarian law.
Images of the girls' funeral ‚Äãwere shown on Iranian state television last week. Their small coffins were draped with Iranian flags and passed from a truck across a ‚Äãlarge crowd towards the grave site.

item.title: Oil tanker seized by US in relation to Venezuela set to transfer crude to smaller vessel
item.description:
HOUSTON, March 11 (Reuters) - An oil tanker seized by the United States for carrying Venezuelan oil is set to transfer the crude to a ‚Äãsmaller vessel off the coast of Texas that can take it ‚Äåto shore, ship tracking data showed on Wednesday.
The United States last month sought to take ownership of the supertanker Skipper and the roughly 1.8 million barrels of Venezuelan oil it ‚Äãwas carrying when it was seized in December. It was the first ‚Äãoil tanker seized as part of a U.S. strategy of ‚ÅÝincreased pressure against Nicolas Maduro before the Venezuelan president was captured in January.
Medium-sized ‚Äãvessel PVT Poseidon was headed toward the Skipper to transfer a part of ‚Äãthe crude, ship tracking data on LSEG and Kpler showed, and is then expected to navigate to shore. The Skipper is too large to transit through the Houston Ship Channel ‚Äãand has been anchored outside of Galveston since its seizure.
The United States ‚Äãis seeking forfeiture of the tanker, a legal process in which the government permanently seizes ‚Äåa ‚ÅÝvessel and its cargo without compensation due to violations such as sanctions or smuggling.
U.S. forces have intercepted 10 tankers since December and released at least two of them to the interim Venezuelan government, according to Reuters analysis.
Oil from seized ships ‚Äãis typically sold, ‚Äãand the proceeds ‚ÅÝare directed to funds related to U.S. victims of state-sponsored terrorism.
The anchoring of seized Venezuela-linked tankers had raised concerns as ‚Äãmany of them were built more than 20 years ago, ‚Äãand also ‚ÅÝlack safety certification and adequate insurance. That means that if they have a collision or oil spill, establishing insurance claims or liability is very difficult to ‚ÅÝimpossible, according ‚Äãto shipping and insurance industry sources.
The age of ‚Äãthese tankers, which exceeds the industry standard of 15 years, also raises concern about potential environmental ‚Äãdamage, the sources said.

item.title: Trump administration asks Supreme Court to end Haitian protected status
item.description:
March 11 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump 's administration asked the U.S. Supreme Court on Wednesday to intervene in its effort to strip humanitarian deportation protections from more than 350,000 Haitians living in the United States despite persistent violence in Haiti that has displaced more than 1 million people.
The Justice ‚ÄãDepartment in an emergency request asked the court to lift a judge's decision that blocked the administration's move to end Temporary Protected Status, or TPS, for ‚ÄåHaitians. The judge found that the administration's action toward the Haitians likely was motivated in part by "racial animus."
Kristi Noem, a Trump appointee then serving as secretary of the U.S. Department of Homeland Security, determined in November 2025 that there were "no extraordinary and temporary conditions" in Haiti that would prevent Haitian migrants from returning to the Caribbean country. The U.S. State Department currently warns against travel to Haiti "due to kidnapping, crime, terrorist activity, civil unrest ‚Äãand limited healthcare."
MASS DEPORTATIONS The Republican president, pursuing a policy of mass deportations since returning to office in January 2025, has moved to strip certain migrants of temporary legal ‚Äãprotections previously provided to them by the U.S. government for humanitarian reasons, expanding the pool of possible deportees.
Under Trump, the Department of Homeland Security ‚ÅÝhas moved to end TPS status for about a dozen countries, saying it was always meant to be temporary.
Acting on a separate emergency request by the administration in October, the Supreme ‚ÄãCourt allowed the administration to terminate TPS for hundreds of thousands of Venezuelan migrants. The administration in February also asked the court to allow its effort to strip TPS status from about 6,100 Syrians living ‚Äãin the United States.
Temporary Protected Status is available to people whose home country has experienced a natural disaster, armed conflict or other extraordinary event. It provides eligible migrants with work authorization and temporary protection from deportation.
Haitians were first given TPS in 2010 under Democratic former President Barack Obama after a devastating earthquake struck their country.
The U.S. government repeatedly extended the status, most recently under Democratic former President Joe Biden's administration, which cited "simultaneous ‚Äãeconomic, security, political and health crises" in Haiti, fueled by gangs and a lack of a functioning government. That extension had given Haitians living in the United States protections through February ‚Äã3, 2026.
More than 1.4 million Haitians have been displaced by violence and instability, according to the International Organization for Migration.
Trump fired Noem on March 5 after months of controversy including the fatal shootings of two ‚ÄåU.S. citizens by ‚ÅÝfederal officers in Minneapolis and questions by lawmakers about a $220 million advertising contract for a campaign promoting her and her department. Noem's TPS decisions were not at issue in her dismissal.
U.S. District Judge Ana Reyes ruled , opens new tab on February 2 in a class-action lawsuit brought by Haitians seeking to stop the administration from exposing them to deportation by ending their TPS standing.
Reyes said in the ruling that Noem likely violated the procedures required to terminate the protected status of Haitian immigrants in the U.S. as well as the U.S. Constitution's Fifth Amendment guarantee of equal ‚Äãprotection under the law.
"Plaintiffs charge that Secretary Noem ‚Äãpreordained her termination decision and did so ‚ÅÝbecause of hostility to nonwhite immigrants. This seems substantially likely," Reyes wrote.
Referencing a December social media post by Noem, Reyes added, "Kristi Noem has a First Amendment right to call immigrants killers, leeches, entitlement junkies and any other inapt name she wants. Secretary Noem, however, is constrained by ‚Äãboth our Constitution and the (Administrative Procedure Act) to apply faithfully the facts to the law in implementing the TPS program."
A three-judge panel ‚Äãof the U.S. Court of ‚ÅÝAppeals for the District of Columbia Circuit on March 6 rejected the administration's bid to pause the ruling by Reyes. The D.C. Circuit, in a 2-1 decision, wrote that Haitians sent home would "be vulnerable to violence amid a 'collapsing rule of law' and lack access to life-sustaining medical care."
Trump's administration tried to curtail those protections in February 2025, when Noem moved to truncate the Biden-era extension so it would ‚ÅÝexpire last ‚ÄãAugust. After a federal judge in New York last July ruled that Noem lacked statutory authority to do so, her ‚Äãdepartment in November moved to terminate Haiti's TPS status.
Trump's administration has repeatedly turned to the Supreme Court's emergency docket to ask the justices to allow implementation of his policies that have been impeded by lower courts. The Supreme Court has sided ‚Äãwith the administration in most of these cases since Trump returned to the presidency.

item.title: US Senate Democrats push for answers on strike targeting Iran girls' school, civilian casualties
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 11 (Reuters) - Nearly every U.S. Senate Democrat signed a letter sent to U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth on Wednesday requesting a "swift investigation" of ‚Äãairstrikes on a girls' school in Iran that killed scores of children and any ‚Äåother potential U.S. military actions causing civilian harm.
Reuters reported on March 5 that U.S. military investigators believe it is likely that U.S. forces were responsible for the strike on the school on February 28, as U.S. and Israeli forces ‚Äãlaunched attacks on Iran.
"The results of this school attack are horrific. The majority of those ‚Äãkilled in the strikes were girls between the ages of 7 and 12 ‚ÅÝyears old. Neither the United States nor the Israeli Government has yet taken responsibility for this ‚Äãattack," the letter, signed by 46 senators, said.
The correspondence was signed by every member of the Senate ‚ÄãDemocratic caucus except John Fetterman of Pennsylvania, a senator known for outspoken opinions and occasional defiance of the party. His office did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
The letter asked for answers to a series of questions, ‚Äãincluding whether U.S. forces conducted the strikes, what steps the military has taken to prevent and mitigate ‚Äãcivilian harm and what role artificial intelligence tools have played in operations.
The Department of Defense did not immediately respond ‚Äåto ‚ÅÝa request for comment on the letter. Hegseth has vowed to restore a "warrior ethos" to the U.S. military and referred to rules of engagement, directives typically given to military forces during a conflict, as "stupid" in a recent press conference.
Iran's U.N. ambassador said on Tuesday the U.S.-Israeli strikes had killed more than ‚Äã1,300 civilians.
No Republicans signed the ‚Äãletter. Members of ‚ÅÝPresident Donald Trump's party, who hold a 53-seat majority in the Senate, have almost unanimously backed his strategy on Iran, with only a handful expressing doubt ‚Äãabout any aspect of the war.
The Democrats sent the letter a ‚Äãweek after ‚ÅÝSenate Republicans blocked a bipartisan resolution aiming to stop the air war and requiring that any hostilities against Iran be authorized by Congress. Every member of the Democratic caucus except Fetterman voted in favor of the ‚ÅÝresolution.
The ‚Äãlawmakers' latest comments came as they awaited a request the White ‚ÄãHouse is expected to make for more funding for the war. Several congressional aides have said they expect Trump to ask ‚Äãfor $50 billion, although others have said that estimate seemed low.

item.title: Chubb to serve as lead US insurer for Gulf shipping amid Iran war
item.description:
March 11 (Reuters) - Insurance giant Chubb (CB.BN) , opens new tab will be the lead partner on the U.S. International Development Finance Corporation's $20 billion Maritime Reinsurance Plan aimed at resuming ‚Äãcommercial shipping in the Gulf, the agency said on Wednesday.
The U.S.-Israeli ‚Äåconflict has widened sharply in recent days and paralyzed shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz , a major global chokepoint in the Gulf.
Iran said the world should be ‚Äãprepared for oil to hit $200 a barrel as its forces attacked ‚Äãmerchant ships on Wednesday in the blockaded Gulf. Meanwhile, U.S. ‚ÅÝPresident Donald Trump has repeatedly tried to reassure markets this week ‚Äãthat the campaign will end soon.
So far there has been no let-up ‚Äãon the ground and no sign ships can safely pass through the Strait of Hormuz, where about a fifth of the world's oil passes, raising the risk ‚Äãof the worst disruption to energy supplies since the oil shocks of ‚Äãthe 1970s.
Maritime insurance covers ships and cargo against risks such as accidents, piracy or ‚Äåconflict, ‚ÅÝwith shipowners paying premiums that rise as insurers assess the likelihood of losses.
War risk coverage is typically excluded from standard policies and must be purchased separately, often at sharply higher premiums for vessels sailing through ‚Äãconflict zones.
Without such coverage, ‚Äãships and ‚ÅÝcargo worth hundreds of millions of dollars would be exposed to losses from attacks or seizures, leaving owners ‚Äãand financiers vulnerable and deterring vessels from sailing through ‚Äãsuch waters.
The ‚ÅÝDFC said its reinsurance facility will insure losses up to roughly $20 billion on a rolling basis and insurance will initially focus on hull and ‚ÅÝcargo.
"Together, DFC ‚Äãand Chubb have identified several American insurance ‚Äãcompanies to provide reinsurance policies behind Chubb and alongside DFC to expand market capacity," the ‚Äãagency added.

item.title: Maersk redistributing ship fuel to ensure supplies as Iran war disrupts supply
item.description:
COPENHAGEN/LOS ANGELES, March 11 (Reuters) - Container shipping group Maersk (MAERSKb.CO) , opens new tab said on Wednesday it is redistributing fuel to ensure supplies for its vessels as the Iran war disrupts the flow and storage of maritime fuel in the ‚ÄãMiddle East, where the Danish carrier also has 10 ships stranded in the Gulf.
"We are proactively ‚Äãredistributing fuel to ensure vessels can continue to bunker where needed and keep ‚ÅÝour ocean network running without interruptions," a Maersk spokesperson said. Bunkering is the maritime industry's term for ‚Äãrefueling.
Drones struck oil storage facilities in Salalah port on Wednesday, a security firm and Oman TV said. ‚ÄãOman's state news agency, citing an energy ministry official, said there has been no disruption to the continuity of oil supplies or petroleum derivatives in the country.
Maersk separately said it paused all operations at the Port of Salalah on Wednesday ‚Äãuntil further notice in response "to an ongoing incident near the general cargo terminal." It did not ‚Äãprovide further detail.
Earlier this week, falling debris from an intercepted drone sparked a fire that damaged storage and disrupted operations ‚Äåat ‚ÅÝthe vital Fujairah ship-fueling hub in the United Arab Emirates.
U.S. and Israeli attacks on Iran have fueled hostilities that threaten one-fifth of the world's oil, which sails out of the region via the Strait of Hormuz - the globe's most important energy chokepoint.
While much of the world's attention has been focused on the ‚Äãrisk of attacks on ‚Äãoil tankers, some of ‚ÅÝthe roughly 100 container ships stuck in the Gulf have come under assault. They include the Japan-flagged ONE Majesty , which sustained minor damage on Wednesday from an ‚Äãunknown projectile 25 nautical miles (46 km) northwest of Ras Al Khaimah in the ‚ÄãUAE, two ‚ÅÝmaritime security firms said.
Its Japanese owner Mitsui O.S.K. Lines (9104.T) , opens new tab and a spokesperson for Ocean Network Express (ONE), its charterer, said the vessel was struck while at anchor in the Gulf and suffered minor damage above the waterline. ‚ÅÝAll crew ‚Äãare reported to be accounted for, the ONE spokesperson said.
Germany's ‚ÄãHapag-Lloyd (HLAG.DE) , opens new tab , Maersk's alliance partner, has a "single-digit" number stuck in the Strait of Hormuz, a spokesperson said.
MSC, the world's largest container carrier, ‚Äãdid not immediately respond to requests for comment.

item.title: Colombia and Venezuela to repair binational gas pipeline after years of inactivity
item.description:
BOGOTA, March 11 (Reuters) - Colombia and Venezuela have agreed ‚Äãto repair a damaged section ‚Äåof a binational pipeline that will allow Bogota to import natural gas from ‚Äãits neighbor, Colombia's energy ministry ‚Äãsaid on Wednesday.
The Antonio Ricaurte gas ‚ÅÝpipeline has been inactive for ‚Äãyears. A roadmap for re-establishing operations ‚Äãwas outlined in a meeting of Colombia's mining and energy ministries, environmental licensing authority ‚Äãand Venezuela's state oil company ‚ÄãPDVSA.
The announcement comes two days before Colombian ‚ÄåPresident ‚ÅÝGustavo Petro is slated to meet with Venezuelan Interim President Delcy Rodriguez.
PDVSA will carry out the repairs ‚Äãin compliance ‚Äãwith ‚ÅÝenvironmental regulations and will follow the pipeline's original route, ‚ÄãColombia's energy ministry said in ‚Äãa ‚ÅÝstatement.
The pipeline stretches 225 kilometers (140 mi) between both countries and has ‚ÅÝa ‚Äãtransport capacity of 500 ‚Äãmillion cubic feet of gas.

item.title: Mexico's lower house rejects President Sheinbaum's electoral reform
item.description:
MEXICO CITY, March 11 (Reuters) - An electoral reform proposed by ‚ÄãMexican President Claudia Sheinbaum was ‚Äårejected on Wednesday by lawmakers in the lower house of Congress who criticized ‚Äãthe bill as a move by ‚Äãthe ruling party to concentrate power.
The ‚ÅÝreform received 259 votes in ‚Äãfavor and 234 against, with one ‚Äãabstention. Despite the majority, the bill fell short of the supermajority required for constitutional changes ‚Äãbecause Sheinbaum's usual allies from ‚Äãthe Green Party (PVEM) and the Labor Party (PT) withheld ‚Äåtheir ‚ÅÝsupport.
Sheinbaum introduced the initiative in late February , arguing it would strengthen democracy, increase citizen participation and cut electoral costs ‚Äãby a ‚Äãquarter. ‚ÅÝThe proposal included a reduction in Senate seats and ‚Äãincreased oversight of electoral bodies.
Pablo ‚ÄãGomez, ‚ÅÝhead of the electoral reform commission, said in late February that Mexico's electoral ‚ÅÝsystem ‚Äãcosts reached $3.55 billion ‚Äãin 2024.

item.title: Guatemala appoints new election tribunal amid corruption fears
item.description:
GUATEMALA CITY, March 11 (Reuters) - Guatemala's opposition-controlled Congress elected new ‚Äãmembers to the Supreme Electoral Tribunal (TSE) late Tuesday, part of a sweeping judicial overhaul that ‚Äåinternational observers warn risks being hijacked by corruption networks.
The appointment of five titular and five alternate magistrates for the 2026-2032 term is a pivotal step in a series of leadership changes across the country's top courts and the prosecutor's office.
The ‚Äãnew TSE will oversee the 2027 general election to replace President Bernardo Arevalo, who won the presidency ‚Äãunexpectedly in 2023 on an anti-corruption agenda. He is barred from seeking re-election.
Arevalo, ‚ÅÝa social democrat, has framed these appointments as a chance to purge the justice system of corruption. ‚ÄãHowever, his agenda has clashed with the recent selection of controversial figures to the Constitutional Court (CC), the nation's ‚Äãhighest judicial authority.
Experts say the process is critical to restoring public trust, which has eroded since the 2019 shutdown of the UN-backed anti-impunity commission, CICIG.
"One of the biggest obstacles Guatemala faces is the co-optation of the judiciary... which has allowed ‚Äãorganized crime and corruption networks to permeate," Ana Maria Mendez, Central America director at the Washington Office ‚Äãon Latin America (WOLA), told Reuters.
Earlier this year, U.S. officials warned that the candidate lists for the TSE were vulnerable to ‚Äåinfluence ‚ÅÝfrom "criminal organizations and drug traffickers."
The stakes remain high as Arevalo prepares to select a new attorney general by May. Current chief Consuelo Porras, who has been sanctioned by the United States and the European Union for alleged corruption, is seeking a third term. Arevalo has described her bid for re-election as a "mockery of ‚Äãthe Guatemalan people."
Porras in 2023 ‚Äãwas at the center ‚ÅÝof what the U.S. and others claimed was an effort to prevent Arevalo from taking office.
A recent study by a local NGO found that over 93% of ‚Äãcriminal cases in Guatemala failed to reach an effective resolution in the 2024-2025 ‚Äãperiod. Meanwhile, ‚ÅÝthe UN Special Rapporteur on the independence of judges and lawyers, Margaret Satterthwaite, urged the government to bar any candidates facing credible allegations of human rights abuses or corruption.
The Constitutional Court has also faced scrutiny for rulings ‚ÅÝthat paralyzed ‚ÄãArevalo's Movimiento Semilla party. Recent re-appointments to the court include ‚ÄãDina Ochoa and Roberto Molina, both of whom have faced criticism from international observers regarding their impartiality and past ties to political ‚Äãand military elites.

item.title: Portugal warns foreign state-backed hackers targeting officials' and military messaging accounts
item.description:
LISBON, March 11 (Reuters) - Foreign state-backed hackers have launched a global cyber campaign to access the ‚ÄãWhatsApp and Signal accounts of government officials, ‚Äådiplomats and military personnel, the national intelligence service (SIS) warned on Wednesday.
In a rare statement, the SIS said ‚Äãthe hackers seek to trick users ‚Äãof these platforms "into sharing sensitive data, such ‚ÅÝas passwords", in order to gain access ‚Äãto individual and group chats and shared files.
"The ‚Äãtargets are government officials, diplomats, military personnel, and civil society members with access to privileged information from Portugal ‚Äãand allied countries," SIS said.
SIS did not ‚Äãidentify the foreign state behind the hackers, who are "exploiting ‚Äåpotential ‚ÅÝcareless use by individuals relying on the end-to-end encryption of the two applications," widely used by officials, military personnel, and executives.
It said the ‚Äãattacks "do not ‚Äãmean that ‚ÅÝWhatsApp or Signal have been compromised," but did not rule out ‚Äãthe possibility.
Two intelligence agencies in the ‚ÄãNetherlands ‚ÅÝsaid on Monday that Russian-backed hackers had also launched a global cyber campaign to gain ‚ÅÝaccess ‚Äãto both platforms.
SIS said it ‚Äãissued the alert also to help the public prepare for ‚Äãcyberattacks.

item.title: Pain at the pumps will ease in a month or two, billionaire Catsimatidis says
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 11 (Reuters) - Consumers will get relief from the rapid ‚Äãincreases in fuel prices at the pumps in a month or ‚Äåtwo, John Catsimatidis, the billionaire owner of a Pennsylvania refinery and New York supermarket chains, told Reuters in an interview on Tuesday.
Supply disruptions caused by the Israel-U.S. war with Iran have sent fuel ‚Äãprices skyrocketing, posing a major threat to U.S. President Donald Trump and his Republican Party ‚Äãahead of midterm elections in November.
"I believe prices will come back ‚ÅÝdown in the next month, worst case scenario, two months," said Catsimatidis, a ‚Äãmajor Republican donor, who said he thought the worst of the increases was over.
The ‚ÄãU.S. national average price of gasoline has gained nearly 60 cents since joint U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran started February 28, and stood at $3.58 a gallon on Wednesday, AAA data showed. Diesel prices have ‚Äãrisen more than $1, and Americans expect prices to rise over the next year, ‚Äãaccording to a Reuters/Ipsos poll that closed on Monday.
Catsimatidis, chairman and chief executive officer for ‚ÄãUnited Refining Co, ‚Äãsaid the crisis highlights ‚ÅÝa need for more investment in U.S. oil production and refining, but it will require stability in White House policy. Asked ‚Äãif he would consider upgrading or expanding United Refining's 70,000 ‚Äãbarrel-per-day refinery ‚ÅÝin Warren, Pennsylvania, he said: "Absolutely, yes."
The U.S. has not opened a major new refinery in nearly half a century, and energy experts questioned the need for one after Trump on Tuesday ‚ÅÝannounced the ‚Äãconstruction of a plant on the southern U.S. ‚Äãborder. U.S. refining economics are under pressure from the opening of mammoth new plants in Nigeria and elsewhere.

item.title: Brazil's Raizen secures $12.6 billion out-of-court debt restructuring deal
item.description:
SAO PAULO, March 11 (Reuters) - Brazilian sugar and ethanol producer Raizen (RAIZ4.SA) , opens new tab said on Wednesday it had reached an out-of-court agreement with creditors and bondholders to restructure approximately 65.1 billion reais ($12.61 billion) in debt obligations.
The joint venture between oil ‚Äãmajor Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab and Brazilian conglomerate Cosan (CSAN3.SA) , opens new tab had been in months-long talks seeking ways to strengthen ‚Äãits capital structure and tackle its significant debt burden.
Raizen said in a securities filing ‚ÅÝthat creditors holding 47% of its unsecured debt had already endorsed the plan. The company will ‚Äãhave 90 days to gain sufficient support for final approval, after which the new payment terms will ‚Äãapply to 100% of the covered claims.
Raizen's shares fell as much 17% in early trading on Wednesday before recovering some ground, though they remained volatile.
The world's largest sugar producer, Raizen had been facing mounting ‚Äãpressure due to a combination of elevated capital expenditures, unfavorable weather, and wildfires that damaged harvests ‚Äãand reduced cane-crushing volumes.
It signaled last week that it could pursue an out-of-court restructuring to resolve its debt crisis, which ‚Äåhad ‚ÅÝpreviously led the company to flag "significant uncertainty" about its ability to continue operations.
Raizen's reorganization is the largest ever out-of-court restructuring in Brazil, according to Luiz Fabiano Saragiotto, chairman of the board of TMA Brasil, the Brazilian chapter of the global non-profit focused on corporate restructuring.
Saragiotto said Raizen's move is "more a positive step ‚Äãtoward a definitive solution ‚Äãthan a concern about ‚ÅÝthe company's operations."
A Shell spokesperson said it supported the reorganization, adding it was necessary to address Raizen's significant financial challenges.
Shell has proposed injecting 3.5 billion reais ‚Äãto support Raizen.
Cosan said in a regulatory filing that the restructuring has no ‚Äãimpact on ‚ÅÝCosan's obligations, operations, capital structure, or financial position. Its own activities and commercial relationships remain unchanged, it said.
According to Raizen's Wednesday's filing, the restructuring plan could involve shareholder capitalization, the conversion of part of its outstanding ‚ÅÝcredits into ‚Äãequity, new debt issuances, and asset sales.
Raizen said the agreement ‚Äãdoes not affect obligations towards customers, suppliers, distributors or other commercial partners, and that operations would continue to run normally.
($1 = 5.1639 ‚Äãreais)

item.title: Trump on Iran: We won, but don't want to leave early
item.description:
HEBRON, Kentucky, March 11 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump said on ‚ÄåWednesday that "we won" the Iran war but that the United States will stay in ‚Äãthe fight to finish the ‚Äãjob.
"You never like to say too ‚ÅÝearly you won. We won," ‚ÄãTrump told a campaign-style rally in Hebron, Kentucky. "In ‚Äãthe first hour it was over."
He said the United States had knocked out 58 ‚ÄãIranian naval ships.
Trump has seesawed ‚Äãon Iran, crediting the U.S. military for significantly ‚Äådegrading ‚ÅÝIran's military but resisting a speedy end to the conflict.
"We don't want to leave early do we?" Trump ‚Äãsaid. "We ‚Äãgot to ‚ÅÝfinish the job."
He said the United States has "virtually destroyed ‚ÄãIran." He seemed to signal that ‚Äãthe ‚ÅÝUnited States would continue the fight for now.
"We don't want to go ‚ÅÝback ‚Äãevery two years," he ‚Äãsaid.

item.title: Trump's portrayal of migrants as criminals may incite hate crimes, UN racism body warns
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 11 (Reuters) - The United Nations Committee on the Elimination of Racial Discrimination on Wednesday warned about "derogatory ‚Äãand dehumanizing language" used by U.S. President Donald Trump when ‚Äåreferring to migrants.
It said it was deeply concerned not only about the language but also the dissemination of negative and harmful stereotypes targeting migrants, among others, by portraying ‚Äãthem as criminals or as a burden.
"Portraying them as criminals or ‚Äãas a burden, by politicians and influential public figures at ‚ÅÝthe highest level, particularly the President may incite racial discrimination and hate ‚Äãcrimes," the committee said.
The White House accused the U.N. of bias and ‚Äãsaid Trump had delivered on many of his campaign promises, including securing the U.S. border.
"No one cares what the biased United Nations‚Äô so-called ‚Äòexperts‚Äô think, because Americans are living in ‚Äãa safer, stronger country than ever before," White House spokeswoman Olivia Wales ‚Äãsaid when reached for comment.
Trump, a Republican, has sought to implement an aggressive immigration agenda ‚Äåsince ‚ÅÝreturning to the White House last year.
The campaign has resulted in increased immigration arrests and detentions . Trump's administration has also moved to strip legal status from hundreds of thousands of immigrants in the U.S. legally.
Democrats and activists have criticized ‚Äãthe Trump administration for ‚Äãthe way in ‚ÅÝwhich it has gone about carrying out that agenda, especially after two U.S. citizens were killed during federal immigration ‚Äãoperations in Minnesota earlier this year.
At least 675,000 people ‚Äãhave been ‚ÅÝdeported since January 2025, the U.N. committee said, adding that it was alarmed about reports of excessive use of force and violence during immigration enforcement operations.
It ‚ÅÝsaid ‚Äãit was also gravely concerned about reports that ‚Äãthe Trump administration was targeting people it perceived as migrants - a move that it described ‚Äãas increasing racial profiling.

item.title: Trading Day: Oil and yields up, up, and away
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 11 (Reuters) - Oil prices rose sharply on Wednesday despite a record release of global crude reserves, stoking inflation fears and lifting two-year Treasury yields to the highest since September. The weight on stocks was too much, and Wall Street ended mostly lower.
In my column today I sketch out ‚Äãwhy structurally higher oil prices are bad news for U.S. corporate earnings , as businesses and consumers face far higher direct and indirect ‚Äåenergy costs than they were budgeting for.
If you have more time to read, here are a few articles I recommend to help you make sense of what happened in markets today.
Today's Key Market Moves
Today's Talking Points
* Private credit strains deepen
Worries about the health of the $2 trillion private credit market continue to deepen. The latest red flags: JPMorgan reducing the value of some loans to ‚Äãprivate credit funds, and reports of Cliffwater 's flagship private credit fund capping redemptions.
Scarce or nonexistent liquidity, opaque pricing, limited transparency and spiking redemptions - this is how ‚Äãinvestors are increasingly viewing the sector. That may not be a wholly fair assessment, but right now the bar to convincing them otherwise is getting higher.
* Oil can't ‚Äåget no ‚ÅÝrelief
Oil prices spiked 5% on Wednesday, the same day the International Energy Agency agreed to release 400 million barrels of reserves in response to the crisis, the largest such move in its history.
You can look at oil's reaction in two ways. It was equivalent to 'buy the rumor, sell the fact', as crude plunged the day before when this move was flagged. Or, it shows supply fears run much deeper than thought, and we are in for a sustained period ‚Äãof significantly higher prices.
* Japan FX intervention ‚Äãrisks rise
The Japanese yen is ‚ÅÝweakening rapidly, and is now within sight of 160 per dollar. It's at levels that prompted the New York Fed to 'check rates' in dollar/yen in January, seen as a warning of potential joint U.S.-Japanese intervention to support the yen.
Tokyo ‚Äãis in a bind though. Safe-haven demand is driving the dollar higher across the board, and yen sentiment ‚Äãis particularly bearish because ‚ÅÝJapan imports 95% of its energy, which is now much more expensive. Would intervention be warranted if the 'fundamentals' justify a weaker yen?
What could move markets tomorrow?
Want to ‚Äãreceive Trading Day in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for my newsletter here .
