Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust ‚ÄãPrinciples , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Trump administration estimates Iran war cost at over $11 billion in six days, source says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 11 (Reuters) - Officials from ‚ÄãPresident Donald Trump's administration estimated during a congressional briefing this week that the ‚Äåfirst six days of the war on Iran had cost the United States at least $11.3 billion, a source familiar with the matter said on Wednesday.
That figure, from a closed-door briefing for senators on Tuesday, ‚Äãdid not include the entire cost of the war, but was provided to ‚Äãlawmakers as they have clamored for more information about the conflict.
Several ‚ÅÝcongressional aides have said they expect the White House to soon submit a request ‚Äãto Congress for additional funding for the war. Some officials have said the request ‚Äãcould be for $50 billion, while others have said that estimate seems low.
The administration has not provided a public assessment of the cost of the conflict or a clear idea of its expected duration. ‚ÄãTrump said during a trip to Kentucky on Wednesday that "we won" the war but that ‚Äãthe United States will stay in the fight to finish the job.
The $11.3 billion figure was first reported ‚Äåon ‚ÅÝWednesday by the New York Times.
The campaign against Iran began on February 28 with U.S. and Israeli airstrikes and has so far killed around 2,000 people , mostly Iranians and Lebanese, as the conflict has spread into Lebanon and thrown global energy markets and transport into ‚Äãchaos.
Administration officials also have ‚Äãtold lawmakers that $5.6 ‚ÅÝbillion of munitions were used during the first two days of strikes.
Members of Congress, who may soon have to approve additional funding ‚Äãfor the war, have expressed concern that the conflict will ‚Äãdeplete U.S. ‚ÅÝmilitary stocks at a time when the defense industry was already struggling to keep up with demand.
Trump met executives from seven defense contractors last week as the Pentagon worked to ‚ÅÝreplenish supplies.
Democratic ‚Äãlawmakers have demanded public testimony under oath from administration ‚Äãofficials about the Republican president's plans for the war, including how long it might last and what his ‚Äãplans are for Iran once the fighting has stopped.

item.title: FBI opens first Ecuador office in fight against drug trafficking
item.description:
QUITO, March 11 (Reuters) - The United States has opened its ‚Äãfirst Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) office ‚Äåin Ecuador, the U.S. Embassy in Quito said on Wednesday.
The office will aim to ‚Äãwork with Ecuadorean officials to fight the trafficking ‚Äãof drugs and weapons, along with ‚ÅÝmoney laundering and the financing of ‚Äãterrorism, the embassy said in a social ‚Äãmedia post.
Ecuador's Interior Minister John Reimber told reporters that collaboration with the FBI office would ‚Äãstart immediately, after prior joint efforts ‚Äãwith the U.S.
"What has changed is that we have ‚ÄåFBI ‚ÅÝagents permanently in Ecuador working with a national police unit that has been set up so that they can ‚Äãwork together," ‚Äãhe said.
‚ÅÝEcuadorean President Daniel Noboa has made a military crackdown on ‚Äãorganized crime a cornerstone of ‚Äãhis ‚ÅÝadministration. Last week his government staged a joint operation with U.S. officials to ‚ÅÝtarget ‚Äãdrug trafficking operations in ‚Äãthe South American country.

item.title: Senegal lawmakers approve new, harsher anti-LGBT bill
item.description:
DAKAR, March 11 (Reuters) - Lawmakers in Senegal on Wednesday endorsed a new bill doubling the maximum ‚Äåprison term for same-sex sexual acts and criminalizing any efforts to promote homosexuality.
The vote - with 135 in favour, zero against and three abstaining - is the final step in adopting the legislation which was a campaign promise of the ‚Äãgovernment that came to power in 2024, led by President Bassirou Diomaye Faye and ‚ÄãPrime Minister Ousmane Sonko.
Senegal's penal code already carried an article, last amended in ‚ÅÝ1966, imposing prison terms of up to five years and fines of up to 1,500,000 CFA ‚Äãfrancs ($2,676.18) for "acts against nature."
But proponents of the new bill said that article was too vague and ‚Äãnot tough enough.
The new version says acts against nature can be punished with prison terms of up to 10 years and fines of up to 10 million CFA francs (roughly $17,700).
It says a judge may not grant a suspended ‚Äãsentence, nor reduce a prison term below the minimum.
It specifies that acts against nature relate to ‚Äãhomosexuality, bisexuality, "transsexuality", zoophilia and necrophilia.
Those found guilty of promoting or financing such acts also face prison time.
In the weeks ‚Äåleading ‚ÅÝup to Wednesday's vote, supporters of the law, including lawmakers from the ruling Pastef party, organised multiple demonstrations in Dakar in which participants shouted "no to homosexuality" and held signs with rainbows crossed out.
The period has also been marked by a surge in arrests of men on suspicion of "acts against ‚Äãnature" as well as, ‚Äãin some cases, "voluntary transmission" ‚ÅÝof HIV - a crime carrying up to 10 years in prison.
Some 27 men were arrested between February 9 and February 24, according to the ‚ÄãInternational Federation for Human Rights.
The Senegalese law is part of a wave ‚Äãof anti-LGBT legislation ‚ÅÝin the region.
Last year Burkina Faso passed a law criminalizing same-sex sexual relations for the first time, imposing prison terms of up to five years.
Lawmakers in Ghana are considering a new bill that would ‚ÅÝincrease ‚Äãthe maximum penalty for same-sex sexual acts from three to ‚Äãfive years and impose jail time for the "wilful promotion, sponsorship, or support of LGBTQ+ activities".
($1 = 560.5000 CFA francs)

item.title: Iranian oil flows through Strait of Hormuz even as Gulf neighbors' exports shut
item.description:
NEW YORK/LONDON/HOUSTON, March 11 (Reuters) - Iranian crude oil has continued to flow through the Strait of Hormuz ‚Äãat a near-normal pace even as Tehran-linked attacks on ships in the narrow waterway have decimated exports from other Gulf countries, a Reuters ‚Äåreview of tanker tracking data showed.
Iran has exported about 13.7 million barrels of crude oil since Israel and the U.S. launched attacks on the country on February 28, according to analysis from TankerTrackers.com, a maritime intelligence company that specializes in tracking the so-called shadow fleet, a network of vessels used to transport oil and gas from countries under Western sanctions.
Vessel tracking service Kpler pegged ‚ÄãIranian exports in the first 11 days of March even higher at about 16.5 million barrels.
Iran's retaliation to the Israeli and U.S. attacks has included strikes on ships ‚Äãin the Strait of Hormuz and energy infrastructure across the Middle East, bringing non-Iranian vessel transits through the main gateway for ‚ÅÝmuch of Middle Eastern oil exports to a near standstill and forcing producers in the region to cut output .
Iran's ability to keep exporting oil without any reported interceptions contrasts ‚Äãsharply with what happened during the U.S. military campaign in Venezuela , which involved a naval blockade of the Latin American nation and seizures of vessels attempting to enter or exit Venezuelan ‚Äãwaters.
"I'm surprised, given their successful seizures of Venezuela-related vessels this past December, that the U.S. did not initiate a similar campaign prior to starting this conflict, or has not done so at this time," said David Tannenbaum, a director at consulting firm Blackstone Compliance Services.
However, U.S. efforts to stop Iran-linked tankers could unleash more attacks on vessels passing the Strait of Hormuz, Next Barrel ‚Äãoil and shipping analyst Matias Togni said.
So long as Iran is moving its vessels through the region, Iran has an incentive to keep the Strait of Hormuz ‚Äãopen at least to some degree, said James Lightbourn, shipping financier and founder of Cavalier Shipping, maritime investing and advisory business.
"If the U.S. were seizing tankers, it would give Iran less ‚Äåto lose ‚ÅÝby shutting the strait entirely (such as with mines)," Lightbourn said.
U.S. President Donald Trump's White House did not immediately reply to a request for comment on whether Washington plans any actions against Iranian oil exports.
The TankerTracker.com and Kpler data indicate Iran's crude oil exports equate to between 1.1 million barrels per day and 1.5 million bpd from February 28 through March 11. The country's average exports last year were 1.69 million bpd, according to Kpler records.
The pace could pick up in the ‚Äãdays ahead. Multiple very large crude ‚Äãcarriers, the largest oil vessels in service, ‚ÅÝare still loading oil at Iran's Kharg Island export hub , according to satellite imagery reviewed by TankerTrackers.com.
Prior to the February 28 strikes, Iran had ramped up exports to about 2.17 million bpd in February in anticipation of Israeli-U.S. military action, Kpler data showed. ‚ÄãRecord oil exports from Iran were about 3.79 million bpd in the week of February 16, the data showed.
Six crude ‚Äãoil tankers have left ‚ÅÝIran since February 28, including the U.S.-sanctioned vessel Cuma, which sailed this week, according to analysis from Kpler and Lloyd's List Intelligence. Two liquefied petroleum gas tankers, also under U.S. sanctions, sailed out of Iran on Friday after loading cargoes, Reuters earlier reported.
At least 11 million barrels of crude oil have been shipped out of Iran, with four supertankers that left Iran ‚ÅÝcarrying 8 ‚Äãmillion barrels arriving in waters around Singapore, a separate analysis showed.
The vessels follow the same pattern of ‚Äãsailing within Iran's exclusive economic zone, which extends up to 24 miles and beyond local territorial limits of 12 nautical miles.
This is seen as providing the vessels with a measure of protection by keeping ‚Äãthem within Iran's waters, shipping sources said.

item.title: Petrobras sells 20 million liters of diesel at auction in southern Brazil
item.description:
RIO DE JANEIRO, March 11 (Reuters) - Brazilian state-run oil firm Petrobras (PETR3.SA) , opens new tab sold 20 million liters of diesel at an auction in the southern Rio Grande do Sul state, the ‚Äãcompany said in a statement on Wednesday.
The move comes as Petrobras tries to generate ‚Äãcompetition in the domestic market amid an increase in prices due to ‚ÅÝthe conflict in the Middle East.
The company did not provide further details about the ‚Äãsale, but two sources told Reuters that it priced the fuel at up to 1.78 ‚Äãreais ($0.3450) per liter above the price for distributors in the state, which is 3.18 reais per liter.
The auction kicked off at 1 real per liter above the current price tag, they added on condition of ‚Äãanonymity.
"The market needed volume," one of the sources said.
The spike in diesel prices stemming ‚Äãfrom joint U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran is an immediate threat to Brazil's farm sector, raising costs for producers ‚Äåharvesting a ‚ÅÝrecord soybean crop and planting corn that cannot be delayed.
Although Petrobras, which accounts for most of Brazil's supply, has not changed its prices, diesel prices are up domestically since some fuel is imported or produced by local refineries that follow global oil prices.
Reuters reported on ‚ÄãTuesday, citing sources, that ‚ÄãPetrobras was planning to ‚ÅÝhold the auction as reports of a diesel shortage in Rio Grande do Sul emerged, with one person saying the initiative was necessary ‚Äãto "try to calm market nerves."
Petrobras had feared supplying the fuel at ‚Äãa lower ‚ÅÝprice only for distributors to store it and make a profit when the state-run firm raises prices. An auction creates competition and prevents this situation.
Through the auction, the company was able ‚ÅÝto meet ‚Äãa volume of extra orders without benefiting specific groups ‚Äãand to pass on part of the price gap with the international market without changing its prices in the ‚Äãtraditional sales system.
($1 = 5.1593 reais)

item.title: The 16 US trading partners hit by Trump's '301' probes
item.description:
March 11 (Reuters) - The United States on Wednesday said it was launching ‚Äãunfair trade practice investigations into 16 ‚Äåkey trading partners , part of an urgent effort by President Donald Trump's ‚Äãadministration to restore tariffs recently struck ‚Äãdown by the U.S. Supreme ‚ÅÝCourt as illegal.
The probes are ‚Äãbeing conducted under Section 301 of ‚Äãthe Trade Act of 1974, which empowers the U.S. Trade Representative to ‚Äãimpose tariffs or other retaliatory ‚Äãmeasures against trading partners determined to employ ‚Äåunfair ‚ÅÝtrade practices.
The administration alleges the 16 all employ policies that disadvantage U.S. goods producers, resulting in ‚Äãall but ‚Äãone ‚ÅÝof them running substantial goods trade surpluses with ‚Äãthe U.S.
Here are the 16 ‚Äãsubject ‚ÅÝto the investigation:
Source: U.S. Census ‚ÄãBureau trade data

item.title: US opens new unfair-trade probes to rebuild Trump's tariff pressure
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 11 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump 's administration on Wednesday launched a new trade investigation into excess industrial capacity in 16 major trading partners in a move ‚Äãto rebuild tariff pressure after the U.S. Supreme Court tore down the centerpiece of Trump's tariff program last month.
U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer said that the "Section ‚Äå301" unfair trade practices investigation could lead to new tariffs imposed against China, the European Union, India, Japan, South Korea and Mexico by this summer.
Other trading partners subject to the excess capacity probe include Taiwan, Vietnam, Thailand, Malaysia, Cambodia, Singapore, Indonesia, Bangladesh, Switzerland and Norway. Canada, the second-largest U.S. trading partner, was not mentioned as a target of the probe.
"So these investigations will focus on economies that we have evidence appear ‚Äãto exhibit structural excess capacity and production in various manufacturing sectors, such as through larger persistent trade surpluses or underutilized or unused capacity," Greer told reporters on ‚Äãa conference call.
Greer also said that on Thursday he would initiate another probe under Section 301 of the Trade Act ‚ÅÝof 1974 to ban U.S. imports of goods produced with forced labor. That investigation covers more than 60 countries.
The U.S. has already cracked down on solar panel imports and other goods ‚Äãfrom China's Xinjiang region under the Uyghur Forced Labor Protection Act signed into law by former President Joe Biden, and the probe could expand such actions to other countries.
Greer said he ‚Äãwanted other countries to enforce bans on goods produced with forced labor similar to those enshrined in a nearly century-old trade law.
The U.S. alleges that Chinese authorities have established labor camps for ethnic Uyghur and other Muslim groups in the western region, though Beijing denies allegations of abuse.
Greer said that he hoped to conclude the Section 301 investigations, including proposed remedies, before new temporary tariffs imposed by Trump in late ‚ÄãFebruary expire in July. After the Supreme Court struck down Trump's global tariffs as illegal under a national emergencies law on February 20, he imposed a 10% tariff for ‚Äã150 days under Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974.
He laid out a swift timeline for the excess capacity probe, with public comments accepted through April 15 and a public hearing slated for ‚Äåabout May ‚ÅÝ5.
The probes offer the Trump administration an avenue to rebuild a credible tariff threat against trading partners to keep them negotiating and implement trade deals that were cut to reduce his higher tariff rates under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act.
Greer said the new probes, long telegraphed by administration officials, should come as no surprise to trading partners, and they should stick to their deals, although he stopped short of saying that this would make them immune to all new Section 301 tariffs.
He said that Trump was determined to pursue tariffs and "will find ‚Äãa way to deal with unfair trading ‚Äãpractices. He'll find a way to get ‚ÅÝour trade deficit down. He'll find a way to protect U.S. manufacturing. We have a lot of tools to do it," Greer said.
The probes come as Trump officials led by U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent prepare this week to meet with Chinese counterparts in Paris to ‚Äãset the stage for Trump to meet Chinese President Xi Jinping in Beijing at the end of March.
Trump's tariffs on Chinese ‚Äãgoods were effectively cut ‚ÅÝby 10 percentage points by the Supreme Court decision and subsequent temporary tariffs, reducing U.S. leverage on China trade and export controls.
Trump during his first term used a Section 301 probe to back his tariffs on many Chinese imports of about 25% and the law is widely viewed as legally robust, having withstood prior court challenges.
The excess capacity probe targets an area of concern raised ‚ÅÝwith China by ‚Äãsuccessive administrations from Trump's first term through the Biden administration, growing state-supported manufacturing output that is flooding ‚Äãthe world with cheap goods.
Greer said this includes production "untethered" to market demand and that the problem has spread to other countries. He said the probe will focus on evidence including large global current account surpluses, government subsidies, suppressed ‚Äãdomestic wages, noncommercial activities of state-owned enterprises, inadequate environmental and labor standards, subsidized lending and currency practices.

item.title: Exclusive: US intelligence says Iran government is not at risk of collapse, say sources
item.description:
NEW YORK/WASHINGTON, March 11 (Reuters) - U.S. intelligence indicates that Iran's leadership is still largely intact and is not at risk of collapse any time soon after nearly two weeks of relentless U.S. and Israeli bombardment, according to three ‚Äãsources familiar with the matter.
A "multitude" of intelligence reports provide "consistent analysis that the regime is not in danger" of collapse and "retains control of the Iranian public," ‚Äåsaid one of the sources, all of whom were granted anonymity to discuss U.S. intelligence findings.
The latest report was completed within the last few days, the source said.
With political pressure building over soaring oil costs , President Donald Trump has suggested he will end the biggest U.S. military operation since 2003 "soon." But finding an acceptable end to the war could be difficult if Iran's hardline leaders remain firmly entrenched.
The intelligence reporting underscores the cohesion ‚Äãof Iran's clerical leadership despite the killing of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei on February 28, the first day of the U.S. and Israeli strikes.
Israeli officials in closed discussions also ‚Äãhave acknowledged there is no certainty the war will lead to the clerical government's collapse, a senior Israeli official told Reuters.
The sources stressed that ‚ÅÝthe situation on the ground is fluid and that the dynamics inside Iran could change.
The Office of the Director of National Intelligence and the Central Intelligence Agency declined to comment.
The ‚ÄãWhite House did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Since launching their war, the U.S. and Israel have struck a range of Iranian targets , including air defenses, nuclear sites, and ‚Äãmembers of the senior leadership.
The Trump administration has given varying reasons for the war. In announcing the beginning of the U.S. operation, Trump urged Iranians to "take over your government," but top aides have since denied that the objective was to oust Iran's leadership.
In addition to Khamenei, the strikes have killed dozens of senior officials and some of the highest-ranking commanders in the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), an elite paramilitary force that ‚Äãcontrols large parts of the economy.
Still, the U.S. intelligence reports indicate that the IRGC and the interim leaders who assumed power after Khamenei's death retain control of the country.
The Assembly ‚Äãof Experts, a group of senior Shiite clerics, earlier this week declared Khamenei's son, Mojtaba, the new supreme leader .
Israel has no intention of allowing any remnants of the former government to stay intact, said a ‚Äåfourth source ‚ÅÝfamiliar with the matter.
It is unclear how the current U.S.-Israeli military campaign would topple the government.
It would likely require a ground offensive that would allow people inside Iran to safely protest in the streets, said the source.
The Trump administration has not ruled out sending U.S. troops into Iran.
Reuters reported last week that Iranian Kurdish militias based in neighboring Iraq consulted with the U.S. about how and whether to attack Iran's security forces in the western part of the country.
Such an incursion could put pressure on Iranian security services ‚Äãthere, allowing Iranians to rise up against the ‚Äãgovernment.
Abdullah Mohtadi, the head of the ‚ÅÝKomala Party of Iranian Kurdistan, part of a six-party coalition of Iranian Kurdish parties, said in an interview on Wednesday that the parties are highly organized inside Iran and that "tens of thousands of young people are ready to take up arms" against the government if they receive ‚ÄãU.S. support.
Mohtadi said he has received reports from inside Iranian Kurdistan that IRGC units and other security forces have abandoned bases ‚Äãand barracks out of fear ‚ÅÝof U.S. and Israeli strikes.
"We have been witnessing tangible signs of weakness in Kurdish areas," he said.
But recent U.S. intelligence reports have cast doubt on the ability of the Iranian Kurdish groups to sustain a fight against Iranian security services, according to two sources familiar with those assessments.
The intelligence indicates that the groups lack the firepower and numbers, they said.
The Kurdish Regional Government, which governs ‚ÅÝthe autonomous region ‚Äãof Iraqi Kurdistan where the Iranian Kurdish groups are based, did not immediately respond to a request for ‚Äãcomment.
The Iranian Kurdish groups have in recent days asked senior officials in Washington and U.S. lawmakers for the U.S. to provide them with weapons and armored vehicles, another person familiar with the matter said.
But Trump said on Saturday ‚Äãthat he had ruled out having the Iranian Kurdish groups go into Iran.

item.title: Russia accuses Ukraine, Britain of 'terrorist attack' on border city
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 11 (Reuters) - Russia's Foreign Ministry on Wednesday ‚Äåcondemned a deadly Ukrainian strike on the western Russian city of Bryansk ‚Äãas a "terrorist attack" and accused ‚ÄãBritain, whose missiles it said ‚ÅÝwere used, of overstepping international ‚Äãlegal norms.
In a statement, the ‚Äãministry said the strike was intended to derail efforts toward a peace process ‚Äãand fuel an escalation ‚Äãof the conflict.
Bryansk regional governor Alexander Bogomaz ‚Äåsaid ‚ÅÝon Wednesday one more person had died in the attack, bringing the death toll to ‚Äãseven. Bogomaz ‚Äãsaid ‚ÅÝearlier that Tuesday's strike had injured 42 people, ‚Äãwithout saying what was hit.
Ukraine ‚Äãsaid ‚ÅÝit had struck a key plant producing missile components. Moscow ‚ÅÝaccused ‚Äãit of deliberately ‚Äãtargeting civilians.

item.title: Trump and Iran signal no quick end to war as tankers burn in Iraqi waters
item.description:
DUBAI/TEL AVIV/WASHINGTON, March 12 (Reuters) - The United States and Iran have signaled no quick end to their war, with U.S. President Donald Trump saying it was necessary to finish the job and Iran warning that the world should be ‚Äãready for oil at $200 a barrel after striking tankers in Iraqi waters and other ships near the vital Strait of Hormuz.
The war unleashed with joint U.S. and Israeli air strikes nearly two weeks ‚Äåago has so far killed around 2,000 people , mostly Iranians and Lebanese, as it has spread into Lebanon and thrown global energy markets and transport into chaos.
The United Nations Children's Fund (UNICEF) said more than 1,100 children had been killed or injured.
At a campaign-style rally in Kentucky ahead of November midterm elections in which his Republican party is trailing badly, Trump said the United States had "won" the war but didn't want to have to go back every two years .
"We don't want to leave early do we?" he said on Wednesday. "We got to ‚Äãfinish the job."
Oil prices, which shot up earlier in the week to nearly $120 a barrel before settling back to around $90, rose nearly 5% on Wednesday and extended gains in Asian trade on Thursday amid renewed ‚Äãfears about supply disruption. Wall Street's main share indexes fell .
Despite what the Pentagon has described as the most intense airstrikes since the start of the war , Iran on Wednesday hit ‚ÅÝtargets in Israel and across the Middle East, demonstrating it can still fight back.
Iranian explosive-laden boats appear to have attacked two fuel tankers in Iraqi waters setting them ablaze and killing one crew member after projectiles struck three ‚Äãvessels in Gulf waters, said port, maritime security and risk firms.
"This appears to mark a direct and forceful Iranian response to the IEA‚Äôs overnight announcement of a massive strategic reserve release aimed at cooling runaway prices," said Tony ‚ÄãSycamore, analyst at IG.
The International Energy Agency, made up of major oil consuming nations, recommended releasing 400 million barrels from global strategic reserves to dampen one of the worst oil shocks since the 1970s, the biggest such intervention in history.
Trump said the IEA decision "will substantially reduce oil prices as we end this threat to America and the world."
U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright said Trump had authorized the release of 172 million barrels from the U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve from next week.
The war has seen ports and cities in ‚Äãthe Gulf states as well as targets in Israel hit by drone and missile barrages from Iran.
So far there has been no sign that ships can safely sail through the Strait of Hormuz , the now-blockaded channel ‚Äãalong the Iranian coast that serves as a conduit for around a fifth of the world's oil.
On Wednesday, an Iranian military spokesperson said the Strait was "undoubtedly" under Iran's control and the G7 group of nations - the United States, Canada, Japan, Italy, ‚ÄåBritain, Germany and ‚ÅÝFrance - agreed to examine the option of providing escort for ships so they can navigate freely in the Gulf.
Trump said U.S. forces had knocked out 58 Iranian naval ships and that Iran was "pretty much at the end of the line."
He said the U.S. would now "look very strongly" at the Strait of Hormuz, adding: "The straits are in great shape. We've knocked out all of their boats. They have some missiles, but not very many."
Trump said earlier ships "should" transit through the strait but sources said Iran had deployed about a dozen mines in the channel, further complicating the blockade.
ABC News said the Federal Bureau of Investigation had warned of Iranian drones potentially striking the U.S. West Coast, although Trump said he ‚Äãwas not worried that Iran might launch strikes on ‚ÄãU.S. soil .
The U.S. State Department also warned that Iran ‚ÅÝand aligned militias may be planning to target U.S.-owned oil and energy infrastructure in Iraq and warned that militias had in the past targeted hotels frequented by Americans.
U.S. and Israeli officials have said their aim is to end Iran's ability to project force beyond its borders and destroy its nuclear programme. An Israeli military official said the ‚Äãmilitary still had an extensive list of targets to hit in Iran, including ballistic missile and nuclear-related sites.
The U.S. military told Iranians to stay clear of ports ‚Äãwith Iranian navy facilities, drawing ‚ÅÝa warning from Iran's military that if the ports were threatened, economic and trade centres in the region would be "legitimate targets".
With pump prices surging , oil prices have become an increasingly urgent element in the calculations behind the war.
Iran has made clear it intends to impose a prolonged economic shock, with the spokesperson for Iran's military command saying in remarks directed at the U.S. on Wednesday: "Get ready for oil to be $200 a barrel, because the oil price depends on regional security, which you ‚ÅÝhave destabilised."
The rate ‚Äãat which IEA countries can release strategic reserves will vary and the amount released would account for just a fraction of the supply ‚Äãthrough the Hormuz Strait.
After offices of a bank in Tehran were hit overnight, Iran said it would respond with attacks on banks that do business with the U.S. or Israel and people across the Middle East should stay 1,000 metres from banks.

item.title: Australia police recover two bodies in search for missing Chinese tourists
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 12 (Reuters) - Australian police have recovered two bodies during a search for two Chinese backpackers who went missing in ‚Äãa flood-hit region in the country's east, local ‚Äåmedia reported on Thursday.
The bodies, believed to be those of the missing Chinese tourists, were found inside a car, national broadcaster Australian ‚ÄãBroadcasting Corp reported, citing police.
Police divers and emergency personnel ‚Äãwere expected to be at the scene in ‚ÅÝthe Gympie region of Queensland state on Thursday to ‚Äãconfirm the identities of the bodies, ABC News said.
Australian police ‚Äãsaid in a statement that the missing tourists - a 26-year-old man and 23-year-old woman - had been travelling to North Burnett in Queensland, about ‚Äã380 km (236 miles) northwest of Brisbane, the state capital.
Gympie ‚Äãmayor Glen Hartwig described the incident as an "absolute tragedy".
"These people have come ‚Äåto ‚ÅÝAustralia to see our beautiful country ... and tragically they've ended up losing their lives," he told ABC News.
Major flood warnings remained in place in parts of Queensland and the ‚ÄãNorthern Territory after ‚Äãincessant rains ‚ÅÝcaused rivers to burst their banks, cutting off rural towns and forcing hundreds of residents ‚Äãto evacuate.
A helicopter rescued two people from a ‚Äãcar ‚ÅÝstuck in crocodile-inhabited waters at a creek crossing near the town of Katherine in Northern Territory, television footage showed on ‚ÅÝWednesday.
Authorities ‚Äãhave urged residents to avoid driving ‚Äãthrough floodwaters, warning that several crocodiles have been sighted in inundated suburbs around ‚ÄãKatherine.

item.title: North Korean leader Kim test fires handguns during visit to munitions factory, KCNA reports
item.description:
SEOUL, March 12 (Reuters) - North Korean ‚Äãleader Kim Jong Un visited a munitions factory ‚Äåon Wednesday, state media KCNA reported, releasing photographs that showed him touring the facility with his teenage daughter and the pair ‚Äãtest-firing handguns.
During the visit, Kim oversaw firearms production and ‚Äãcalled for modernisation of the process, KCNA said.
The factory ‚ÅÝis "playing a very important role in increasing the ‚Äãcombat efficiency of the army," Kim was quoted as ‚Äãsaying.
He emphasised a need to expand the production capacity of the factory "in a far-sighted way," according to KCNA.
Kim, who frequently tries ‚Äãto show off the nuclear-armed state's military prowess, was ‚Äãrecently shown overseeing the test-firing of a strategic cruise missile on ‚Äåa ‚ÅÝnewly launched destroyer, according to KCNA.
Kim's daughter, known as Ju Ae, accompanied her father during the visit to the munitions factory, state media photographs showed. They ‚Äãwere both clad ‚Äãin black ‚ÅÝleather jackets, with Ju Ae shown participating in a shooting drill alongside military officials.
There ‚Äãhas been speculation that Ju Ae is ‚Äãbeing ‚ÅÝgroomed as a potential leadership successor.
South Korea's National Intelligence Service (NIS) last month told lawmakers there were signs that ‚ÅÝKim's ‚Äãdaughter was providing input on policy ‚Äãmatters, reflecting that she "was in the stage of being internally appointed successor."

item.title: Venezuela's Rodriguez promotes deputy to head oil ministry
item.description:
March 11 (Reuters) - Venezuela's acting President Delcy Rodriguez said on Wednesday that she has appointed ‚ÄãPaula Henao as oil minister, filling the key role as the country ‚Äåoverhauls its oil industry amid U.S. pressure.
The position became vacant when Rodriguez, who had been serving as both oil minister and vice president, rose to the presidency following the United States' ‚Äãcapture of President Nicolas Maduro in January.
Henao, previously vice oil minister, will take ‚Äãthe helm as the U.S. pushes to open Venezuela to American ‚ÅÝinvestment, especially in oil, gas and mining. U.S. President Donald Trump has praised ‚ÄãRodriguez for cooperating on efforts to move oil to the United States.
In January, Venezuela's ‚Äãruling party-dominated legislature unanimously passed a reform to the hydrocarbons law that expands the oil ministry's decision-making power , in addition to lowering taxes and granting autonomy for private producers and making possible asset ‚Äãtransfer and outsourcing.
The changes are aimed at boosting oil and gas production and ‚Äãforeign investment following a $100 billion reconstruction plan for the industry proposed by Trump shortly after Maduro's ‚Äåcapture.
Venezuela ‚ÅÝis home to the world's largest proven oil reserves.
"I am fully confident that, with her professionalism, extensive experience and many years of service within this ministry, we will advance in the recovery and development of the energy sector, a fundamental pillar for ‚Äãeconomic growth and the ‚Äãwell-being of the ‚ÅÝVenezuelan people," Rodriguez said in a social media post.
U.S. special forces captured Maduro and his wife Cilia Flores in a nighttime raid ‚Äãin Caracas on January 3, following months of pressure from ‚ÄãTrump's administration ‚ÅÝon the socialist leader to step down, which included sanctions on Venezuelan oil - some of which have since been eased - and a blockade of sanctioned vessels going in or out ‚ÅÝof ‚ÄãVenezuelan waters.
U.S. prosecutors said Maduro abused his power ‚Äãto help drug traffickers throughout his 13-year tenure. Maduro and Flores have pleaded not guilty to drug trafficking charges .

item.title: US to release 172 million barrels of oil from strategic petroleum reserve
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 11 (Reuters) - The U.S. will ‚Äãrelease 172 million barrels of oil from its strategic ‚Äåpetroleum reserve in a bid to reduce oil prices that have soared due to supply shocks from the U.S.-Israeli war on ‚ÄãIran, U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright said on ‚ÄãWednesday.
Wright said the release is part of a broader ‚ÅÝrelease of 400 million barrels of oil agreed ‚Äãto by the 32-nation International Energy Agency earlier in the ‚Äãday.
Wright said the release will begin next week and will take about 120 days to deliver.
The U.S. and Israel began attacks on ‚ÄãIran on February 28. Iran has responded with its ‚Äãown strikes on Israel and Gulf countries with U.S. bases.
Raising the ‚Äåstakes ‚ÅÝfor the global economy, Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said it would block oil shipments from the Gulf unless the U.S. and Israeli attacks cease. The war ‚Äãhas shaken markets ‚Äãaround the ‚ÅÝworld.
When asked earlier on Wednesday whether he was looking at the threshold for the ‚Äãstrategic petroleum reserve, President Donald Trump said Washington ‚Äãwill "reduce ‚ÅÝit a little bit."
"The United States has arranged to more than replace these strategic reserves with approximately 200 million ‚ÅÝbarrels ‚Äãwithin the next year," the U.S. ‚Äãenergy secretary said in a statement.

item.title: China set to pass new ethnic minority law, prioritise use of Mandarin language
item.description:
BEIJING, March 12 (Reuters) - China ‚Äãis expected to pass a law on a "shared" national identity among the country's 55 ethnic minority groups on ‚ÄåThursday, a move critics say will further erode the identity of people who are not majority Han Chinese and risk making anyone challenging that "unity" a separatist punishable by law.
Called "Promoting Ethnic Unity and Progress", the ethnic minority law aims to forge national unity and advance the rejuvenation of the Chinese nation with ‚Äãthe Chinese Communist Party (CCP) at its core, a draft copy of the law showed.
It is due to be passed ‚Äãat the closing session of the annual meeting of the National People's Congress , China's legislature.
Officially, China has 56 ‚ÅÝofficially recognised ethnic groups, dominated by the Han Chinese, who account for more than 91% of the country's 1.4 billion people.
China's ‚Äãethnic minority populations‚Äîincluding Tibetans, Mongols, Hui, Manchus, and Uyghurs ‚Äî are concentrated in regions that together cover roughly half of the country's ‚Äãland area, much of it rich in natural resources.
The law aims to promote integration across ethnic groups through education, housing, migration, community life, culture, tourism, and development policy, the law said.
It mandates that Mandarin is the basic language of instruction in schools, and for government and official business.
In public settings, ‚Äãwhere Mandarin and minority languages are used together, Mandarin must be given "prominence in placement, order, and similar respects," the draft said.
"The ‚Äãstate respects and protects the learning and use of minority languages and scripts," it added.
Religious groups, religious schools, and religious venues must adhere "to the ‚Äådirection ‚ÅÝof the Sinicization of religion in China," according to the draft.
The law also seeks to ban any interference with marriage choices based on ethnicity, custom, or religion, to enable more intermarriage between ethnic groups.
Allen Carlson, an associate professor of government at Cornell University and an expert on Chinese foreign policy, said the law underlined a move towards assimilation.
"The law makes it clearer ‚Äãthan ever that in President Xi ‚ÄãJinping's PRC non-Han peoples must ‚ÅÝdo more to integrate themselves with the Han majority, and above all else be loyal to Beijing,‚Äù he said, referring to China by the initials for its official name.
Ethnic affairs are incorporated ‚Äãinto China's social governance system, with clauses that include anti-separatism, border security, risk prevention, and ‚Äãsocial stability.
Organisations and ‚ÅÝindividuals outside China that carry out acts against the country "that undermine ethnic unity and progress or create ethnic separatism shall be pursued for legal liability in accordance with the law," the draft said.
An editorial in state newspaper China Daily said that the law had followed ‚ÅÝa rigorous ‚Äãlegislative process, been through multiple readings and consultations with lawmakers and representatives ‚Äãfrom ethnic minority communities.
"The law stresses the protection of cultural traditions and lifestyles of all ethnic groups... it is misleading to claim that ethnic minorities in China ‚Äãmust choose between economic development and cultural preservation," it said.

item.title: SoftBank's wartime bet on PayPay IPO is shrewd
item.description:
HONG KONG, March 12 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Sometimes victory is just getting out the door. That‚Äôs the case for SoftBank Group-backed (9984.T) , opens new tab PayPay, which on Wednesday priced its U.S. initial public ‚Äãoffering at $16 per share, markedly below a target range of $17 to $20. Set against the backdrop of a ‚Äåglobally disruptive war in the Middle East with no clear end, however, the decision to press ahead with listing the Japanese payments group is sensible.
The IPO raised $880 billion at a valuation of $10.7 billion, well short of SoftBank founder Masayoshi Son‚Äôs earlier ambition of up to $20 billion. That ‚Äãwas always a moonshot and would have required valuing the e-payments and credit card provider at 8.4 times its sales ‚Äãfor the 2026 fiscal year based on its annualised result for the first nine months, ‚ÅÝor five times the multiple carried by American peer PayPal (PYPL.O) , opens new tab . But even if the company's grand plans to build a ‚Äãone-stop financial superapp in the $4 trillion economy look daunting, prospects for the core business look robust thanks to its home market's ‚Äãbelated but speedy uptake of electronic payments.
Japan‚Äôs economic and geopolitical position also help explain why the deal could go ahead despite the U.S. and Israel launching a war against Iran. The Asian country has about 250 days of oil reserves to help ward off pain at the pump ‚Äãfor consumers who use PayPay‚Äôs services. The listing was also already overdue, having seen one big delay in November when its ‚Äãregulatory review stalled due to a U.S. government shutdown. Then its roadshow, originally slated to launch on March 2, got pushed back as the ‚Äåonset of ‚ÅÝwar rattled markets. Waiting for calmer trade might have cost PayPay more, however.
Qatar Investment Authority and Abu Dhabi Investment Authority were among investors who pledged more than $200 million toward the deal. Those commitments could have evaporated with Iranian missiles raining down on their home countries. Gulf states have started reviewing how they deploy their sovereign wealth in anticipation of offsetting severe economic losses triggered ‚Äãby the war, Reuters reported ‚Äãon Wednesday citing an ‚ÅÝunnamed Gulf official.
At least SoftBank still owns more than 90% of PayPay and will benefit from any future gains in secondary trading. If the pace of new commitments from petrodollar sovereign ‚Äãfunds slows, it will threaten deals around the world.
Follow Hudson Lockett on Bluesky , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .

item.title: Air New Zealand to cut flights as fuel price surge wreaks havoc on travel
item.description:
WELLINGTON, March 12 (Reuters) - Air ‚ÄãNew Zealand (AIR.NZ) , opens new tab said on Thursday it would slash 5% of its flights, or around 1,100 services, through early ‚ÄåMay as the Iran war sends jet fuel prices surging and disrupts travel even in rural areas thousands of miles from the conflict zone.
The New Zealand carrier led other airlines including Australia's Qantas Airways (QAN.AX) , opens new tab , Scandinavia's SAS and Thai Airways (THAI.BK) , opens new tab in announcing airfare hikes this week, blaming an abrupt spike in the cost of fuel that has ‚Äãrattled the global aviation sector.
The Middle Eastern conflict has forced many airlines to cancel flights to and from the region ‚Äãor use alternative routes due to drone and missile fire that has severely curtailed airspace and caused ‚ÅÝthe biggest aviation industry crisis since the pandemic.
Oil prices climbed on Thursday after Iraqi security officials said Iranian explosive-laden boats had ‚Äãhit two fuel oil tankers amid other global supply disruptions and Iran said the world should be ready for oil at $200 a ‚Äãbarrel .
Air New Zealand CEO Nikhil Ravishankar told state-owned Radio New Zealand that about 44,000 customers of the 1.9 million flying through early May would have to be reaccommodated due to domestic and international flight cuts.
Airports servicing areas such as popular New Zealand winemaking region Marlborough and west coast city New Plymouth ‚Äãwill see a reduction in services over the coming weeks.
Fewer long-haul flights would be cut, Ravishankar said, as its U.S. routes have ‚Äãbecome a more popular stopover on the way to Europe since widespread Middle Eastern airspace closures.
"People want to get to Europe still, and over ‚Äåthe U.S. ‚ÅÝairspace we can get them into Europe, and that's what we're focused on doing," he said.
Air New Zealand's shares were down 1% on Thursday, in line with drops in Hong Kong's Cathay Pacific (0293.HK) , opens new tab , Australia's Qantas Airways (QAN.AX) , opens new tab and Japan Airlines (9201.T) , opens new tab .
On Wednesday, two drones fell near Dubai's main airport - the world's busiest hub for global passengers - and Bahrain evacuated some planes, as attacks on infrastructure across the Gulf continued ‚Äãto wreak havoc on air ‚Äãtraffic.
The war has also disrupted ‚ÅÝshipping via the world's most vital oil export route, sent oil prices surging and upended global travel, pushing airline tickets on some routes sky-high, and sparking fears of a deep travel slump .
Travellers are also scrambling ‚Äãto switch to carriers that avoid Middle East airspace, with Thai Airways saying it was already ‚Äãtaking on board ‚ÅÝmore passengers to and from Europe.
Cathay Pacific has cancelled its flights to Dubai and Riyadh through the end of March and is instead adding more services to London and Zurich, taking advantage of a spike in demand for Asia-Europe flights that avoid the Middle East.
Highlighting the ripple ‚ÅÝeffects of ‚Äãthe conflict beyond the Middle East, the government in Vietnam warned on Wednesday ‚Äãthat domestic airlines may be at risk of fuel shortages as soon as next month.

item.title: Bangladesh secures spot LNG cargoes as Mideast conflict lifts costs
item.description:
DHAKA, March 12 (Reuters) - Bangladesh has bought three liquefied natural gas (LNG) cargoes on the spot market at higher prices, as it scrambles ‚Äãto steady supplies amid disruptions from the escalating Iran‚ÄìIsrael conflict, energy ‚Äåofficials said.
State-run Petrobangla has increasingly turned to the volatile spot market to bridge the supply gap, said energy officials in the South Asian nation, after some suppliers ‚Äãwere forced to halt shipments.
"If the disruption drags on, we‚Äôll have ‚Äãto lean more on costly spot LNG, which will add ‚ÅÝto our import burden and tighten supplies for power and industry," an ‚Äãenergy ministry official said, speaking on condition of anonymity.
The nation of 175 ‚Äãmillion relies on imports for roughly 95% of its energy needs. It has imposed fuel rationing for vehicles, curbed diesel sales and shut universities as the Iran war disrupts Middle East oil ‚Äãexports.
TotalEnergies will supply one cargo priced at $21.58 per million British thermal ‚Äãunits (mmBtu) for delivery on April 5 to 6, while two from Posco International Corp priced ‚Äåat $20.76 ‚ÅÝper mmBtu each are set for delivery on April 9 to 10 and April 12 to 13.
The purchases come after QatarEnergy suspended LNG deliveries to Bangladesh under a long-term contract, citing such disruptions.
Petrobangla also arranged additional ‚Äãspot LNG cargoes this month ‚Äãto bridge ‚ÅÝthe shortfall.
One shipment from commodity trader Gunvor, priced at $28.28 per mmBtu, is expected to arrive from March 15 to ‚Äã16, while another cargo from Vitol, priced at $23.08 per ‚ÄãmmBtu, is ‚ÅÝscheduled for March 18 to 19.
The latest purchases are a sharp increase over Bangladesh‚Äôs earlier LNG procurement this year. In January, it secured spot cargoes ‚ÅÝat ‚Äãabout $10 per mmBtu, reflecting rapid price escalation as ‚Äãtension surged.
The government's gas rationing effort has forced the shutdown of four fertiliser plants, to prioritise ‚Äãpower generation and other key areas.

item.title: Goldman Sachs raises Q4 Brent, WTI crude price forecast amid longer Hormuz disruption
item.description:
March 12 (Reuters) - Goldman Sachs raised its Brent, WTI crude oil price forecasts for the fourth quarter of 2026 to $71/67 per barrel from $66/62 as it sees longer ‚Äãdisruption to oil flows in the Strait of Hormuz due to ‚Äåthe U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.
Brent prices have gained more than 36% since the war began on February 28, while WTI has risen about 39%. Both benchmarks briefly topped $119 on ‚ÄãMonday, their highest levels since mid‚Äë2022.
The fighting has effectively shut the ‚ÄãStrait of Hormuz, leaving tankers stranded for more than a week ‚ÅÝand forcing producers to suspend output as storage nears capacity.
Goldman analysts in ‚Äãa note on Thursday said they now assume 21 days of low Strait ‚Äãof Hormuz (SoH) oil flows at 10% of normal levels followed by a 30-day gradual recovery, compared with their earlier expectation of a 10-day disruption.
The bank also said that daily ‚Äãoil prices are likely to exceed their 2008 peak if SoH flows ‚Äãremain depressed through March.
Goldman incorporated a larger policy response in its models, wherein 254 million barrels ‚Äåof ‚ÅÝactual global special petroleum reserve (SPR) releases and 31mb of draws in Russian crude would reduce the hit to global commercial oil inventories by nearly 50%.
The International Energy Agency on Wednesday agreed to release a record 400 million barrels of oil from ‚Äãstrategic stockpiles to combat ‚Äãa spike in ‚ÅÝglobal crude prices since the start of the war, with the U.S. contributing the bulk of the supply.
In Goldman's base case ‚Äãwhere Strait of Hormuz flows start recovering March 21 onwards, ‚Äãit assumes ‚ÅÝIEA member states won't fully release the 400 million barrels available.
This is because the bank assumes a logistical limit of 3 million barrels per day on draws from ‚ÅÝthe ‚ÄãOrganisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) SPR and ‚Äãa four-week phase-out of releases through early June when WTI prices are expected to moderate to ‚Äãthe low $70s.

item.title: Indonesia and Australia to expand security cooperation to include Japan, Papua New Guinea
item.description: JAKARTA, March 12 (Reuters) - Indonesia and Australia are planning to expand their security cooperation to include ‚ÄãJapan and Papua New Guinea, Jakarta's Defence Minister said on ‚ÄåThursday after a meeting with his Australian counterpart.

item.title: Crude oil futures separate from reality as Asia physical market buckles
item.description:
LAUNCESTON, Australia, March 12 (Reuters) - Crude oil futures prices are reflecting a view that the market can successfully navigate the Iran war, while prices for physical cargoes and refined products are signalling an imminent crisis.
Only one of these price signals is correct - and it's not ‚Äãwhat is happening in the paper oil market.
Global benchmark Brent crude futures ended at $91.98 a barrel on Wednesday, up 4.8% from the prior close but ‚Äåstill down from the brief spike on March 9 that saw them reach $119.50, the highest in nearly four years.
In the physical market, the premium for a physical cargo of Middle East benchmark Dubai crude over its paper equivalent rose to almost $38 a barrel on Wednesday, the highest since Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine.
Paper oil traders seem to believe the rhetoric from U.S. President Donald Trump and some in his administration ‚Äãthat the campaign against Iran is going well and there is no real threat to oil and product shipments through the Strait of Hormuz.
They also appear to ‚Äãbelieve that the International Energy Agency's release of a record 400 million barrels of crude from stockpiles will help solve some of the ‚ÅÝsupply disruptions.
However, the current issues can't be solved by comments from political leaders that appear untethered from the reality on the ground, as well as a stockpile release that will ‚Äãprobably not put enough oil in Asia, where it is needed.
While the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively blocked, the situation can only get worse and the pace at which it ‚Äãdoes so will start to accelerate.
This is especially the case in Asia, which takes most of the 18 million to 20 million barrels per day (bpd) of crude and products that flowed through the strait prior to the U.S. and Israel launching an aerial campaign against Iran on February 28.
SYSTEM BREAKING
The stress on supply chains in Asia is already becoming apparent and prices for actual cargoes of crude and refined ‚Äãproducts are reflecting this reality.
The premium for a barrel of cash Dubai crude over paper swaps jumped $4.17 to $37.87 on Wednesday, a level not seen since the Russian invasion of Ukraine, ‚Äãan event that also led to fears of oil shortages as Western buyers stopped buying Moscow's crude.
The difference between the Russian invasion of Ukraine and the current conflict in Iran is that in 2022, ‚Äåthere was no ‚ÅÝreal loss of supply of crude, merely a reshuffling of flows as Russian oil was re-routed to China and India.
But the current situation is vastly different, with even the re-routing of some crude exports from the Gulf to Saudi Arabia's Red Sea port and the United Arab Emirates facility on the Gulf of Oman nowhere near enough to offset the impact of the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
It's not just a crude-supply issue that is hurting Asia, it's the emerging tightness in refined products, which is threatening to turn very ‚Äãquickly into a serious emergency for importing ‚Äãnations like Australia, Indonesia and New Zealand.
Refineries ‚ÅÝin Asia are cutting processing rates and some countries, such as China , are moving to restrict fuel exports in order to ensure they can meet domestic demand.
This is sending prices for refined products soaring, with the cash differential for diesel hitting a fresh record high ‚Äãof $28.69 a barrel on Wednesday in Singapore.
This price reflects the premium for a physical cargo over the paper price and has surged from ‚Äãjust 84 cents a ‚ÅÝbarrel on February 27, the day before the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran.
It's a similar story for jet kerosene , with spot prices hitting a record high of $225.44 a barrel on March 4, before retreating to end at $157.12 on Wednesday, but this price is still 68% above the $93.45 on February 27.
What the prices in the physical crude and product markets in Asia are saying is ‚ÅÝthat the supply ‚Äãchain is buckling and the situation will likely get worse as countries start to hoard crude and fuel.
Enjoying ‚Äãthis column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI) , your essential new source for global financial commentary. ROI delivers thought-provoking, data-driven analysis of everything from swap rates to soybeans. Markets are moving faster than ever. ROI can help you keep ‚Äãup. Follow ROI on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
The views expressed here are those of the author, a columnist for Reuters.

item.title: How insider-trading fears harm prediction markets
item.description:
LONDON, March 12 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Sites like Polymarket and Kalshi can, in theory, reveal the truth about the future. Yet they risk being undermined by those who already know it. Prediction markets have seen some eye-catchingly well-timed trades this year, including on the ousters of Iran‚Äôs Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro . It has raised unsubstantiated concerns about insider trading by U.S. officials with knowledge of military ‚Äãstrikes. True or not, the perception of a rigged market could put off users, slowing the platforms‚Äô growth.
There are virtually no major public securities markets that allow insiders to trade on so-called material non-public information, ‚Äåor MNPI. There‚Äôs a moral element to the rules, but the logic is also practical: people may not invest at all if they fear being steamrollered by clued-up insiders. It‚Äôs a version of what economists call adverse selection , opens new tab . If a trader thinks a counterparty might possess more valuable information than they do, it‚Äôs rational to do nothing, meaning that insider-dominated markets can collapse.
The United States created , opens new tab the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) in 1934 to shore up public confidence following the 1929 crash. This meant policing fraud and standardising corporate disclosures. Penalties for insider trading became more stringent in both the ‚ÄãU.S. and United Kingdom during the corporate dealmaking boom of the 1980s, following some high-profile scandals.
Even traditional gambling companies try to stop insider bets, which in practice means verifying customers‚Äô identities and monitoring accounts for suspicious activity. Routine ‚Äãsurveillance by licensed sportsbooks helped lead to a spate of recent FBI arrests involving a player and a former coach in the National Basketball Association, both of whom denied providing non-public information about ‚ÅÝupcoming games to allow criminal partners to set up bets.
The SEC‚Äôs insider-trading rules don‚Äôt apply to prediction markets, where users can bet on everything from who will win the soccer World Cup , opens new tab to whether Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez will be the next Democratic presidential nominee , opens new tab . But ‚Äãunder U.S. law, so-called event contracts are classed as derivatives , opens new tab , which brings them under the purview , opens new tab of the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC). The CFTC expects platforms to ‚Äúmaintain audit trails, conduct surveillance, and enforce rules against prohibited practices‚Äù, including insider trading.
This applies to Kalshi‚Äôs markets, though ‚ÄãPolymarket‚Äôs situation is more complicated. The group, run by founder Shayne Coplan, effectively has two separate products , opens new tab . The first is a wildly popular offshore version, which is theoretically off-limits , opens new tab to users in the U.S. and some other major markets. The second is a nascent , opens new tab American alternative that‚Äôs regulated by the CFTC. The latter, more compliance-focused product is still being rolled out.
The danger is that virtual private networks (VPNs) can help traders even in banned locations place bets on the global version of Polymarket, which is where one trader netted a roughly $400,000 profit through well-timed wagers on Maduro‚Äôs removal in January. The company has ‚Äãsaid that it complies with all applicable laws, and its terms of use , opens new tab also prohibit the use of VPNs.
Coplan and his firm have arguably shown an ambivalent attitude towards inside information. On Tuesday, Polymarket announced , opens new tab a partnership with Palantir Technologies (PLTR.O) , opens new tab to establish controls aimed ‚Äãat preventing, identifying, and reporting suspicious activity in its sports markets.
Yet in a November 2025 TV interview, Coplan displayed a more libertarian streak. Responding to a question on inside information, the Polymarket founder said , opens new tab it was important to be ‚Äúclear and stringent on where the line is drawn‚Äù, ‚Äåbut that it‚Äôs ‚ÅÝalso ‚Äúsort of an inevitability that this will happen, and there‚Äôs a lot of benefits from it.‚Äù
One school of thought is that the presence of informed insiders simply makes prices more accurate. This arguably fits with Coplan‚Äôs conception , opens new tab of the platform as a ‚Äúglobal truth machine‚Äù. Imagine that a hypothetical White House insider knows about plans to hike tariffs on imports from Canada. They could profit from that information by purchasing ‚Äúyes‚Äù contracts on the relevant market , opens new tab . That would move the price, showing a higher probability of steeper levies. The price change would, in theory, count as useful information for businesses that send goods across the border. They could even use the market to hedge the risk of higher duties.
Yet insiders need someone to trade with, and those counterparties might not show up if ‚Äãthey‚Äôre just donating money to better-informed rivals. Prediction markets are ‚Äãessentially zero sum. The winners‚Äô profit on a successful ‚Äúyes‚Äù contract ‚ÅÝis roughly equal to the money lost by those who bet ‚Äúno‚Äù, after subtracting trading costs. The most extreme danger, therefore, is that using prediction markets to hedge risks is like buying car insurance from someone who already knows whether the engine is going to fail: the price will be set in the insiders‚Äô favour.
That‚Äôs a problem for some of the more expansive visions , opens new tab offered ‚Äãby industry advocates, including Jeff Yass, co-founder of trading giant Susquehanna International. He and others have argued that bigger institutions could use prediction markets to manage risks tied to specific ‚Äãevents like geopolitical ructions. The bigger ‚ÅÝthe fear of insider-dominated markets, however, the less likely that is to happen.
Granted, many specific markets are less vulnerable to non-public information, like where , opens new tab the S&P 500 Index of stocks will land by the end of March. And Coplan pointed out in an interview , opens new tab with the Wall Street Journal last year that suspected insiders are often quickly uncovered on social media site X, arguably meaning that users can simply avoid trading with them.
Still, it‚Äôs notable that Kalshi is making a public splash of banning , opens new tab insider trading and rooting out offenders. The platform barred an ‚ÅÝemployee of popular ‚ÄãYouTube streamer MrBeast for two years after he traded about $4,000 on markets related to the channel. Meanwhile the ‚Äú participant agreement , opens new tab ‚Äù for individuals hoping to use Polymarket‚Äôs ‚Äãnew onshore regulated U.S. product appears to call for government issued identification, which could help with Kalshi-style combatting of insider trading. It might go against the movement‚Äôs libertarian zeal. But insider trading represents a key business risk at a time when both platforms are eyeing roughly $20 billion valuations, according , opens new tab to the Wall Street ‚ÄãJournal. Clamping down, and keeping honest punters coming through the door, simply makes financial sense.
Follow Jennifer Johnson on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Russia's Dmitriev says he discussed global energy crisis with U.S. counterparts
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 12 (Reuters) - Russian presidential envoy Kirill Dmitriev said on Thursday he had ‚Äãdiscussed the current energy crisis with his U.S. counterparts ‚Äåas part of a meeting of the U.S.-Russia working group on economy that took place in Florida.
Nearly 20 million barrels per ‚Äãday (bpd) of supply ‚Äì roughly a fifth of global output ‚Äì ‚Äãhave been trapped inside the Gulf since the ‚ÅÝeffective closure of the Strait of Hormuz shortly after ‚Äãthe launch of the joint Israel‚ÄëU.S. war against Iran on ‚ÄãFebruary 28.
"Today, many countries, primarily the United States, are beginning to better understand the key, systemic role of Russian oil and gas in ‚Äãensuring the stability of the global economy, as well ‚Äãas the inefficiency and destructive nature of sanctions against Russia," Dmitriev ‚Äåsaid ‚ÅÝin remarks posted on his Telegram channel.
Dmitriev said that he had, on instructions from President Vladimir Putin, held meetings in the United States with the heads of the working ‚Äãgroup on economic ‚Äãcooperation between ‚ÅÝRussia and the United States.
"We discussed both promising projects that can contribute to the restoration ‚Äãof Russian-American relations, as well as the ‚Äãcurrent crisis ‚ÅÝsituation in global energy markets," Dmitriev said.
U.S. special envoy Steve Witkoff said that the teams discussed a "variety of topics" ‚ÅÝand agreed ‚Äãto stay in touch.
Other attendees ‚Äãincluded Jared Kushner, who is U.S. President Donald Trump's son-in-law, and White House ‚Äãadviser Josh Gruenbaum.

item.title: Italian base in Iraqi Kurdistan hit by missile, defence ministry says
item.description:
ROME, March 12 (Reuters) - An Italian military base in Iraqi Kurdistan was ‚Äãstruck by a missile ‚Äåovernight though no injuries were reported, the Italian defence ministry said ‚Äãon Thursday.
"A missile hit our ‚Äãbase in Erbil. There are ‚ÅÝno casualties or injuries ‚Äãamong the Italian personnel. They are ‚Äãall fine," the ministry said on X shortly after midnight on Thursday.
Defence ‚ÄãMinister Guido Crosetto has ‚Äãbeen in constant contact with senior military ‚Äåcommanders ‚ÅÝover the incident, the ministry added.
Foreign Minister Antonio Tajani said in a separate message on ‚ÄãX ‚Äãthat Italian ‚ÅÝmilitary personnel had taken shelter in a ‚Äãbunker and all were "well ‚Äãand ‚ÅÝsafe".
Italy has around 300 troops in Erbil, working on training ‚ÅÝKurdish ‚Äãsecurity forces, the defence ‚Äãministry said on its website.

item.title: South Korea to discipline officials over delays in recovering remains of Jeju Air crash victims
item.description: SEOUL, March 12 (Reuters) - South ‚ÄãKorean President ‚ÄåLee Jae Myung ‚Äãhas ‚Äãordered tough disciplinary ‚ÅÝaction ‚Äãagainst officials ‚Äãresponsible for delays in ‚Äãrecovering ‚Äãthe remains of ‚Äåvictims ‚ÅÝfrom the 2024 Jeju ‚ÄãAir ‚Äãcrash ‚ÅÝthat killed ‚Äã179 people, ‚Äãhis ‚ÅÝoffice said ‚ÅÝThursday.

item.title: Is Europe's gas demand recovery derailed or just dented by Iran crisis?
item.description:
LITTLETON, Colorado, March 12 (Reuters) - Most of Europe's largest natural gas consumers cranked gas-fired power production to multi-year highs in early 2026, raising hopes among liquefied natural gas (LNG) exporters that the region was regaining its taste for the fuel.
However, a major slowdown in gas consumption is evident so far in March, with average gas generation levels across key consumers dropping by around a third from the month before.
A ‚Äãsharp surge in regional gas costs in the wake of the outbreak of the U.S.-Iran war on February 28 likely accounts for at least some of that slowdown.
Above-normal temperatures across much of Central and ‚ÄåWestern Europe have also cut regional gas use, as heating demand has dropped sharply compared with the start of the year.
Further obscuring the demand picture is the current low level of regional gas inventories, which must be replenished ahead of next winter and will trigger regular import orders even if power and industrial gas use stays soft.
This confusing setup is likely to cause major headaches for the global LNG sector, which is investing billions of dollars in new export capacity on the assumption that Europe's gas needs will keep expanding.
Several clean-tech sectors will also be impacted ‚Äãby any forthcoming swings in European gas use, as renewable power developers and producers of heat pumps and battery systems all stand to gain from lower gas use.
To help analysts and industries grappling with this issue, here are ‚Äãsome key data points and trends on Europe's gas use by utilities and industry that may provide useful guideposts on the region's true demand potential.
POWER TRENDS
A key aspect of Europe's ‚ÅÝpower markets is that gas consumption for electricity generation peaks in winter, when heating demand is highest, and then sags sharply from spring through autumn.
Between 2019 and 2025, gas-fired output averaged 110 terawatt hours (TWh) per month from October to March, falling ‚Äãto about 87 TWh a month from April to September, Ember data shows.
That roughly 26% fall in mid-year consumption produces an uneven burn rate in Europe's power system, even though the fuel still provides a vital 25% of total electricity output ‚Äãover the year.
With weather forecasts for western Europe calling for above-normal temperatures through April, the annual dip in gas use by utilities is likely already underway and could crimp overall gas consumption irrespective of market jitters about the Middle East crisis.
That said, any sudden cold snaps over the spring could result in fresh bursts of gas demand for heating, which could further tighten regional fuel inventories.
STORAGE WOES
Europe's current gas stockpiles are hovering at around 27% of capacity, the lowest for this time of year since 2022.
An upbeat outlook for global LNG export volumes through 2026 ‚Äãhad lulled utilities into drawing down stocks over the past winter, although the recent stoppage of LNG exports from Qatar has prompted a speedy reassessment of that calculus.
Europe's storage operators must now steadily replenish those stockpiles ahead of the coming winter ‚Äãwhile Qatar - the world's second-largest LNG exporter in 2025 - remains offline.
Historically, Europe's total gas inventories hovered just below 2,000 billion cubic feet (bcf) by the start of November, which was sufficient to meet normal heating needs through winter.
Inventories are currently around 370 bcf, and so must ‚Äåexpand by around ‚ÅÝ1,600 bcf over the next 235 days or so.
To hit that total, gas storage operators must inject roughly 6.9 bcf per day (bcfd) through November, which is the equivalent of two large LNG tankers daily.
On average, three large LNG tankers discharged their cargo per day in Europe in 2025, according to Kpler, so it is possible for storage firms to secure the equivalent of two tankers per day for themselves.
However, a majority of Europe's gas supplies are delivered by pipeline, with around 17 bcfd distributed across Europe from the likes of Norway, North Africa and Azerbaijan, according to LSEG.
Tank farms will prefer cheaper pipelined supplies as they refill storage, but will tap the LNG import market if prices are attractive.
INDUSTRIAL PIVOT
The health of Europe's industrial base is ‚Äãanother key factor shaping gas demand.
Fertilizer manufacturers, chemical plants, steel ‚Äãmills and a swath of production lines have all ‚ÅÝhistorically been steady gas consumers.
But collective gas use by businesses has dropped sharply since Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022, and has stayed soft amid subdued economic activity across Europe.
Europe's once-esteemed car sector embodies the broader industrial malaise, with Volkswagen (VOWG.DE) , opens new tab , its largest carmaker, reporting layoffs and a drop in profits this year.
To help lower operating costs and provide greater regulatory certainty for industry, European policymakers ‚Äãare drafting new industrial heat rules aimed at helping some sectors replace volatile gas with cheaper electricity.
Lawmakers are also taking steps to ramp up supplies of biomethane - generated mainly ‚Äãfrom agriculture facilities and municipal waste ‚ÅÝdumps - to reduce the need for imported gas.
If successful, these measures may reduce total industrial gas use, although this would create extra electricity demand, which the power sector would be on the hook to deliver at low cost.
Any new measures are unlikely to materially affect gas consumption for several years, so for now gas-dependent businesses will have little choice but to continue burning gas when they can afford it and cut output when they can't.
This in turn will likely mean that Europe's total gas use trends stay choppy ‚ÅÝfor the foreseeable ‚Äãfuture, even if power and industrial users gradually reduce their underlying reliance on gas.
(The opinions expressed here are those of the author , a columnist for Reuters.)
Enjoying ‚Äãthis column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance 7 days a week.

item.description:
Follow on Apple or Spotify . Listen on the Reuters app .

Follow Jonathan Guilford on X and LinkedIn .
